cases.title: Cough, rusty phlegm, and one shaking chill
cases.chiefComplaint: I've been coughing up rust-colored phlegm with fever and a sharp pain in my right side when I breathe.
cases.history: A 67-year-old woman presents with three days of productive cough bringing up rust-colored, blood-tinged sputum, accompanied by fevers to 39 degrees C and right-sided chest pain that is sharp and worsens with deep inspiration. The illness began abruptly after a few days of mild nasal congestion; yesterday she had a single dramatic shaking chill that lasted nearly 30 minutes. She reports malaise and decreased appetite but no leg swelling or recent travel. Past medical history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Medications are lisinopril 10 mg daily and metformin 1000 mg twice daily; she has no known drug allergies. She is a former smoker with a 20-pack-year history, quit five years ago, drinks alcohol socially, and lives at home with her husband. She has not received the pneumococcal vaccine and last had an influenza vaccine over a year ago. Family history is notable for coronary artery disease. She denies recent hospitalization, antibiotic use, or known sick contacts.
cases.exam: The patient appears flushed and mildly uncomfortable but is alert and fully oriented. Examination of the right lower lung field reveals dullness to percussion, increased tactile fremitus, bronchial breath sounds, late inspiratory crackles, and egophony (E-to-A change). The left lung is clear. There is no accessory muscle use or cyanosis, and she speaks in full sentences. Cardiac exam shows tachycardia with a regular rhythm and no murmurs. There is no jugular venous distention, peripheral edema, or calf tenderness. Mucous membranes are slightly dry. The abdomen is soft and nontender.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Chest radiograph (PA and lateral)
CT pulmonary angiogram
Serum D-dimer
Bedside spirometry
stages.explanation: A standard chest radiograph is the recommended first-line study to confirm pneumonia in a patient with fever, productive cough, pleuritic pain, and focal findings of consolidation; it demonstrates the infiltrate and screens for complications such as a parapneumonic effusion. A CT pulmonary angiogram targets pulmonary embolism, but her rust-colored sputum, focal crackles, egophony, and rigor point strongly to infection, so it adds cost, contrast, and radiation without yield. A D-dimer is nonspecific and is predictably elevated by any acute infectious process, so a positive result would not advance the diagnosis. Bedside spirometry evaluates airflow obstruction and has no role in the acute diagnosis of an infiltrate. Confirming consolidation radiographically also anchors subsequent severity scoring and disposition.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 16.8 ×10³/µL (88% neutrophils, 6% bands)
Hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL
Na 137 mEq/L
BUN 16 mg/dL
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
Glucose 184 mg/dL
Chest X-ray: dense right lower lobe consolidation with air bronchograms; no pleural effusion
SpO2 94% on room air

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which organism is the most likely cause of this lobar pneumonia?
options: Mycoplasma pneumoniae
Streptococcus pneumoniae
Legionella pneumophila
Pseudomonas aeruginosa
stages.explanation: Streptococcus pneumoniae is the most common cause of community-acquired pneumonia and classically produces acute lobar consolidation with rust-colored sputum, a single rigor, and high fever. Mycoplasma causes a subacute walking pneumonia in younger patients with a diffuse interstitial pattern, not dense lobar consolidation. Legionella causes severe pneumonia often with GI symptoms, hyponatremia, and transaminitis, usually with a water-aerosol exposure history. Pseudomonas affects structurally abnormal lungs (bronchiectasis, cystic fibrosis) or healthcare-associated patients, not a community-dwelling woman with no such risk factors. Her presentation fits the textbook pneumococcal pattern.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given her diabetes, what is the most appropriate outpatient antibiotic regimen?
options: Azithromycin monotherapy
Oral amoxicillin/clavulanate plus azithromycin
Oral doxycycline monotherapy
Oral ciprofloxacin
stages.explanation: For outpatients with comorbidities such as diabetes, 2019 ATS/IDSA guidelines recommend either combination therapy with a beta-lactam (amoxicillin/clavulanate or a cephalosporin) plus a macrolide, or respiratory fluoroquinolone monotherapy (levofloxacin or moxifloxacin). Macrolide monotherapy is discouraged when comorbidities exist and where pneumococcal macrolide resistance exceeds 25 percent. Doxycycline monotherapy is reserved for otherwise healthy outpatients without comorbidities. Ciprofloxacin lacks reliable pneumococcal coverage (it is an antipseudomonal but not a respiratory fluoroquinolone) and is the wrong fluoroquinolone for CAP. The beta-lactam plus macrolide combination reliably covers S. pneumoniae and atypical pathogens.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Her CURB-65 score is calculated. What is the most appropriate disposition?
options: Admit to the ICU
Admit to a general medical ward
Treat as an outpatient with close follow-up
Observe in the ED for 24 hours, then reassess
stages.explanation: CURB-65 assigns one point each for Confusion, Urea (BUN greater than 19 mg/dL), Respiratory rate at least 30, low Blood pressure (SBP under 90 or DBP 60 or less), and age 65 or older. She scores only 1 point (age): no confusion, BUN 16, RR 22, and normal blood pressure. A score of 0 to 1 predicts low mortality and supports outpatient management with oral antibiotics and close follow-up, given she tolerates oral intake and has home support. Routine ICU or ward admission is unnecessary and exposes her to nosocomial risks. Prolonged ED observation adds no value once she is stratified as low risk and stable on room air.

cases.diagnosis: Pneumococcal (Streptococcus pneumoniae) community-acquired lobar pneumonia.
pearls: Rust-colored sputum, a single rigor, and lobar consolidation are classic for Streptococcus pneumoniae.
CURB-65 of 0-1 supports outpatient treatment; 2 prompts admission and 3 or more suggests ICU evaluation.
Outpatients with comorbidities need a beta-lactam plus macrolide or a respiratory fluoroquinolone, not macrolide monotherapy.
Ciprofloxacin is not a respiratory fluoroquinolone and should not be used for CAP.

cases.title: A college student with a nagging cough that won't quit
cases.chiefComplaint: I've had a dry, hacking cough and felt run down for almost two weeks, but I'm still going to class.
cases.history: A 19-year-old male college student presents with twelve days of a gradually worsening dry, hacking cough accompanied by low-grade fevers, sore throat, headache, and fatigue. Despite feeling unwell, he has continued attending lectures and exercising, describing himself as functional but miserable. Over the past two days he has developed mild right ear pain. He denies dyspnea at rest, pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, or rigors. He lives in a crowded dormitory, and his roommate had a similar prolonged cough last month. Past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. He has no known drug allergies. He has never smoked, drinks alcohol on weekends, denies illicit drug use, and is sexually active with one partner using condoms inconsistently. He has received all childhood immunizations. Family history is noncontributory. He has taken over-the-counter dextromethorphan without relief and has not seen a physician for this illness until now.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no acute distress, conversant and fully oriented. Otoscopic examination of the right ear reveals bullae on an erythematous tympanic membrane (bullous myringitis). The oropharynx is mildly erythematous without exudate. Auscultation reveals scattered bilateral wheezes and a few fine crackles at the lung bases, which are notably milder than expected given his prolonged symptoms, a classic finding where the chest exam appears out of proportion to the duration of illness. There is no focal dullness, egophony, or increased fremitus. There is no cervical lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly. The skin shows no targetoid rash.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which clinical feature best distinguishes this presentation from typical bacterial lobar pneumonia?
options: Presence of high fever with a single rigor
Dense lobar consolidation with egophony
Indolent course with diffuse findings and an exam milder than the prolonged symptoms suggest
Rapid progression to septic shock
stages.explanation: The hallmark of atypical (walking) pneumonia is an indolent, gradual course in a young patient who remains ambulatory, with a relatively unimpressive chest exam compared with the duration of symptoms, plus extrapulmonary features such as bullous myringitis. This contrasts sharply with typical pneumococcal disease. A high fever with a single rigor and dense lobar consolidation with egophony describe classic bacterial lobar pneumonia, the opposite pattern. Rapid progression to septic shock indicates severe bacterial sepsis, not the mild, smoldering illness seen here. Recognizing the dissociation between mild findings and prolonged symptoms is the key clinical clue to an atypical pathogen.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 9.8 ×10³/µL
Hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL (baseline 14.2 g/dL one year ago)
Reticulocyte count 4.8%
LDH 320 U/L
Total bilirubin 2.1 mg/dL (indirect 1.6)
Haptoglobin <10 mg/dL
Peripheral smear: red cell agglutination (clumping) at room temperature
Chest X-ray: bilateral patchy reticulonodular interstitial infiltrates, worse at the bases

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What best explains this patient's anemia?
options: Iron deficiency from chronic blood loss
Anemia of chronic disease
Cold-agglutinin-mediated autoimmune hemolytic anemia
Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency crisis
stages.explanation: Mycoplasma pneumoniae produces IgM cold agglutinins that bind the red cell I antigen at low temperatures, causing complement-mediated hemolysis, evidenced here by a falling hemoglobin, reticulocytosis, elevated LDH and indirect bilirubin, undetectable haptoglobin, and red cell agglutination on the smear. Iron deficiency gives a microcytic anemia without hemolysis markers or reticulocytosis. Anemia of chronic disease is typically mild and hypoproliferative with low reticulocytes. G6PD-related hemolysis is triggered by oxidant drugs or infection but produces bite cells and Heinz bodies, not cold agglutination, and lacks the characteristic room-temperature clumping. The cold-agglutinin link to Mycoplasma unifies his cough, ear findings, and hemolysis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate antibiotic for this patient?
options: Amoxicillin
Ceftriaxone
Vancomycin
Azithromycin
stages.explanation: Mycoplasma lacks a cell wall, so beta-lactams (amoxicillin, ceftriaxone) and vancomycin, which target peptidoglycan synthesis, are ineffective because the organism has no target for them. Macrolides such as azithromycin are first-line; doxycycline and respiratory fluoroquinolones are alternatives, particularly where macrolide resistance is a concern. Azithromycin reliably covers the atypical organism and shortens symptom duration. The three cell-wall-active agents listed would predictably fail, which is a high-yield teaching point distinguishing atypical from typical pneumonia management.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which extrapulmonary complication is most classically associated with this infection?
options: Erythema multiforme / mucocutaneous eruption (Mycoplasma-induced rash and mucositis)
Acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
Reactive arthritis with uveitis
Ascending cholangitis
stages.explanation: Mycoplasma is well known for extrapulmonary disease, including mucocutaneous eruptions ranging from erythema multiforme to Mycoplasma-induced rash and mucositis (MIRM), as well as cold-agglutinin hemolytic anemia, encephalitis, Guillain-Barre syndrome, and transverse myelitis. Acute glomerulonephritis follows streptococcal infection, not Mycoplasma. Reactive arthritis with uveitis follows enteric or chlamydial infections and is HLA-B27 associated. Ascending cholangitis is a biliary obstruction and infection unrelated to Mycoplasma. Recognizing these associations ties together his prolonged cough, bullous myringitis, and hemolysis as one unifying diagnosis.

cases.diagnosis: Mycoplasma pneumoniae (atypical 'walking') pneumonia with cold-agglutinin autoimmune hemolytic anemia.
pearls: Mycoplasma causes a subacute walking pneumonia in young, crowded populations (dorms, barracks) with an exam milder than symptoms suggest.
Bullous myringitis, cold-agglutinin hemolysis, and mucocutaneous eruptions are classic extrapulmonary clues.
Mycoplasma has no cell wall, so treat with a macrolide, doxycycline, or fluoroquinolone, never a beta-lactam.
Cold agglutinins are IgM antibodies against the red cell I antigen that cause complement-mediated hemolysis.

cases.title: High fever and confusion after a cruise
cases.chiefComplaint: My husband has been getting sicker for days with high fevers, diarrhea, and now he's confused.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his wife with six days of progressively worsening illness that began two days after returning from a week-long Caribbean cruise. He developed high fevers up to 39.5 degrees C, a cough productive of scant sputum, increasing shortness of breath, throbbing headache, and several days of watery, non-bloody diarrhea. Over the past 24 hours he has become lethargic and intermittently confused, prompting his wife to call for help. He used the ship's hot tub and spa frequently. Past medical history includes COPD and hypertension. Medications are tiotropium and amlodipine; he has no known drug allergies. He has a 40-pack-year smoking history and continues to smoke, and he drinks two to three beers daily. He works as an accountant and has no sick contacts at home. Family history is unremarkable. He has had no recent antibiotics or hospitalizations. On arrival he is hypoxic and is quickly escalated to the ICU for respiratory monitoring.
cases.exam: The patient is ill-appearing, lethargic, and oriented only to person. Despite a temperature of 39.6 degrees C, his heart rate is only 96 bpm, a relative bradycardia (pulse-temperature dissociation). Pulmonary exam reveals coarse crackles over the right mid and lower lung fields with diminished breath sounds; he is tachypneic and using accessory muscles. There is no jugular venous distention or peripheral edema. The abdomen is soft with hyperactive bowel sounds and mild diffuse tenderness without rebound. Neurologic exam shows global slowing without focal deficit, and the neck is supple. The skin is warm without rash.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Beyond a chest radiograph and blood cultures, which test most specifically targets the suspected organism in this severe pneumonia?
options: Urinary Legionella antigen (paired with Streptococcus pneumoniae antigen)
Serum procalcitonin
Nasopharyngeal swab for respiratory viral panel only
Cold agglutinin titer
stages.explanation: The combination of severe pneumonia, watery diarrhea, hyponatremia, transaminitis, confusion, relative bradycardia, and a recent cruise with hot-tub exposure strongly suggests Legionella. The urinary Legionella antigen (detecting serogroup 1) is rapid, sensitive, and remains positive even after antibiotics; pairing it with the pneumococcal urinary antigen is standard in severe CAP. Procalcitonin is nonspecific and cannot identify the organism. A viral panel alone would miss the bacterial cause driving his ICU-level illness. Cold agglutinin titers point toward Mycoplasma, which causes mild walking pneumonia in young patients, not fulminant multiorgan disease. Lower-respiratory culture on buffered charcoal yeast extract agar is confirmatory but slower.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 15.4 ×10³/µL
Na 127 mEq/L
K 3.9 mEq/L
Creatinine 1.6 mg/dL
AST 96 U/L / ALT 88 U/L
Phosphate 1.8 mg/dL
Procalcitonin 6.2 ng/mL
Urinary Legionella antigen: POSITIVE
Chest X-ray: patchy right-sided airspace opacities with early multilobar involvement
ABG (room air): pH 7.46, PaO2 54 mmHg, PaCO2 32 mmHg

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which constellation of findings most strongly supports the diagnosis?
options: Microcytic anemia, target cells, and elevated ferritin
Eosinophilia, wheezing, and peripheral infiltrates
Hyponatremia, diarrhea, transaminitis, and relative bradycardia after hot-tub exposure
Lobar consolidation with rust-colored sputum and a single rigor
stages.explanation: Legionella pneumophila classically produces severe pneumonia with prominent extrapulmonary features: hyponatremia (often from SIADH), watery diarrhea and other GI symptoms, mild transaminitis, hypophosphatemia, headache and confusion, and relative bradycardia, frequently after exposure to aerosolized water such as hot tubs, cooling towers, or cruise ships. This patient matches the pattern and has a positive urinary antigen. Microcytic anemia with target cells suggests thalassemia or iron issues. Eosinophilia with migratory infiltrates suggests eosinophilic pneumonia or ABPA. Lobar consolidation with rust-colored sputum and a single rigor describes pneumococcal pneumonia, which lacks the multisystem GI, neurologic, and electrolyte signature seen here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive antibiotic therapy?
options: Ceftriaxone alone
Amoxicillin/clavulanate
Vancomycin
A respiratory fluoroquinolone (e.g., levofloxacin) or a macrolide (azithromycin)
stages.explanation: Legionella is an intracellular organism, so effective agents must penetrate cells: respiratory fluoroquinolones (levofloxacin, moxifloxacin) or macrolides (azithromycin) are first-line, with fluoroquinolones often preferred in severe or immunocompromised disease. Beta-lactams such as ceftriaxone and amoxicillin/clavulanate do not reliably treat Legionella because the organism replicates intracellularly and is not covered at achievable concentrations. Vancomycin targets gram-positive cocci such as MRSA and has no Legionella activity. In severe CAP, empiric therapy should already include atypical coverage, which here becomes the definitive treatment once Legionella is confirmed.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: His serum sodium is 127 mEq/L with concentrated urine and clinical euvolemia. What is the most likely mechanism?
options: Diuretic-induced sodium loss
SIADH related to the pneumonia
Primary adrenal insufficiency
Psychogenic polydipsia
stages.explanation: Hyponatremia in Legionella and other pneumonias is commonly due to SIADH, in which inappropriate ADH release causes water retention, concentrated urine (high urine osmolality and sodium), and euvolemic hyponatremia. He is not on diuretics, so diuretic-induced loss is excluded. Adrenal insufficiency would typically cause hyponatremia with hyperkalemia and hypotension out of proportion, but his potassium is normal. Psychogenic polydipsia produces dilute urine from water overload, the opposite of the concentrated urine in SIADH. Treatment centers on the underlying pneumonia plus fluid restriction, and sodium should be corrected slowly to avoid osmotic demyelination.

cases.diagnosis: Severe Legionella pneumophila pneumonia (Legionnaires' disease) complicated by SIADH.
pearls: Suspect Legionella in severe pneumonia with diarrhea, hyponatremia, transaminitis, confusion, and water-aerosol exposure (hot tubs, cruises, cooling towers).
Urinary antigen detects serogroup 1 and stays positive after antibiotics; culture requires buffered charcoal yeast extract agar.
Treat with a respiratory fluoroquinolone or azithromycin; beta-lactams fail against this intracellular organism.
Relative bradycardia (pulse-temperature dissociation) is a classic, though nonspecific, clue.

cases.title: Sudden fever, body aches, and exhaustion
cases.chiefComplaint: I was totally fine yesterday morning and then got hit with fever, chills, and aching all over within hours.
cases.history: A 34-year-old woman presents to clinic with the abrupt onset of high fever, shaking chills, diffuse muscle aches, headache, dry cough, and profound fatigue that began roughly 30 hours ago. She says she felt completely well the previous morning and then got hit by a truck within a few hours. She has a mild sore throat and nasal congestion but denies dyspnea, pleuritic pain, or sputum production. It is mid-February, and several of her elementary-school students and her own young son have had similar febrile illnesses this week. She did not receive this season's influenza vaccine. Past medical history is unremarkable; she takes only a daily multivitamin and an oral contraceptive pill and has no known drug allergies. Her last menstrual period was two weeks ago and she is not pregnant. She is a lifelong nonsmoker, drinks alcohol rarely, and works as a schoolteacher. Family history is noncontributory. She has been taking acetaminophen with partial relief of the fever and aches.
cases.exam: The patient appears tired and flushed but is in no respiratory distress and is fully alert. The oropharynx is mildly erythematous without tonsillar exudate, and there is no peritonsillar swelling. Anterior cervical lymph nodes are small and mildly tender. Tympanic membranes are normal bilaterally. The lungs are clear to auscultation with no crackles, wheezes, or focal findings, and she speaks comfortably in full sentences. Cardiac exam is unremarkable aside from mild tachycardia. There is diffuse muscle tenderness but no joint swelling. There is no neck stiffness, photophobia, or rash.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in evaluation?
options: Chest radiograph and blood cultures
Rapid influenza molecular (PCR) or antigen test
Lumbar puncture
CT of the sinuses
stages.explanation: Her abrupt high fever, myalgias, headache, and dry cough during peak influenza season with multiple sick contacts is a classic influenza presentation. A rapid influenza molecular assay or antigen test confirms the diagnosis quickly and guides antiviral and infection-control decisions, though testing should not delay treatment when clinical suspicion is high. Chest radiograph and blood cultures are reserved for suspected pneumonia or sepsis; she has a clear chest and stable vitals. Lumbar puncture would be indicated for meningitis (neck stiffness, photophobia, altered mentation), which she lacks. CT of the sinuses targets complicated sinusitis, not an acute systemic viral syndrome. Clinical diagnosis during an outbreak is often sufficient to act.

stages.label: RAPID TEST
stages.content:
Rapid influenza A/B molecular assay: POSITIVE for influenza A
SpO2 98% on room air
Chest exam: clear (no imaging obtained)
Clinic note: symptom onset approximately 30 hours ago

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Supportive care only; antivirals are not indicated this late
Oral amoxicillin
Oseltamivir started now
Azithromycin
stages.explanation: Neuraminidase inhibitors such as oseltamivir provide the greatest benefit when started within 48 hours of symptom onset; she presents at about 30 hours, comfortably inside the window, so antiviral therapy will shorten illness duration and reduce complications. Calling it too late is wrong because she is well under 48 hours. Antibiotics such as amoxicillin and azithromycin treat bacterial infections and have no activity against influenza virus; they would be appropriate only if a bacterial complication like secondary pneumonia developed. Even beyond 48 hours, antivirals are recommended for severe, hospitalized, or high-risk patients, but here the correct action is prompt oseltamivir within the window plus symptomatic care.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which secondary complication should you counsel her to watch for?
options: Acute glomerulonephritis
Deep vein thrombosis
Acute angle-closure glaucoma
Bacterial superinfection (S. pneumoniae or S. aureus pneumonia) with recurrent fever and productive cough
stages.explanation: A classic and dangerous complication of influenza is secondary bacterial pneumonia, often from Streptococcus pneumoniae or Staphylococcus aureus (including post-influenza MRSA), which classically presents as initial improvement followed by recurrent fever, worsening productive cough, and dyspnea. She should return for these red flags. Acute glomerulonephritis follows streptococcal skin or throat infection, not influenza. DVT is not a direct influenza complication in an ambulatory, mobile patient. Acute angle-closure glaucoma is unrelated to a viral respiratory infection. Counseling on bacterial superinfection ensures timely evaluation if she worsens after an initial recovery.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate advice regarding her household and prevention?
options: Isolate while febrile, vaccinate unvaccinated household members, and consider antiviral prophylaxis for high-risk close contacts
No precautions are needed once she feels better in a day
Lifelong antiviral therapy is required
Antibiotics should be given to all household members
stages.explanation: Patients with influenza should stay home and avoid contact with others, generally until at least 24 hours after fever resolves without antipyretics, to limit transmission. Annual vaccination is the cornerstone of prevention and should be offered to unvaccinated household members. Post-exposure antiviral prophylaxis such as oseltamivir can be considered for high-risk close contacts (very young, elderly, pregnant, or immunocompromised). Returning to normal activity in a day while still infectious would spread illness. Influenza is self-limited, so lifelong antivirals are nonsensical. Empiric antibiotics for all household members is inappropriate because influenza is viral and overuse drives resistance.

cases.diagnosis: Influenza A (seasonal influenza).
pearls: Influenza presents with abrupt high fever, myalgias, headache, and dry cough during flu season; abruptness helps distinguish it from the common cold.
Oseltamivir is most effective within 48 hours of symptom onset but is still indicated later for severe, hospitalized, or high-risk patients.
Watch for secondary bacterial pneumonia (S. pneumoniae, S. aureus/MRSA): improvement then recurrent fever and productive cough.
Annual vaccination is the best prevention; consider antiviral prophylaxis for high-risk exposed contacts.

cases.title: Strikingly low oxygen but he feels okay
cases.chiefComplaint: My father seems only a little short of breath, but the clinic said his oxygen was dangerously low.
cases.history: A 72-year-old man is brought in by his daughter on day 8 of illness with fever, dry cough, fatigue, and loss of smell and taste. He reports only mild breathlessness when walking to the bathroom and insists he feels alright, but a home pulse oximeter read 86 percent, prompting the visit. He denies chest pain, hemoptysis, or leg swelling. There is a community surge of respiratory illness, and his wife tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 last week. Past medical history includes hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and obesity (BMI 33). Medications are lisinopril, metformin, and atorvastatin; he has no known drug allergies. He received an initial COVID-19 vaccine series two years ago but no recent boosters. He is a former smoker (25 pack-years, quit a decade ago), does not drink alcohol, and is retired. Family history includes coronary artery disease. On arrival he is conversant and remarkably comfortable-appearing despite his oxygen saturation, an example of silent hypoxia, and is admitted to the medical ward.
cases.exam: The patient is alert, oriented, and speaking in full sentences, appearing far more comfortable than his oxygen saturation would predict, classic silent (happy) hypoxemia. The respiratory rate is mildly elevated at 24 with minimal accessory muscle use. Auscultation reveals bibasilar fine inspiratory crackles without wheezing. There is no jugular venous distention, no S3, and no peripheral edema, making decompensated heart failure less likely. The calves are nontender and symmetric without swelling. Extremities are warm with brisk capillary refill. There are no rashes, and mucous membranes are moist. With supplemental oxygen by nasal cannula his saturation improves.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic and confirmatory workup?
options: Empiric anticoagulation and CT angiogram only
Echocardiogram and diuresis
SARS-CoV-2 PCR, chest imaging, and oxygen/ABG assessment
Reassurance and discharge with home pulse oximetry
stages.explanation: With fever, dry cough, anosmia, a positive household contact, and a community surge, SARS-CoV-2 PCR confirms the diagnosis, while chest imaging characterizes the pneumonia and oxygen or ABG assessment quantifies the silent hypoxemia driving disposition and steroid decisions. Jumping to empiric anticoagulation and CT angiography assumes pulmonary embolism without first pursuing the far more likely viral pneumonia, though PE stays on the differential if clues emerge. Echocardiography and diuresis treat heart failure, but he has no JVD, S3, or edema. Reassurance and discharge are unsafe given an SpO2 of 86 percent, which mandates admission, supplemental oxygen, and evaluation regardless of how well he appears.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
SARS-CoV-2 PCR: POSITIVE
WBC 6.2 ×10³/µL with lymphopenia (ALC 0.7 ×10³/µL)
CRP 88 mg/L
D-dimer 0.9 µg/mL (mildly elevated)
LDH 410 U/L
Chest X-ray: bilateral peripheral ground-glass and patchy airspace opacities
ABG (room air): pH 7.47, PaO2 56 mmHg, PaCO2 33 mmHg
SpO2 improves to 93% on 4 L/min nasal cannula

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He now requires supplemental oxygen to maintain saturation. What is the most appropriate addition to his treatment?
options: No corticosteroids; reserve them only for mechanical ventilation
Systemic dexamethasone
High-dose inhaled bronchodilators alone
Broad-spectrum antibiotics as primary therapy
stages.explanation: Dexamethasone reduces mortality in COVID-19 patients who require supplemental oxygen or ventilatory support; the key threshold is a new oxygen requirement, which he now meets, so systemic dexamethasone is indicated, often alongside an antiviral such as remdesivir and consideration of an immunomodulator (baricitinib or tocilizumab) in escalating disease. Withholding steroids until intubation is wrong because the benefit begins at the supplemental-oxygen stage, and steroids are not recommended for patients without hypoxemia. Inhaled bronchodilators treat bronchospasm, not the inflammatory pneumonitis here. Antibiotics address bacterial superinfection, not the primary viral process, and should not be the main therapy absent evidence of coinfection.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which additional inpatient intervention is most appropriate for this hospitalized patient?
options: Therapeutic anticoagulation for presumed massive PE
Routine systemic steroid taper over 6 weeks
Prophylactic-dose anticoagulation for venous thromboembolism prevention
Immediate intubation
stages.explanation: Hospitalized COVID-19 patients are at increased risk of venous thromboembolism and, unless contraindicated, should receive prophylactic-dose anticoagulation such as low-molecular-weight heparin. Therapeutic anticoagulation is not started empirically without evidence of thrombosis; his D-dimer is only mildly elevated and there is no confirmed PE. A 6-week steroid taper is inappropriate because dexamethasone for COVID-19 is a fixed short course (about 10 days or until discharge). Immediate intubation is not warranted since he stabilizes on low-flow nasal cannula and is mentating well; escalation is guided by failure of lower-level oxygen support, not his appearance alone.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate monitoring plan on the ward?
options: Discharge home now that he is on oxygen
Stop oxygen to recondition his lungs
Daily chest CT scans
Continuous pulse oximetry with oxygen escalation and ICU transfer if he progresses to high-flow/NIV failure
stages.explanation: Silent hypoxemia can mask rapid deterioration, so continuous pulse oximetry with a clear escalation pathway, from nasal cannula to high-flow nasal oxygen or noninvasive ventilation and ICU transfer if those fail, is the safest approach. Discharging an oxygen-dependent, hypoxemic patient is unsafe. Withholding oxygen to recondition the lungs is physiologically nonsensical and dangerous in hypoxemic respiratory failure. Daily chest CT scans add radiation and cost without changing management; imaging should be obtained for specific indications such as suspected PE or clinical deterioration. Trends in oxygen requirement and work of breathing guide care far better than appearance.

cases.diagnosis: COVID-19 (SARS-CoV-2) pneumonia with hypoxemic respiratory failure (silent hypoxia).
pearls: Silent (happy) hypoxia: COVID-19 patients can be strikingly hypoxemic yet comfortable, so measure oxygen saturation rather than relying on appearance.
Dexamethasone reduces mortality once a patient needs supplemental oxygen; avoid steroids in non-hypoxemic disease.
Hospitalized COVID-19 patients need VTE prophylaxis; bilateral peripheral ground-glass opacities are the typical imaging pattern.
Lymphopenia with elevated CRP, LDH, and D-dimer is the characteristic lab signature.

cases.title: Coughing during meals with a new fever
cases.chiefComplaint: Since his stroke he coughs every time he eats, and now he has a fever and is breathing fast.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man on the inpatient rehabilitation unit develops two days of fever, productive cough with foul-smelling sputum, and increasing shortness of breath. He suffered a left middle cerebral artery ischemic stroke ten days ago and has residual right-sided weakness and dysphagia; nursing staff have witnessed him coughing and choking during meals, and a formal bedside swallow evaluation was failed. He is most often fed while semi-recumbent. He denies chest pain or hemoptysis. Past medical history includes atrial fibrillation, hypertension, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and the recent stroke. Medications are apixaban, metoprolol, lisinopril, and atorvastatin; he has no known drug allergies. He has a 30-pack-year smoking history but quit fifteen years ago and rarely drinks alcohol. He lives with his wife and was independent before the stroke. Family history is notable for hypertension. He has not received antibiotics recently. The covering team is asked to evaluate the new fever and hypoxia and to weigh whether anaerobic coverage is required for the developing infiltrate.
cases.exam: The patient is mildly dyspneic but protecting his airway and oriented to person and place. There is a right-sided facial droop and right arm and leg weakness consistent with his prior stroke. Pulmonary auscultation reveals coarse crackles and bronchial breath sounds over the right lower posterior lung field with dullness to percussion in the same region. His breath has a putrid, foul odor. There is no stridor. Cardiac rhythm is irregularly irregular without murmurs. There is no jugular venous distention or peripheral edema. He pockets food in his right cheek, and a small amount of secretions pools in the posterior pharynx. There is no clubbing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which factor best explains why this patient developed pneumonia in the right lower lobe specifically?
options: The right lower lobe receives the most pulmonary blood flow
The right mainstem bronchus is wider and more vertical, so aspirated material gravitates there, especially when semi-recumbent
The left lung has more lobes, which protects it
Right lower lobe pneumonia indicates a pulmonary embolism
stages.explanation: The right mainstem bronchus is wider, shorter, and more vertically oriented than the left, so aspirated oropharyngeal contents preferentially track into the right lung; in a recumbent or semi-recumbent patient, gravity directs material to the posterior segment of the right upper lobe or the superior and basal segments of the right lower lobe. His post-stroke dysphagia and witnessed choking make aspiration the mechanism. Pulmonary blood flow distribution does not dictate aspiration location. The left lung actually has fewer lobes (two versus three), and lobe count does not protect it. A right lower lobe infiltrate in this context reflects aspiration, not pulmonary embolism, which presents with pleuritic pain and a different pattern.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 15.1 ×10³/µL (left shift)
Lactate 1.4 mmol/L
Chest X-ray: consolidation in the right lower lobe (superior segment) with a developing air-fluid level suggesting early cavitation
Sputum: foul-smelling, mixed flora on Gram stain
SpO2 89% on room air, improves to 95% on 3 L nasal cannula
Modified barium swallow: gross aspiration with thin liquids

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: An air-fluid level suggests early lung abscess. What is the most appropriate antibiotic choice?
options: Azithromycin monotherapy
Ceftriaxone alone
Ampicillin/sulbactam (covers oral anaerobes and typical pathogens)
Oral vancomycin
stages.explanation: Most aspiration pneumonia is treated like standard pneumonia, but when there is evidence of necrotizing pneumonia or lung abscess, here an air-fluid level, dedicated anaerobic coverage is warranted. Ampicillin/sulbactam covers oral anaerobes plus typical aerobic pathogens and is a first-line choice; clindamycin is an alternative. Uncomplicated aspiration pneumonia without abscess often does not need added anaerobic agents, but this patient has cavitation. Azithromycin monotherapy and ceftriaxone alone lack reliable anaerobic activity. Oral vancomycin is not absorbed systemically (it is used for C. difficile colitis) and would never treat pneumonia. Metronidazole alone is inadequate because it misses microaerophilic streptococci.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Beyond antibiotics, which intervention most directly prevents recurrent aspiration?
options: Routine prophylactic antibiotics indefinitely
Strict dysphagia precautions: swallow therapy, upright positioning, modified diet or NPO pending re-evaluation
Daily chest physiotherapy alone
An empiric proton pump inhibitor as the sole intervention
stages.explanation: The root problem is post-stroke dysphagia with documented aspiration, so the highest-yield prevention is aspiration precautions: speech-language-pathology-guided swallow rehabilitation, keeping the patient upright during and after feeds, diet texture modification, and temporary NPO with alternative nutrition if aspiration is severe. Indefinite prophylactic antibiotics breed resistance and do not fix the mechanical problem. Chest physiotherapy may aid clearance but does not prevent the aspiration event itself. A proton pump inhibitor alone does not prevent aspiration of oropharyngeal contents and can even alter gastric flora; it is not a substitute for swallow management. Addressing the swallowing deficit is the definitive preventive strategy.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication is he at greatest risk of if the infiltrate is inadequately treated?
options: Spontaneous pneumothorax
Pulmonary fibrosis within days
Lobar atelectasis from mucus plugging only
Lung abscess / empyema
stages.explanation: Aspiration of anaerobe-laden oral contents can progress to necrotizing pneumonia, lung abscess, and empyema, especially with delayed or inadequate anaerobic coverage; his early air-fluid level signals this trajectory. Spontaneous pneumothorax is associated with bullous lung disease or tall thin young patients, not aspiration. Pulmonary fibrosis develops over months to years from chronic injury, not within days of an acute infection. Mucus plugging can cause atelectasis but is not the principal danger here; the infectious cavitary process is. Recognizing the abscess and empyema risk justifies prolonged antibiotics and drainage if a significant pleural collection develops.

cases.diagnosis: Aspiration pneumonia (right lower lobe) with early lung abscess, secondary to post-stroke dysphagia.
pearls: Aspiration favors the right lung because the right mainstem bronchus is wider and more vertical; the segment depends on patient position.
Add anaerobic coverage (ampicillin/sulbactam or clindamycin) for necrotizing pneumonia, abscess, or empyema, not for every aspiration.
Post-stroke dysphagia demands swallow evaluation and aspiration precautions, which prevent recurrence better than antibiotics.
An air-fluid level on imaging signals lung abscess and the need for prolonged therapy and possible drainage.

cases.title: New fever and worsening oxygenation on the ventilator
cases.chiefComplaint: Intubated patient is now spiking fevers with thicker secretions and rising oxygen needs on ventilator day 6.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man has been mechanically ventilated for six days after intubation for acute hypoxemic respiratory failure from severe acute pancreatitis. Over the past 18 hours he has developed new fevers to 39 degrees C, copious thick purulent endotracheal secretions, and rising oxygen requirements, with the FiO2 escalated from 40 to 70 percent. He was hospitalized for two weeks three months ago and received broad-spectrum antibiotics at that time, and he has chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Home medications include tiotropium, an inhaled corticosteroid, and metformin; he has no known drug allergies. He has a 50-pack-year smoking history. He has been receiving enteral nutrition through a nasogastric tube and is on stress-ulcer prophylaxis. His prior hospitalization and recent antibiotic exposure place him at high risk for multidrug-resistant organisms. The ICU team is asked to evaluate the new fever and infiltrate, choose empiric therapy, and plan an antibiotic de-escalation strategy once cultures return.
cases.exam: The patient is intubated and sedated (RASS -2), responsive to voice. The ventilator shows increased peak airway pressures, and copious thick yellow-green secretions are suctioned from the endotracheal tube. Auscultation reveals new coarse crackles and bronchial breath sounds over the left lower lung field. There is no purulent drainage at central line sites, and the abdomen is soft with a healing appearance and present bowel sounds. There is no calf swelling or cord. The skin is warm and well perfused with brisk capillary refill, but he is mildly tachycardic and borderline hypotensive on low-dose norepinephrine. There is no new rash or decubitus infection.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Beyond meeting clinical criteria, what is the most appropriate diagnostic step at the start of antibiotics?
options: Obtain a lower respiratory tract sample (endotracheal aspirate or BAL) for culture before starting empiric antibiotics
Start antibiotics and never culture, since results will not change therapy
Obtain only blood cultures and a procalcitonin
Remove the endotracheal tube to clear the infection
stages.explanation: Suspected ventilator-associated pneumonia (new or progressive infiltrate plus fever, leukocytosis, purulent secretions, and worsening gas exchange after at least 48 hours of ventilation) should be evaluated with a lower respiratory tract sample, endotracheal aspirate or bronchoalveolar lavage, obtained ideally before antibiotics, because it identifies the organism and enables later de-escalation. Starting antibiotics without any culture forfeits the chance to tailor therapy, exactly what this case emphasizes. Blood cultures and procalcitonin support the workup but are insufficient alone; procalcitonin cannot identify the pathogen and is used mainly to guide duration. Extubation is impossible given his FiO2 of 70 percent and would not treat the pneumonia.

stages.label: GRAM STAIN & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 18.4 ×10³/µL
Chest X-ray: new left lower lobe infiltrate
Endotracheal aspirate Gram stain: many neutrophils, gram-negative rods
Procalcitonin 4.8 ng/mL
Blood cultures: pending

stages.label: EMPIRIC THERAPY
stages.question: While awaiting cultures, what is the most appropriate empiric regimen given his MDR risk factors and the gram-negative rods seen?
options: Oral azithromycin
Two antipseudomonal agents from different classes plus MRSA coverage (e.g., cefepime or meropenem, plus an aminoglycoside or antipseudomonal fluoroquinolone, plus vancomycin or linezolid)
Ceftriaxone plus doxycycline
A single narrow agent such as ampicillin
stages.explanation: For VAP with risk factors for multidrug-resistant organisms (prior IV antibiotics within 90 days, prolonged hospitalization, high local resistance), guidelines recommend broad empiric coverage: two antipseudomonal agents from different classes so at least one is active, plus MRSA coverage with vancomycin or linezolid, then narrowing once cultures return. Azithromycin targets atypicals and is inadequate for nosocomial gram-negative pneumonia. Ceftriaxone plus doxycycline is a community-acquired pneumonia regimen lacking antipseudomonal and MRSA activity. A single narrow agent like ampicillin has no antipseudomonal or MRSA coverage. The empiric breadth is deliberately wide to avoid inadequate initial therapy, which raises mortality in VAP, with de-escalation planned.

stages.label: CULTURE & SUSCEPTIBILITIES
stages.content:
Respiratory culture (final): Pseudomonas aeruginosa
Susceptibilities: RESISTANT to piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, and meropenem; SUSCEPTIBLE to ceftolozane-tazobactam and amikacin
No MRSA isolated
Blood cultures: no growth at 48 hours

stages.label: DE-ESCALATION
stages.question: Cultures show MDR Pseudomonas susceptible only to ceftolozane-tazobactam and amikacin, with no MRSA. What is the best de-escalation strategy?
options: Continue all empiric broad-spectrum agents for the full course to be safe
Switch to oral ciprofloxacin regardless of susceptibilities
Stop MRSA coverage and the redundant antipseudomonal agents, and treat with the susceptible agent (ceftolozane-tazobactam) guided by the antibiogram
Stop all antibiotics now that the organism is identified
stages.explanation: De-escalation is the core principle: once the pathogen and susceptibilities are known and there is no MRSA, discontinue vancomycin or linezolid and the redundant second antipseudomonal agent and treat with the agent to which the organism is susceptible, here ceftolozane-tazobactam (with amikacin considered case-by-case). This narrows the spectrum and limits toxicity, cost, and resistance pressure while maintaining efficacy. Continuing all broad agents needlessly drives resistance and adverse effects. Switching to oral ciprofloxacin ignores the reported resistance pattern and risks treatment failure. Stopping antibiotics entirely abandons treatment of a confirmed, virulent MDR pathogen causing active pneumonia. Targeted therapy based on susceptibilities is the evidence-based move.

stages.label: DURATION
stages.question: Assuming clinical improvement, what is the recommended duration of therapy for this VAP?
options: A single dose
About 7 days, guided by clinical response and procalcitonin trends
At least 6 weeks
Lifelong suppressive therapy
stages.explanation: Current guidelines recommend a 7-day course for most VAP, including that caused by non-fermenting gram-negative rods like Pseudomonas, provided the patient is improving; shorter fixed durations reduce antibiotic exposure and resistance without worsening outcomes, and serial procalcitonin can support discontinuation. A single dose is grossly inadequate for established pneumonia. Six weeks is reserved for deep-seated infections such as osteomyelitis or endocarditis, not uncomplicated VAP. Lifelong suppression applies to certain prosthetic or relapsing infections, not this scenario. Duration should be individualized to clinical response, but the default has shifted from 14 to about 7 days.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which bundle element most directly reduces the risk of future ventilator-associated pneumonia?
options: Routine prophylactic systemic antibiotics for all ventilated patients
Keeping the patient flat to prevent hypotension
Changing the ventilator circuit every 8 hours routinely
Head-of-bed elevation 30-45 degrees, daily sedation interruption with spontaneous breathing trials, and oral care to minimize aspiration
stages.explanation: VAP prevention bundles emphasize semi-recumbent positioning (head of bed 30-45 degrees) to reduce aspiration, daily sedation interruption (sedation vacations) paired with spontaneous breathing trials to shorten ventilation time, oral hygiene, and minimizing unnecessary prophylaxis. Routine systemic antibiotic prophylaxis is not recommended and promotes resistance. Keeping the patient supine increases aspiration and VAP risk, the opposite of the goal. Frequent scheduled ventilator-circuit changes do not reduce VAP and may increase contamination; circuits are changed when soiled or malfunctioning. The bundle's shared aim is fewer ventilator days and less aspiration, the two biggest modifiable drivers of VAP.

cases.diagnosis: Ventilator-associated pneumonia due to multidrug-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa.
pearls: VAP is pneumonia at least 48 hours after intubation; sample the lower airway before antibiotics to enable de-escalation.
Empiric therapy for MDR risk is two antipseudomonal agents plus MRSA coverage, then narrow to susceptibilities.
De-escalation reduces resistance, toxicity, and cost; stop redundant agents once cultures return.
Most VAP needs only about 7 days of therapy; prevent it with head-of-bed elevation and daily sedation and breathing trials.

cases.title: Weeks of cough, night sweats, and blood-streaked sputum
cases.chiefComplaint: I've been coughing for over a month, losing weight, sweating through my sheets, and today I coughed up blood.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man presents to the emergency department with six weeks of worsening productive cough, now with several episodes of blood-streaked sputum today. He reports drenching night sweats, subjective fevers, fatigue, and an unintentional 6 kg weight loss over two months. He immigrated from a tuberculosis-endemic country eight months ago and lives in crowded shared housing with several other recent arrivals, one of whom has had a chronic cough. He denies chest trauma, anticoagulant use, or known lung disease. Past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications; he has no known drug allergies. He is a lifelong nonsmoker, drinks alcohol occasionally, and works in construction. He has never been tested for HIV. Family history is noncontributory. He has not previously received treatment for tuberculosis and is unsure whether he received BCG vaccination as a child. On triage the experienced charge nurse immediately questions whether airborne precautions are needed before he is roomed.
cases.exam: The patient is thin and chronically ill-appearing but in no acute distress and fully alert. Examination of the upper lung zones reveals posttussive crackles and amphoric (cavernous) breath sounds at the right apex. There is no clubbing. There is small-volume blood-streaked sputum in an emesis basin but no massive hemoptysis. Cervical and supraclavicular lymph nodes are not enlarged, and there is no palpable hepatosplenomegaly. Cardiac exam is unremarkable. He has temporal wasting and loose-fitting clothing consistent with weight loss. There is no oral thrush, skin rash, or focal neurologic deficit. The remainder of the exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most important immediate step when this patient arrives?
options: Place him in an airborne-infection isolation (negative-pressure) room with N95 respirators for staff
Place him in standard droplet precautions only
Obtain a CT chest before any isolation
Start empiric antibiotics for community-acquired pneumonia and observe
stages.explanation: A patient with subacute cough, hemoptysis, night sweats, weight loss, and epidemiologic risk for tuberculosis must be placed immediately in an airborne-infection isolation (negative-pressure) room, with staff wearing fit-tested N95 respirators, to prevent transmission of airborne droplet nuclei. Droplet precautions with surgical masks are insufficient because M. tuberculosis spreads via small airborne particles that travel beyond a few feet. Delaying isolation to obtain a CT scan exposes staff and other patients during transport. Empiric CAP therapy without isolation both misses the diagnosis and creates an exposure hazard. Infection control comes first and runs in parallel with diagnostic testing; isolation should never wait for confirmation.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 9.6 ×10³/µL
HIV antigen/antibody: pending
Chest X-ray: right upper lobe cavitary infiltrate with surrounding nodular opacities
Sputum AFB smear (first specimen): positive for acid-fast bacilli
Nucleic acid amplification test (Xpert MTB/RIF): Mycobacterium tuberculosis detected, rifampin resistance NOT detected
SpO2 96% on room air

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How many sputum specimens and what testing are recommended to evaluate for active pulmonary TB?
options: A single random sputum smear is sufficient to exclude TB
Blood cultures alone
Three sputum specimens (often serial or morning) for AFB smear and culture, with at least one nucleic acid amplification test
Serologic (antibody) testing, which is the gold standard
stages.explanation: Diagnosis of active pulmonary TB uses three sputum specimens (classically collected serially, with morning samples favored) sent for acid-fast smear and mycobacterial culture, the culture remaining the gold standard and allowing drug-susceptibility testing, plus at least one nucleic acid amplification test such as Xpert MTB/RIF that rapidly confirms M. tuberculosis and screens for rifampin resistance. A single random smear has limited sensitivity and cannot exclude TB. Blood cultures rarely grow M. tuberculosis except in disseminated disease and are not the standard pulmonary workup. Serologic antibody testing is not recommended for TB diagnosis due to poor accuracy. Culture also guides susceptibility-directed therapy, essential in the era of drug resistance.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Rifampin resistance is not detected. What is the appropriate initial treatment regimen?
options: Isoniazid monotherapy for 9 months
Four-drug therapy: rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol (RIPE) with pyridoxine
Azithromycin and ethambutol
Rifampin alone for 4 months
stages.explanation: Standard treatment for drug-susceptible active pulmonary TB is an intensive phase of four drugs, rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol (RIPE), for two months, followed by a continuation phase of rifampin and isoniazid, typically completing six months total, with pyridoxine (vitamin B6) added to prevent isoniazid-induced neuropathy. Isoniazid monotherapy treats latent TB infection, not active disease, and using it alone for active TB breeds resistance. Azithromycin plus ethambutol is a regimen for nontuberculous mycobacteria such as MAC, not M. tuberculosis. Rifampin alone is an option for latent infection, not active disease. Multidrug therapy prevents emergence of resistance and is ideally given under directly observed therapy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Beyond starting treatment, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: No reporting is necessary if the patient is started on therapy
Discharge home immediately without follow-up once smears are obtained
Begin treatment only after all cultures finalize in 6 weeks
Report the case to the public health department, initiate contact tracing, and test the patient for HIV
stages.explanation: Tuberculosis is a reportable disease; the case must be reported to the public health department, which coordinates contact investigation among his crowded household and close contacts and can arrange directly observed therapy. Given his risk profile and that TB and HIV frequently coexist, HIV testing is essential and influences management. Failing to report is both unsafe and, in most jurisdictions, illegal. Discharging him home without follow-up or isolation arrangements risks ongoing transmission and nonadherence. Waiting weeks for final cultures before treating is wrong because therapy starts on the basis of smear and NAAT positivity and clinical suspicion, with the regimen later tailored to susceptibility results.

cases.diagnosis: Active cavitary pulmonary tuberculosis (Mycobacterium tuberculosis), rifampin-susceptible.
pearls: Subacute cough, hemoptysis, night sweats, and weight loss with upper-lobe cavitation is active pulmonary TB until proven otherwise; isolate immediately.
Airborne precautions (negative-pressure room, N95) come first and do not wait for confirmation.
Diagnose with three sputum AFB smears and cultures plus a NAAT; culture is the gold standard and guides susceptibility.
Treat drug-susceptible TB with RIPE plus pyridoxine; report to public health and test for HIV.

cases.title: A routine work screening test came back positive
cases.chiefComplaint: My hospital's annual health screening said my TB blood test was positive, but I feel completely fine.
cases.history: A 30-year-old woman, a hospital nurse, presents to employee health after a routine annual interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA) returned positive. She feels entirely well and specifically denies cough, hemoptysis, fevers, night sweats, unintentional weight loss, or fatigue. She works on a medical-surgical ward and recalls caring for a patient later diagnosed with active pulmonary tuberculosis several months ago, before isolation was instituted. She has never been treated for tuberculosis and has no prior positive test. Past medical history is unremarkable. Medications are an oral contraceptive pill and a daily multivitamin; she has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke, drinks alcohol socially, and has no history of injection drug use. She is not pregnant, and her last menstrual period was three weeks ago. She has no history of liver disease and rarely drinks. Family history is noncontributory. She was not vaccinated with BCG. She is anxious about what the positive test means and whether she is contagious to her patients and family.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing, in no distress, and at her stated baseline. Vital signs are normal. The lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally with no crackles, wheezes, or abnormal breath sounds. There is no cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy. Cardiac exam is normal. The abdomen is soft and nontender with no hepatosplenomegaly. There is no clubbing, no skin lesions, and no scar suggestive of prior BCG vaccination. She has no temporal wasting and appears well-nourished. The remainder of the physical examination is entirely unremarkable, consistent with an asymptomatic individual.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the essential next step before labeling this as latent TB infection and starting treatment?
options: Obtain a chest radiograph (and clinical review) to rule out active tuberculosis
Start four-drug RIPE therapy immediately
Repeat the IGRA in one year and do nothing now
Begin airborne isolation at home
stages.explanation: Before diagnosing latent TB infection, you must exclude active disease, because treating LTBI with a single drug in someone who actually has active TB promotes resistance. A chest radiograph together with a symptom review is the key step; if the film is normal and she is asymptomatic, active disease is effectively excluded and LTBI treatment can proceed. Starting four-drug RIPE is the treatment for active TB, not LTBI, and is inappropriate here. Merely repeating the IGRA in a year ignores a positive result and a known exposure, missing the chance to prevent progression. Home airborne isolation is unnecessary because latent infection is not contagious; only active pulmonary or laryngeal disease transmits.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
IGRA (QuantiFERON-TB Gold): POSITIVE
Chest X-ray: clear lung fields, no infiltrate, no cavitation, no adenopathy
Symptom screen: negative (no cough, fever, night sweats, or weight loss)
HIV test: negative
Baseline AST/ALT: normal
Urine pregnancy test: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What does this overall picture represent?
options: Active pulmonary tuberculosis
Latent tuberculosis infection
A false-positive test that needs no further action
Prior BCG vaccination causing the positive IGRA
stages.explanation: A positive IGRA with a normal chest radiograph and no symptoms in a person with a documented exposure represents latent tuberculosis infection, immune evidence of prior infection without active disease. Active pulmonary TB is excluded by her normal imaging and absence of symptoms. Dismissing it as a false positive is wrong: IGRAs are highly specific and she has a real exposure, so she warrants treatment to prevent reactivation (lifetime risk roughly 5 to 10 percent, higher early after infection). Importantly, IGRAs, unlike the tuberculin skin test, are not affected by prior BCG vaccination, so BCG cannot explain the result, and she has no BCG history anyway.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which is a preferred (CDC-recommended) regimen for treating her latent TB infection?
options: Six-month four-drug RIPE therapy
Lifelong daily isoniazid
Once-weekly isoniazid plus rifapentine for 12 weeks (3HP)
Azithromycin for 5 days
stages.explanation: Preferred LTBI regimens are now the shorter rifamycin-based options: three months of weekly isoniazid plus rifapentine (3HP), three months of daily isoniazid plus rifampin (3HR), or four months of daily rifampin (4R). These are favored over the older nine-month daily isoniazid (9H) because they are shorter, improve completion rates, and have lower hepatotoxicity. RIPE is multidrug treatment for active disease, not latent infection. Lifelong isoniazid is never indicated. Azithromycin has no role in TB treatment. Note a drug-interaction caveat: rifamycins reduce the efficacy of oral contraceptives, so she should be counseled to use an additional or alternative contraceptive method during treatment.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Which counseling point is most important for this patient on a rifamycin-based regimen?
options: She is contagious and must avoid all patient contact for 6 months
Rifamycins induce hepatic enzymes and reduce oral contraceptive effectiveness; use backup contraception and report signs of hepatotoxicity
She should expect her urine and tears to turn blue
No monitoring or counseling is needed
stages.explanation: Rifamycins (rifampin, rifapentine) are potent hepatic cytochrome P450 inducers and reduce the efficacy of oral contraceptives, so she should use a backup or alternative method; she should also watch for and report signs of hepatotoxicity (nausea, right-upper-quadrant pain, jaundice, dark urine), with baseline and as-needed liver enzyme monitoring. She is not contagious because latent infection does not transmit, so she can continue working without restriction. Rifamycins classically cause orange-red, not blue, discoloration of urine, sweat, and tears, which is harmless but can stain contact lenses. Saying no counseling is needed ignores important interactions and monitoring; adherence support improves completion of therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Latent tuberculosis infection (positive IGRA, normal chest radiograph, asymptomatic).
pearls: Always exclude active TB (symptoms plus chest X-ray) before treating latent infection; single-drug therapy in active disease breeds resistance.
IGRAs are unaffected by prior BCG vaccination, unlike the tuberculin skin test.
Preferred LTBI regimens are short rifamycin-based courses (3HP, 3HR, 4R): better completion and less hepatotoxicity than 9 months of isoniazid.
Rifamycins reduce oral contraceptive efficacy and cause orange-red body fluids; counsel on backup contraception and hepatotoxicity.

cases.title: Weeks of fever and weight loss in a man with advanced HIV
cases.chiefComplaint: I've had fevers, drenching sweats, weight loss, and worsening cough and breathlessness for a month.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man with HIV is admitted with four to six weeks of daily fevers, drenching night sweats, roughly 10 kg of weight loss, progressive dry cough, and worsening exertional dyspnea. He was diagnosed with HIV years ago but has been poorly adherent to antiretroviral therapy and missed multiple clinic visits; his most recent CD4 count was around 40 cells/microL. He also reports a dull headache for the past week. He denies seizures or focal weakness. Past medical history is otherwise notable for prior oral candidiasis. He is on no medications currently, having stopped his antiretrovirals months ago, and has no known drug allergies. He smokes half a pack daily, has a remote history of injection drug use, and is unstably housed. Family history is noncontributory. He has not received treatment for tuberculosis. On review of systems he notes mild blurring of vision. The admitting team is concerned about a disseminated infectious process given his profound immunosuppression and multisystem complaints.
cases.exam: The patient is cachectic and chronically ill-appearing, alert but fatigued. Temperature is 39.2 degrees C with tachycardia and mild tachypnea, and oxygen saturation is 90 percent on room air. Pulmonary auscultation reveals fine bilateral crackles diffusely without focal consolidation. There is mild hepatosplenomegaly and diffuse, small, nontender lymphadenopathy. Dilated funduscopic examination shows scattered pale yellow choroidal nodules (choroidal tubercles). There is no neck rigidity, but he has mild photophobia. Oral thrush is present. The abdomen is soft. There is no focal neurologic deficit, and cranial nerves are intact. The skin shows no rash, and healed track marks are noted on the forearms.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given advanced HIV (CD4 ~40) with diffuse pulmonary and systemic findings, what chest imaging finding would you most expect on the best first imaging step?
options: Chest radiograph showing innumerable tiny (1-3 mm) nodules diffusely distributed (millet seed / miliary pattern)
Chest X-ray showing a single lobar consolidation
No imaging; treat empirically for bacterial sepsis only
Chest X-ray showing a large pleural effusion as the dominant finding
stages.explanation: In disseminated tuberculosis, hematogenous spread seeds the lungs with countless tiny 1-3 mm nodules producing the classic miliary (millet seed) pattern on chest radiograph, often the first and most accessible imaging clue, with high-resolution CT being more sensitive. A solitary lobar consolidation suggests typical bacterial pneumonia, not dissemination. Forgoing imaging to treat only bacterial sepsis would miss the unifying diagnosis driving his multisystem disease in the setting of profound immunosuppression. A dominant large pleural effusion is more typical of primary TB pleurisy than of miliary disease, where the hallmark is diffuse micronodularity. Recognizing the miliary pattern rapidly reframes the workup toward disseminated TB.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CD4 count 38 cells/µL; HIV viral load high
WBC 3.8 ×10³/µL
Hemoglobin 9.2 g/dL
Na 129 mEq/L
AST 110 U/L / ALT 95 U/L / ALP 320 U/L
Chest X-ray: innumerable 1-3 mm nodules uniformly distributed throughout both lungs (miliary pattern)
Funduscopy: choroidal tubercles confirmed
Sputum AFB smear: positive; Xpert MTB/RIF: M. tuberculosis detected, rifampin resistance not detected

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding is most specific for disseminated (miliary) tuberculosis in this patient?
options: Mild hyponatremia
Choroidal tubercles on funduscopy
Oral thrush
Anemia
stages.explanation: Choroidal tubercles, pale yellow nodules in the choroid seen on dilated funduscopy, are a highly specific sign of hematogenously disseminated (miliary) tuberculosis and, together with the miliary chest pattern, strongly confirm the diagnosis. Hyponatremia is common in TB (via SIADH or adrenal involvement) but is nonspecific. Oral thrush reflects his underlying HIV immunosuppression, not TB dissemination specifically. Anemia is a nonspecific feature of chronic disease and marrow involvement. The combination of miliary nodules, choroidal tubercles, hepatosplenomegaly, pancytopenia, and a positive AFB smear in advanced HIV makes disseminated TB the clear diagnosis. Funduscopy is a quick, high-yield bedside test that can clinch the diagnosis before culture.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: He has headache and photophobia. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Ignore the headache as unrelated
Start high-dose steroids and discharge
Perform a lumbar puncture (after appropriate evaluation) to assess for tuberculous meningitis
Obtain only a sinus X-ray
stages.explanation: Miliary TB frequently coexists with tuberculous meningitis, and his headache and photophobia raise that concern, so a lumbar puncture, after clinical assessment and imaging if focal signs or markedly elevated intracranial pressure are suspected, is essential; classic TB meningitis CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, and low glucose. Dismissing the headache risks missing a life-threatening, treatable complication. Starting steroids and discharging an unstable, profoundly immunosuppressed patient is unsafe and skips diagnosis, though adjunctive corticosteroids do have a role in confirmed TB meningitis or pericarditis. A sinus X-ray addresses sinusitis, not CNS involvement. Identifying CNS disease changes treatment duration and prompts adjunctive steroids.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is started on RIPE therapy. When should antiretroviral therapy be initiated given his CD4 of 38?
options: Start ART and TB therapy simultaneously on day 1 in all cases
Start TB treatment first, then initiate ART within about 2 weeks given his very low CD4 (watching for IRIS)
Defer ART until TB treatment is fully completed in 6 months
Never give ART while on TB drugs
stages.explanation: In HIV-associated TB, anti-tuberculous therapy is started first; antiretroviral therapy is then added early, within about two weeks for severely immunosuppressed patients (CD4 under 50), because early ART reduces mortality in this group, while remaining vigilant for immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS). An important exception is TB meningitis, where earlier ART can worsen outcomes and is often delayed. Starting both simultaneously on day 1 is not standard and increases pill burden, overlapping toxicity, and IRIS confusion. Deferring ART until TB therapy finishes leaves a profoundly immunosuppressed patient at high risk of death from other opportunistic infections. Withholding ART entirely is clearly wrong. Drug interactions (rifampin) may require rifabutin.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Two weeks after starting ART, he develops worsening fevers and enlarging lymph nodes despite microbiologic improvement. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Failure of TB therapy requiring a complete regimen change
New bacterial sepsis unrelated to TB
Drug-induced lupus
Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS)
stages.explanation: Paradoxical worsening, new or enlarging lymph nodes, recurrent fevers, and worsening infiltrates after ART initiation in a patient improving microbiologically is characteristic of immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome, driven by the recovering immune system mounting an inflammatory response against TB antigens; it is most common when ART is started with a very low CD4 count. Management is usually to continue both TB therapy and ART and add corticosteroids for significant symptoms, not to abandon the regimen. True treatment failure is less likely given microbiologic improvement and would be diagnosed by persistent positive cultures or resistance. New unrelated bacterial sepsis is possible but fits less well. Drug-induced lupus is unrelated here.

cases.diagnosis: Miliary (disseminated) tuberculosis in advanced HIV/AIDS, with possible CNS involvement.
pearls: Miliary TB is hematogenous dissemination with innumerable 1-3 mm millet-seed nodules; choroidal tubercles on funduscopy are highly specific.
Profound immunosuppression (CD4 under 50) predisposes to disseminated TB with multiorgan and CNS involvement; screen for meningitis.
Treat TB first, then start ART early (within ~2 weeks) when CD4 is very low, except in TB meningitis; watch for IRIS.
IRIS causes paradoxical worsening after ART; continue therapy and add steroids for severe cases rather than stopping treatment.

cases.title: Itchy, blistering rash all over the body
cases.chiefComplaint: She's covered in itchy spots and has had a fever for two days.
cases.history: A 7-year-old girl is brought to clinic by her mother for a widespread itchy rash. Two days ago she developed low-grade fever, malaise, and decreased appetite. Yesterday red spots appeared on her scalp and trunk; overnight many became small fluid-filled blisters, and some have already crusted over. The rash has since spread to her face and arms and is intensely itchy, causing her to scratch constantly. She has had no cough, difficulty breathing, neck stiffness, or lethargy, and is drinking adequately. Her immunizations are up to date except for the varicella vaccine, which her parents declined. She has never had chickenpox. Two classmates reportedly have a similar rash. Past medical history is unremarkable; she takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. She lives with both parents and a 4-month-old brother who is not yet vaccinated, and her mother is in the first trimester of pregnancy. Family history is noncontributory. She attends second grade and has missed two days of school.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, mildly uncomfortable child who is scratching frequently. Skin shows numerous lesions in several simultaneous stages — erythematous macules, papules, tense vesicles on an erythematous base ('dewdrop on a rose petal'), and honey-colored crusts — concentrated on the trunk, scalp, and face with fewer on the extremities. A few shallow ulcers are present on the soft palate. There is no surrounding warmth, fluctuance, or purulent drainage to suggest secondary bacterial infection. Conjunctivae are clear. Neck is supple without meningismus. Lungs are clear bilaterally with no respiratory distress. Abdomen is soft and nontender without hepatosplenomegaly. Neurologic exam is nonfocal with no ataxia.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of the rash most strongly supports the diagnosis?
options: Vesicles that are all at the same stage of development
Lesions in multiple stages — macules, vesicles, and crusts — present simultaneously
Targetoid lesions concentrated on the palms and soles
Grouped vesicles confined to a single dermatome
stages.explanation: Varicella classically produces crops of lesions that evolve at different rates, so macules, papules, vesicles ('dewdrop on a rose petal'), and crusts are all visible at once — the hallmark distinguishing it from other vesicular eruptions. Uniform, same-stage vesicles are characteristic of smallpox, a key historical board distinction. Targetoid lesions on the palms and soles suggest erythema multiforme or hand-foot-and-mouth disease, not varicella. Vesicles confined to one dermatome describe herpes zoster, which is reactivation of latent virus in a sensory ganglion and is uncommon in healthy young children. The widespread, centrally predominant distribution with simultaneous lesion stages in an unvaccinated child during a school outbreak makes primary varicella the clear diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS & BEDSIDE
stages.content:
Diagnosis is clinical; routine testing is not required.
If confirmation were needed: PCR of vesicle fluid — most sensitive and specific.
Tzanck smear: multinucleated giant cells (cannot distinguish VZV from HSV).
CBC: WBC 6.8 ×10³/µL, normal differential.
No leukocytosis or left shift to suggest bacterial superinfection.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate way to establish the diagnosis in this child?
options: Punch skin biopsy of an intact vesicle
Blood cultures drawn before any treatment
Clinical diagnosis based on the characteristic rash and exposure history
Serum VZV IgG titers prior to starting therapy
stages.explanation: Varicella is a clinical diagnosis in an otherwise healthy child: the combination of pruritic vesicles in multiple stages, centripetal distribution, recent exposure, and lack of prior immunization is sufficient, and no testing is needed before management. Skin biopsy is invasive and unnecessary for a self-limited viral exanthem. Blood cultures are appropriate only if bacterial sepsis or superinfection is suspected, which this well-appearing child lacks. Serum IgG titers reflect past immunity or prior infection and rise too late to guide acute diagnosis; they are useful for documenting immune status, not diagnosing acute disease. When laboratory confirmation is genuinely required (e.g., atypical presentation, immunocompromise), PCR of vesicle fluid is the test of choice.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this otherwise healthy 7-year-old?
options: Oral acyclovir is mandatory for all healthy children with varicella
Aspirin for fever control and oral amoxicillin to prevent superinfection
Topical and systemic corticosteroids to reduce pruritus
Supportive care: acetaminophen, oral antihistamines, calamine, and trimmed fingernails
stages.explanation: Healthy children with uncomplicated varicella are managed supportively: acetaminophen for fever, antihistamines and calamine for itch, good hydration, and trimmed nails to limit scratching and secondary bacterial infection. Routine antivirals are not recommended for healthy children under 12 because illness is self-limited; oral acyclovir is reserved for those at higher risk — adolescents/adults, secondary household cases, chronic skin or lung disease, and immunocompromised patients (IV therapy). Aspirin is contraindicated because of the risk of Reye syndrome in the setting of varicella; prophylactic antibiotics do not prevent superinfection and promote resistance. Systemic corticosteroids can worsen viral dissemination and are not used for routine pruritus. The priority is symptom relief and watching for complications such as bacterial superinfection, pneumonia, or neurologic signs.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The mother is pregnant and has a 4-month-old at home. What is the most appropriate counseling?
options: The child is contagious until all lesions have crusted over and should stay home; the susceptible pregnant mother and infant warrant evaluation for VZV exposure prophylaxis
