Acute hepatitis C
stages.explanation: A positive anti-HAV IgM with negative anti-HAV IgG confirms acute hepatitis A. Negative HBsAg and anti-HBc IgM exclude acute or chronic hepatitis B, and a negative HCV antibody argues against hepatitis C (and the fecal-oral, point-source exposure does not fit a parenteral virus). Autoimmune hepatitis would typically show positive autoantibodies (ANA, anti-smooth muscle) and a more chronic, relapsing course rather than this self-limited illness with a clear food exposure. The markedly elevated transaminases, hyperbilirubinemia, and outbreak context all point to acute hepatitis A. Hepatitis A virus is a non-enveloped RNA picornavirus shed in stool, and immunity after natural infection is lifelong, so this patient will not develop a chronic carrier state.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this patient?
options: Start tenofovir antiviral therapy
Begin pegylated interferon
Supportive care with monitoring for signs of liver failure
Urgent referral for liver transplantation
stages.explanation: Hepatitis A is self-limited with no chronic carrier state, so management is supportive: rest, hydration, antiemetics, avoidance of alcohol and hepatotoxins, and monitoring the INR and mental status to detect the rare progression to fulminant hepatic failure. Tenofovir is an antiviral used for hepatitis B and has no role in HAV. Pegylated interferon is used for selected chronic viral hepatitis, not an acute, self-resolving infection. Liver transplantation evaluation is reserved for acute liver failure (rising INR, encephalopathy), which this patient does not have (INR 1.1, no asterixis or confusion). Most patients recover fully within weeks.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Her unvaccinated household contacts ask how to protect themselves. What is the most appropriate post-exposure prophylaxis for a healthy adult close contact?
options: Hepatitis A vaccine
No prophylaxis is available or effective
A 2-week course of oral ribavirin
Hepatitis B immune globulin (HBIG)
stages.explanation: For healthy persons (notably ages 1–40), the hepatitis A vaccine is the preferred post-exposure prophylaxis when given within two weeks of exposure; immune globulin is preferred for infants under 1 year, older or immunocompromised patients, and those with chronic liver disease, and can be co-administered in some cases. Claiming no prophylaxis exists is incorrect. Ribavirin has no role in hepatitis A. Hepatitis B immune globulin (HBIG) is specific to hepatitis B exposures and does not protect against HAV. Vaccinating close contacts also helps interrupt the outbreak. Standard precautions and good hand hygiene further limit household fecal-oral transmission while she remains symptomatic and shedding virus.

cases.diagnosis: Acute hepatitis A
pearls: Hepatitis A is transmitted fecal-orally, often via contaminated food or water or an infected food handler, and causes a self-limited illness with no chronic carrier state.
Anti-HAV IgM confirms acute infection; anti-HAV IgG indicates past infection or vaccine immunity.
Management is supportive; monitor INR and mental status to detect rare fulminant hepatic failure.
Give close contacts post-exposure prophylaxis with hepatitis A vaccine (preferred for healthy ages 1–40) or immune globulin within two weeks.

cases.title: Weeks of fatigue, joint aches, and new jaundice
cases.chiefComplaint: I've been exhausted with achy joints, and now my eyes look yellow.
cases.history: A 40-year-old man presents with three weeks of progressive fatigue, anorexia, nausea, and low-grade fevers, accompanied by migratory joint aches and a brief urticarial rash that has since faded. Over the past four days he has noticed dark urine and yellowing of his eyes. He reports a new sexual partner about three months ago with inconsistent condom use, and he got a tattoo from a street artist roughly four months ago. He denies injection drug use. He has had no recent travel and no known sick contacts. His past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no regular medications and has no allergies. He drinks one or two beers on weekends and does not smoke. He has never been vaccinated against hepatitis B. Family history is notable for a maternal uncle with liver disease of unclear cause. He works as a chef and denies prior blood transfusions or episodes of jaundice.
cases.exam: The patient is alert and cooperative with obvious scleral icterus and mild jaundice. The abdomen is soft with mild right-upper-quadrant tenderness and a smooth, tender hepatic edge palpable 2 cm below the costal margin; there is no splenomegaly, ascites, or fluid wave. There are no stigmata of chronic liver disease such as spider angiomata, palmar erythema, gynecomastia, or caput medusae. There is no asterixis, and he is fully oriented without cognitive impairment. The skin shows no current rash, and there is no peripheral edema or lymphadenopathy. Joints are not swollen or erythematous on exam.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Beyond liver function tests, which panel will best establish the diagnosis and stage of infection?
options: Hepatitis B serologic panel (HBsAg, anti-HBc IgM, anti-HBs, HBeAg)
Anti-HAV IgG alone
Ferritin and transferrin saturation
Anti-smooth muscle and antinuclear antibodies
stages.explanation: His parenteral and sexual risk factors (a new partner, a recent tattoo), a serum sickness-like prodrome (migratory arthralgias and urticaria from immune-complex deposition), and now jaundice point to acute hepatitis B, and a full HBV serologic panel both confirms the diagnosis and stages it. Anti-HAV IgG reflects only past HAV exposure or immunity. Ferritin and transferrin saturation screen for hereditary hemochromatosis, a chronic disorder that does not explain this acute infectious illness. Anti-smooth muscle and antinuclear antibodies evaluate autoimmune hepatitis. The combination of HBsAg, anti-HBc IgM, anti-HBs, and HBeAg differentiates acute from chronic infection and gauges infectivity.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
AST 1,420 U/L, ALT 1,760 U/L
Total bilirubin 7.2 mg/dL
INR 1.2
HBsAg: POSITIVE
Anti-HBc IgM: POSITIVE
Total anti-HBc: POSITIVE
Anti-HBs: negative
HBeAg: POSITIVE
HBV DNA: 2,400,000 IU/mL
Anti-HAV IgM: negative; HCV antibody: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How do you interpret this serologic pattern?
options: Chronic hepatitis B infection
Immunity from prior vaccination
Acute hepatitis B infection
Resolved past hepatitis B infection
stages.explanation: A positive HBsAg together with a positive anti-HBc IgM indicates acute hepatitis B; the positive HBeAg and very high HBV DNA reflect active viral replication and high infectivity. Chronic hepatitis B shows HBsAg positivity persisting beyond six months with anti-HBc IgM negative (total anti-HBc positive). Vaccine-induced immunity shows isolated anti-HBs positivity with negative anti-HBc. Resolved past infection shows positive anti-HBs and total anti-HBc with negative HBsAg. The anti-HBc IgM positivity is the key marker establishing acute, rather than chronic or resolved, infection. Following these markers over time also predicts outcome: HBsAg clearance with anti-HBs seroconversion signals recovery, whereas persistence of HBsAg beyond six months defines progression to chronicity.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this immunocompetent adult?
options: Immediate tenofovir for all acute HBV infections
Supportive care with monitoring, reserving antivirals for severe or fulminant disease
Pegylated interferon for 48 weeks
A 12-week course of sofosbuvir/velpatasvir
stages.explanation: More than 95% of immunocompetent adults with acute hepatitis B clear the virus spontaneously, so management is supportive with monitoring of the INR and mental status for the rare fulminant case. Antiviral therapy (such as tenofovir or entecavir) is reserved for severe acute hepatitis—marked coagulopathy (e.g., INR >1.5), a protracted course, or acute liver failure. Reflexively treating every acute case is not indicated. Pegylated interferon is used in selected chronic HBV, not acute infection. Sofosbuvir/velpatasvir is a direct-acting antiviral regimen for hepatitis C, not hepatitis B. He should avoid alcohol and hepatotoxic drugs during recovery, and his INR and mental status should be tracked closely to detect the rare progression to acute liver failure.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which finding on follow-up testing would confirm that he has cleared the infection and developed immunity?
options: Persistence of HBsAg beyond six months
Appearance of HBeAg
Loss of HBsAg with development of anti-HBs
A rising HBV DNA level
stages.explanation: Recovery from acute hepatitis B is marked by clearance of HBsAg and seroconversion to anti-HBs, with anti-HBc persisting as a marker of past infection. Persistence of HBsAg beyond six months defines progression to chronic infection, the opposite of clearance. Appearance or persistence of HBeAg reflects ongoing replication and infectivity, not recovery. A rising HBV DNA level indicates active or worsening replication. He should also be counseled on transmission risk, and his sexual partner and close contacts should be tested and vaccinated. Anti-HBs is also the marker checked after vaccination to document a protective response, distinguishing vaccine-derived immunity from infection-derived immunity by the absence of anti-HBc.

cases.diagnosis: Acute hepatitis B
pearls: Acute hepatitis B can present with a serum sickness-like prodrome (arthralgias, urticaria) from immune-complex deposition before jaundice appears.
HBsAg plus anti-HBc IgM means acute infection; HBsAg persisting beyond six months without anti-HBc IgM means chronic; isolated anti-HBs means vaccine immunity.
Over 95% of immunocompetent adults clear acute HBV; antivirals are reserved for severe or fulminant disease.
Recovery is confirmed by HBsAg clearance and anti-HBs seroconversion; vaccinate exposed contacts and partners.

cases.title: Abnormal blood test found on routine screening
cases.chiefComplaint: My doctor said a screening blood test came back positive and I should get it checked.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man presents to discuss a positive hepatitis C antibody test obtained during routine age-based screening at his primary care visit. He feels well and denies fatigue, jaundice, abdominal pain, weight loss, easy bruising, or leg swelling. He recalls experimenting with injection drugs a few times in his twenties but none since. He drinks two or three beers on most weekends and has no history of blood transfusion before 1992. He has hypertension controlled on amlodipine 5 mg daily and is otherwise healthy. He has no known drug allergies, does not smoke, and works as a warehouse manager. He has never been treated for hepatitis and has no documented hepatitis A or B vaccination. Family history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes, with no family history of liver disease or liver cancer. He is sexually active with one long-term partner and takes no over-the-counter supplements or herbal products.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress. The sclerae are anicteric. The abdomen is soft and nontender without hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, ascites, or a palpable mass; there is no fluid wave or shifting dullness. The skin shows no jaundice, spider angiomata, or palmar erythema, and there is no gynecomastia. There is no asterixis and no peripheral edema. He is fully alert and oriented with no cognitive deficits. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable, and no lymphadenopathy is appreciated.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: He has a positive HCV antibody. What is the most appropriate next step to determine whether he has active infection?
options: Repeat the HCV antibody test in three months
Quantitative HCV RNA (PCR) testing
Liver biopsy
Begin direct-acting antiviral therapy empirically
stages.explanation: A positive HCV antibody indicates exposure but cannot distinguish active infection from spontaneously cleared past infection, so quantitative HCV RNA (PCR) is the essential next step to confirm active viremia. Repeating the antibody adds nothing, because it remains positive for life after exposure regardless of whether the virus persists. Liver biopsy is not needed to diagnose infection and is now rarely required, as noninvasive fibrosis assessment is available. Empiric antiviral therapy should never be started before confirming active infection and assessing genotype and fibrosis, which guide the regimen and duration.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
HCV antibody: POSITIVE
HCV RNA: 1,850,000 IU/mL (detectable)
HCV genotype: 1a
AST 58 U/L, ALT 72 U/L
Platelets 110 ×10³/µL
Albumin 3.9 g/dL, INR 1.1, total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL
FIB-4 score: 4.2 (high)
Transient elastography (FibroScan): 14.5 kPa, consistent with cirrhosis (F4)
Hepatitis B surface antigen and HIV serologies: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How is this patient best characterized?
options: Resolved (cleared) hepatitis C infection
Acute hepatitis C
Chronic hepatitis C with compensated cirrhosis
False-positive hepatitis C antibody
stages.explanation: Detectable HCV RNA with a remote exposure decades earlier confirms chronic hepatitis C, and the high FIB-4, thrombocytopenia, and elastography of 14.5 kPa indicate cirrhosis. Because his albumin, bilirubin, and INR are normal with no ascites or encephalopathy, the cirrhosis is compensated. Resolved infection would show undetectable HCV RNA. Acute hepatitis C is unlikely given an exposure decades ago and no recent risk. A false-positive antibody is excluded by detectable HCV RNA, which confirms true active infection. Quantifying fibrosis matters because cirrhosis changes management: it mandates hepatocellular carcinoma surveillance and screening for esophageal varices, and it can influence regimen choice and the duration of direct-acting antiviral therapy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Pegylated interferon plus ribavirin for 48 weeks
A pangenotypic direct-acting antiviral regimen (e.g., sofosbuvir/velpatasvir) for 12 weeks
Lifelong tenofovir
No treatment; observe because he is asymptomatic
stages.explanation: Pangenotypic direct-acting antiviral (DAA) regimens such as sofosbuvir/velpatasvir are well tolerated and cure more than 95% of patients in 12 weeks, including those with compensated cirrhosis. Interferon-based therapy is obsolete given poor tolerability and lower cure rates. Tenofovir treats hepatitis B, not C. Withholding treatment is inappropriate—essentially all patients with chronic HCV should be offered DAAs regardless of symptoms, and achieving cure in a cirrhotic patient lowers the risk of hepatic decompensation and slows disease progression. Before starting, he should be screened for hepatitis B (to anticipate reactivation risk) and have potential drug-drug interactions reviewed; adherence over the short course is the main determinant of cure.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Twelve weeks after completing therapy his HCV RNA is undetectable (sustained virologic response). Given his cirrhosis, what ongoing surveillance is required?
options: No further follow-up is needed now that he is cured
Repeat HCV antibody annually
Hepatocellular carcinoma surveillance with liver ultrasound every six months
Annual colonoscopy
stages.explanation: Achieving a sustained virologic response (cure) does not eliminate the hepatocellular carcinoma risk conferred by established cirrhosis, so patients with cirrhosis require lifelong HCC surveillance with abdominal ultrasound (with or without alpha-fetoprotein) every six months. Stopping all follow-up is unsafe in a cirrhotic patient. Repeating the HCV antibody is useless—it stays positive and does not reflect cure or reinfection (RNA does). Colonoscopy screens for colorectal cancer and is unrelated to his liver disease. He should also avoid alcohol and receive hepatitis A and B vaccination. Because cure clears the virus but does not reverse the structural cirrhosis, this twice-yearly surveillance must continue indefinitely.

cases.diagnosis: Chronic hepatitis C with compensated cirrhosis
pearls: A positive HCV antibody must be confirmed with HCV RNA to distinguish active infection from prior cleared exposure.
Pangenotypic direct-acting antivirals cure over 95% of chronic HCV in 8–12 weeks and are recommended for nearly all patients, including those with compensated cirrhosis.
Noninvasive tools (FIB-4, transient elastography) have largely replaced biopsy for staging fibrosis.
Patients with cirrhosis need ongoing hepatocellular carcinoma surveillance with ultrasound every six months even after viral cure.

cases.title: Spreading red, warm patch on the lower leg
cases.chiefComplaint: The skin on my shin has gotten red, warm, and tender over the last two days.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man presents with two days of expanding redness, warmth, swelling, and tenderness over his left lower leg. It began as a small area near the ankle and has spread up the shin. He feels mildly feverish but denies chills, and there is no pus, blister, or draining wound. He recalls a small scratch on his foot from gardening about a week ago and notes longstanding itchy, scaly skin between his toes. He has had no recent significant trauma, animal bites, or freshwater exposure. His past medical history includes obesity and chronic venous insufficiency with occasional ankle swelling. He takes no regular medications and has no drug allergies. He does not smoke and drinks alcohol socially. He had one similar episode in the same leg two years ago that resolved with oral antibiotics. He works as a landscaper, and family history is noncontributory.
cases.exam: There is a poorly demarcated area of erythema, warmth, and nonpitting edema over the anterior and medial left lower leg, tender to palpation, with borders that fade gradually into normal skin. There is no fluctuance, no pointing pustule, and no purulent drainage; no crepitus is felt. The interdigital spaces show macerated, scaly skin consistent with tinea pedis, a likely portal of entry. There is no lymphangitic streaking and no inguinal lymphadenopathy. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are intact, capillary refill is brisk, and sensation is normal. The contralateral leg is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: How is this skin and soft-tissue infection best classified, and what does that imply?
options: Purulent cellulitis, implying the need for incision and drainage
Non-purulent cellulitis, most often caused by beta-hemolytic streptococci
Necrotizing soft-tissue infection requiring urgent surgery
Stasis dermatitis, requiring no antibiotics
stages.explanation: Spreading erythema, warmth, edema, and tenderness without any abscess, fluctuance, or purulent drainage define non-purulent cellulitis, which is most commonly caused by beta-hemolytic streptococci (and less often methicillin-sensitive S. aureus). Because there is no pus, this is not purulent cellulitis or an abscess requiring incision and drainage. Pain out of proportion, crepitus, bullae, or systemic toxicity would suggest a necrotizing infection—none are present here. Stasis dermatitis is typically bilateral, chronic, and non-tender without fever, unlike this acute, unilateral, febrile, tender process with a clear portal of entry. Most cases are diagnosed clinically, and blood cultures or imaging are not routinely required in uncomplicated, non-toxic presentations like this one.

stages.label: BEDSIDE ASSESSMENT
stages.content:
WBC 11.8 ×10³/µL
Bedside point-of-care ultrasound: no drainable fluid collection or abscess
Blood cultures: not drawn (no systemic toxicity)
Borders of erythema outlined with a skin marker for monitoring
Distal neurovascular exam: intact

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate antibiotic therapy?
options: Oral cephalexin targeting streptococci and MSSA
Oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for MRSA
Oral metronidazole
Topical mupirocin alone
stages.explanation: Non-purulent cellulitis warrants empiric coverage of beta-hemolytic streptococci (and methicillin-sensitive S. aureus) with an agent such as cephalexin or dicloxacillin; routine MRSA coverage is unnecessary without purulence, penetrating trauma, or specific risk factors. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole covers MRSA but has relatively poor streptococcal activity, making it a poor sole choice for non-purulent cellulitis. Metronidazole targets anaerobes and is irrelevant here. Topical mupirocin treats impetigo or is used for decolonization, not an actively spreading cellulitis, which requires systemic antibiotics. If MRSA risk factors, penetrating trauma, purulence, or treatment failure later emerge, coverage can be broadened, but empiric anti-MRSA therapy is not warranted at the outset for typical non-purulent cellulitis.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate follow-up plan after starting oral antibiotics?
options: Admit for IV vancomycin
Mark the borders of erythema and re-evaluate in 48 hours, advising leg elevation
Obtain an MRI of the leg now
Switch to oral antibiotics only if blood cultures are positive
stages.explanation: For uncomplicated cellulitis without systemic toxicity, outpatient oral antibiotics with leg elevation and a marked erythema border allow objective assessment of response at 48–72 hours; some initial border expansion can occur as inflammation evolves, but worsening with fever or failure to improve prompts escalation. Admission for IV vancomycin is unnecessary without systemic toxicity, MRSA risk, or failure of oral therapy. MRI is reserved for suspected necrotizing infection or osteomyelitis. Blood cultures are low-yield in uncomplicated cellulitis and should not gate the start of therapy. Clear return precautions should be given for spreading redness, fever, blistering, or severe pain, which would suggest progression or a deeper, more serious infection.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: To reduce his risk of recurrent cellulitis in this leg, which intervention is most important?
options: Long-term daily oral antibiotics for life
Treating his tinea pedis and managing lower-extremity edema
Daily antiseptic leg soaks indefinitely
Avoiding compression stockings, which are contraindicated after cellulitis
stages.explanation: Interdigital tinea pedis is a common portal of entry for streptococci, and chronic edema impairs local defenses, so treating the tinea and reducing edema (elevation, weight management, and compression once the acute infection resolves) are the most effective measures to prevent recurrent cellulitis. Lifelong daily antibiotics are reserved for frequent recurrences despite addressing predisposing factors, not first-line. Antiseptic soaks have no proven preventive benefit. Compression therapy is actually beneficial for edema-related recurrent cellulitis and is not contraindicated. Good foot hygiene, keeping the interdigital spaces dry, and promptly treating recurrent tinea further reduce the likelihood of future episodes in a predisposed leg.

cases.diagnosis: Non-purulent cellulitis (most likely streptococcal)
pearls: Non-purulent cellulitis (no abscess or drainage) is usually streptococcal; empiric therapy targets strep and MSSA with cephalexin or dicloxacillin, without routine MRSA coverage.
Mark the erythema border and reassess at 48–72 hours; lack of improvement or systemic toxicity prompts escalation.
Tinea pedis and chronic edema are common portals and risk factors; treating them prevents recurrence.
Bilateral, chronic, non-tender, afebrile leg redness suggests stasis dermatitis, not cellulitis—avoid unnecessary antibiotics.

cases.title: Sudden bright-red, swollen face with fever and chills
cases.chiefComplaint: My cheek turned bright red and swollen overnight and I have fever and chills.
cases.history: A 65-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with the abrupt onset, over less than 24 hours, of fever, shaking chills, and a rapidly enlarging, painful, bright-red swelling on her right cheek. She first noticed a stinging, hot patch the previous evening that has since become markedly swollen and shiny. She reports a recent minor crack at the corner of her nose from a cold. She denies eye pain, visual changes, trismus, or recent dental procedures. Her past medical history includes hypertension and well-controlled type 2 diabetes. Medications are lisinopril and metformin, and she has no drug allergies. She does not smoke and rarely drinks alcohol. She has had no facial trauma, insect bites, or new cosmetics, and no prior episodes like this. She lives alone and is fully independent. Family history is noncontributory, and her immunizations are up to date.
cases.exam: On the right cheek there is a sharply demarcated, raised, intensely erythematous, warm, and tender plaque with a well-defined border clearly elevated above the surrounding normal skin, extending toward the bridge of the nose. The surface has a tense, shiny, 'peau d'orange' appearance, without fluctuance, pustules, necrosis, or crepitus. There is mild tender right submandibular and preauricular lymphadenopathy. Extraocular movements are intact, the eye is not proptotic, and there is no pain with eye movement or vision change. There is no facial droop. The oropharynx and dentition appear normal, and the remainder of the skin exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes this condition from ordinary cellulitis?
options: Presence of purulent drainage
Sharply demarcated, raised borders involving the superficial dermis
Pain out of proportion to examination with crepitus
A chronic, bilateral, non-tender distribution
stages.explanation: Erysipelas is a superficial dermal and lymphatic infection that produces a sharply demarcated, raised, indurated plaque with a clearly palpable, elevated border—distinct from cellulitis, which is deeper with indistinct margins that fade gradually into normal skin. Purulent drainage suggests an abscess or purulent cellulitis, not erysipelas, which is typically non-suppurative. Pain out of proportion with crepitus signals necrotizing fasciitis, a surgical emergency that this well-defined, raised, stable plaque does not fit. A chronic, bilateral, non-tender pattern describes stasis dermatitis rather than an acute febrile infection. The face and lower legs are the most common sites, and the abrupt onset with chills and a glistening, indurated, well-bordered plaque is characteristic of this superficial dermal process.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
WBC 16.4 ×10³/µL (neutrophil predominance)
CRP 142 mg/L
Glucose 168 mg/dL
Blood cultures: pending
Facial exam: no orbital involvement; vision and extraocular movements intact

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely causative organism?
options: Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA)
Pseudomonas aeruginosa
Streptococcus pyogenes (group A streptococcus)
Clostridium perfringens
stages.explanation: Erysipelas is most commonly caused by beta-hemolytic streptococci, predominantly Streptococcus pyogenes (group A streptococcus), which spread through superficial dermal lymphatics to produce the characteristic raised, sharply bordered plaque. Staphylococcus aureus more often causes purulent infections and abscesses rather than classic erysipelas. Pseudomonas aeruginosa is associated with specific exposures (hot tubs, neutropenia, puncture wounds), not typical facial erysipelas. Clostridium perfringens causes gas gangrene (myonecrosis) with crepitus and profound systemic toxicity, which is not this presentation. Because cultures of intact skin are usually unrevealing, the diagnosis is clinical and empiric therapy is directed at streptococci; blood cultures are reserved for systemically ill or immunocompromised patients.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given her fever and facial involvement, what is the most appropriate initial therapy?
options: Oral acyclovir
Topical mupirocin alone
Intravenous antibiotics active against streptococci (e.g., penicillin or ceftriaxone)
Incision and drainage
stages.explanation: Erysipelas with systemic signs (fever, tachycardia) and facial involvement warrants intravenous antibiotics targeting streptococci, such as penicillin G or ceftriaxone, transitioning to oral therapy once improving; milder cases without systemic symptoms can be treated orally with penicillin or amoxicillin. Acyclovir treats herpetic infections, not bacterial erysipelas. Topical mupirocin is inadequate for an invasive, febrile dermal infection. Incision and drainage is for fluctuant abscesses; erysipelas is non-suppurative and has nothing to drain. Most patients improve within 24–48 hours of appropriate antibiotics as the erythema fades and fever resolves; failure to respond should prompt reconsideration of the diagnosis, an occult abscess, or a resistant organism.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is an important next step in managing this patient?
options: Identify and treat the skin portal of entry and monitor her response
Start broad antifungal therapy
Obtain urgent CT and surgical consultation for debridement
Discharge immediately on no treatment since it is self-limited
stages.explanation: Identifying and addressing the portal of entry (here, the cracked nasal skin) reduces recurrence, and monitoring her clinical response, glucose control, and for complications is essential. Antifungals are not indicated for a streptococcal infection. Urgent CT and surgical debridement are reserved for suspected necrotizing infection or orbital/deep extension—her well-demarcated superficial plaque without orbital signs, crepitus, or pain out of proportion does not warrant this. Discharging without treatment is unsafe, as untreated erysipelas can progress to systemic infection. Optimizing her glycemic control and reassessing within 48 hours to confirm clinical improvement are also part of appropriate follow-up for this diabetic patient.

cases.diagnosis: Erysipelas (group A streptococcal superficial dermal infection)
pearls: Erysipelas is a superficial dermal and lymphatic infection with sharply demarcated, raised borders and abrupt fever—distinct from the indistinct margins of deeper cellulitis.
It is almost always caused by beta-hemolytic streptococci, chiefly Streptococcus pyogenes; penicillin is first-line.
Look for and treat a portal of entry (skin cracks, tinea, fissures) to prevent recurrence.
Pain out of proportion, crepitus, bullae, or rapid necrosis should redirect you toward necrotizing fasciitis, a surgical emergency.

cases.title: Severe leg pain and confusion after a minor wound
cases.chiefComplaint: His leg pain is unbearable and he's becoming confused (reported by family).
cases.history: A 58-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes is brought to the emergency department and transferred to the ICU for two days of rapidly worsening left leg pain that he describes as far more severe than the appearance of his skin would suggest. The pain began near a small puncture wound he sustained while working in his yard. Over the past 12 hours the leg has become increasingly swollen and discolored, and his family notes he is now febrile, lethargic, and intermittently confused. He has had chills, nausea, and decreased urination. His past medical history includes type 2 diabetes with a recent HbA1c of 10.8%, hypertension, and obesity. Medications include metformin and lisinopril, and he has no drug allergies. He drinks alcohol heavily on weekends and does not smoke. He has had no recent travel or saltwater exposure. Family history is significant for diabetes. He lives with his wife, works as a mechanic, and has had no prior surgery on the leg.
cases.exam: The patient is toxic-appearing, diaphoretic, and intermittently confused. The left lower leg is markedly swollen with dusky, mottled, and erythematous skin extending proximally beyond the area of tenderness; there are tense hemorrhagic bullae and a small wound draining thin, foul, 'dishwater'-colored fluid. Palpation reveals crepitus, and there are patches of skin that are anesthetic to light touch despite the patient's report of severe pain. The erythema margins advance visibly over a few hours. Distal pulses are diminished but present. He is tachypneic with cool, clammy extremities and delayed capillary refill, consistent with early septic shock. No other skin lesions are noted.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Beyond fluid resuscitation and broad-spectrum antibiotics, what is the most important immediate priority?
options: Urgent surgical consultation for operative exploration and debridement
Obtain an MRI of the leg before any intervention
Apply warm compresses and elevate the limb
Start IV heparin for presumed deep vein thrombosis
stages.explanation: The combination of pain out of proportion, rapidly spreading dusky skin, hemorrhagic bullae, crepitus, skin anesthesia, 'dishwater' drainage, and septic shock indicates a necrotizing soft-tissue infection—a surgical emergency in which early operative exploration and debridement is the single greatest determinant of survival. Imaging such as MRI should never delay surgery when suspicion is high; delays increase mortality. Warm compresses and elevation are wholly inadequate and dangerous. Anticoagulation for DVT does not address the infection, and the picture (crepitus, bullae, toxicity) is not that of a clot. Surgery alongside resuscitation and antibiotics is mandatory.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 24.5 ×10³/µL with bandemia
Sodium 128 mEq/L
Glucose 412 mg/dL
Creatinine 2.1 mg/dL (baseline 1.0)
C-reactive protein 280 mg/L
Lactate 4.8 mmol/L
Creatine kinase 1,450 U/L
Plain radiograph: soft-tissue gas tracking along the fascial planes
LRINEC score: 9 (high risk)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: These findings are most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Uncomplicated cellulitis
Deep vein thrombosis with phlegmasia
Necrotizing fasciitis
Erysipelas
stages.explanation: Soft-tissue gas along fascial planes, a high LRINEC score, hyponatremia, elevated creatine kinase (reflecting muscle and soft-tissue necrosis), leukocytosis with bandemia, and lactic acidosis in a diabetic with pain out of proportion confirm necrotizing fasciitis. Uncomplicated cellulitis does not cause crepitus, bullae, anesthesia, gas on imaging, or shock. DVT with phlegmasia produces a swollen, painful limb but not fascial gas, dishwater discharge, or skin necrosis. Erysipelas is a superficial, sharply demarcated, non-necrotic infection without crepitus or systemic collapse. The diagnosis is clinical and surgical; imaging supports but must not delay intervention.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While arranging emergent surgery, which empiric antibiotic regimen is most appropriate?
options: Oral cephalexin
Ceftriaxone alone
Metronidazole alone
Vancomycin plus piperacillin-tazobactam plus clindamycin
stages.explanation: Empiric therapy for necrotizing fasciitis must be broad: cover MRSA (vancomycin or linezolid), gram-negatives and anaerobes (piperacillin-tazobactam or a carbapenem), plus clindamycin, which suppresses streptococcal and staphylococcal toxin and exotoxin production (the 'Eagle effect') and is especially valuable in group A streptococcal and clostridial disease. Oral cephalexin is far too narrow, and the oral route is inappropriate in shock. Ceftriaxone alone misses MRSA, anaerobes, and toxin suppression. Metronidazole alone covers only anaerobes. Antibiotics are adjunctive and do not substitute for surgical debridement. They should be started immediately and not held for culture results, then narrowed once operative tissue and blood cultures identify the responsible organisms.

stages.label: PITFALL
stages.question: The surgical team is delayed and a clinician suggests waiting for an MRI to 'confirm' the diagnosis before operating. What is the best course of action?
options: Proceed to immediate operative exploration; do not delay surgery for imaging
Wait for MRI confirmation before any surgery
Trial 24 hours of IV antibiotics and reassess
Discharge with oral antibiotics and close follow-up
stages.explanation: Necrotizing fasciitis is a clinical diagnosis, and definitive treatment is emergent surgical exploration and debridement; every hour of delay increases mortality. When clinical suspicion is high—as here, with crepitus, bullae, shock, and gas on plain film—imaging adds little and dangerously delays the operating room. A 24-hour antibiotic trial allows ongoing necrosis and death, because antibiotics cannot adequately penetrate avascular, necrotic tissue. Discharge is clearly unsafe in septic shock. Surgical exploration also confirms the diagnosis directly (gray necrotic fascia, lack of bleeding, easy finger dissection of tissue planes). When the diagnosis is uncertain, an early bedside surgical exploration or small incision to inspect the fascia is preferable to a time-consuming scan.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After initial debridement and ICU stabilization, what is an essential component of ongoing management?
options: A single debridement is definitive; no further surgery is needed
Immediate limb amputation in all cases
Planned repeat operative re-exploration within 24-48 hours to debride residual necrosis
Stopping antibiotics once the patient leaves the operating room
stages.explanation: Necrotizing infections almost always require planned serial re-exploration every 24–48 hours until all necrotic tissue is removed and the infection is controlled, because necrosis often progresses beyond the initially debrided margins. Assuming one debridement is definitive risks ongoing necrosis and death. Amputation is sometimes necessary but is not mandatory in every case and is reserved for uncontrollable infection or a nonviable limb. Antibiotics must continue—de-escalated by culture results—alongside source control, resuscitation, and ICU support; stopping them prematurely is harmful. Adjuncts such as vasopressors, organ support, and intravenous immunoglobulin in selected streptococcal toxic shock cases may be needed, but none replace repeated, adequate surgical source control.

cases.diagnosis: Necrotizing fasciitis (necrotizing soft-tissue infection)
pearls: Pain out of proportion to exam, rapid progression, crepitus, hemorrhagic bullae, skin anesthesia, and 'dishwater' drainage signal necrotizing fasciitis.
It is a surgical emergency—early, aggressive debridement saves lives, and imaging must never delay the operating room.
Empiric antibiotics are broad (MRSA plus gram-negatives/anaerobes) plus clindamycin for toxin suppression, but they are adjuncts to surgery.
Plan serial re-exploration every 24-48 hours; one debridement is rarely sufficient.

cases.title: Painful, pus-filled lump on the thigh
cases.chiefComplaint: I have a painful red bump on my thigh that's filling up with pus.
cases.history: A 28-year-old man presents with five days of a progressively enlarging, painful, red lump on his right outer thigh. It started as a small tender bump and now has a central area that appears to be filling with pus, with surrounding redness. He denies fever, chills, or red streaks spreading up the leg. He is an avid weightlifter and recreational wrestler who shares gym equipment and towels, and a teammate recently had a similar 'boil.' He has no diabetes or immunosuppression and takes no medications. He has had two similar skin boils over the past year, one of which drained on its own. He has no known drug allergies, does not smoke, and drinks alcohol occasionally. He works as a personal trainer. Family history is noncontributory. He has not traveled recently and has no history of injection drug use.
cases.exam: On the right anterolateral thigh there is a 3 cm tender, erythematous, fluctuant nodule with a central pointing pustule and a surrounding rim of erythema extending roughly 1 cm beyond the nodule. The lesion is warm and exquisitely tender, and gentle palpation demonstrates a fluctuant, fluid-filled center. There is no crepitus, no extensive surrounding cellulitis, and no lymphangitic streaking. The inguinal lymph nodes are not enlarged. Distal sensation, capillary refill, and pulses are intact. The patient is well-appearing and afebrile, and no other skin lesions or sinus tracts are seen.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the primary treatment for this lesion?
options: Oral antibiotics alone
Incision and drainage
Warm compresses and observation only
Topical mupirocin
stages.explanation: A fluctuant, pointing cutaneous abscess is treated primarily by incision and drainage—evacuating the purulent collection is both diagnostic and therapeutic, and antibiotics alone do not adequately treat a walled-off abscess. Warm compresses may help a small early furuncle point but are insufficient for an established 3 cm fluctuant abscess. Topical mupirocin cannot penetrate a deep purulent collection. While adjunctive systemic antibiotics are often added (especially for larger abscesses or surrounding cellulitis), the indispensable, primary intervention is drainage—the pus must be released. Sending the drained purulence for culture and susceptibilities also guides any adjunctive antibiotic choice and identifies resistant organisms in this recurrent presentation.

stages.label: GRAM STAIN & CULTURE
stages.content:
Incision and drainage performed; moderate purulent material expressed and sent for culture
Gram stain: gram-positive cocci in clusters
Wound culture: Staphylococcus aureus, oxacillin-resistant (MRSA)
Susceptibilities: susceptible to trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, doxycycline, and clindamycin
Afebrile with no signs of systemic toxicity

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What organism is the most likely cause, given the culture and his risk factors?
options: Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA)
Streptococcus pyogenes
Pseudomonas aeruginosa
Pasteurella multocida
stages.explanation: Gram-positive cocci in clusters growing as oxacillin-resistant S. aureus confirm community-associated MRSA, the most common cause of purulent skin abscesses, and his risk factors (contact sports, shared equipment and towels, a teammate with a 'boil,' and recurrent furuncles) fit the classic CA-MRSA epidemiology. Streptococcus pyogenes typically causes non-purulent cellulitis or erysipelas rather than a discrete abscess and appears as cocci in chains. Pseudomonas aeruginosa is a gram-negative rod tied to specific exposures such as hot tubs or puncture wounds. Pasteurella multocida follows cat or dog bites, which he has not had.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given the surrounding cellulitis and his history, which adjunctive oral antibiotic is most appropriate after drainage?
options: Amoxicillin
Penicillin V
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole
Cephalexin
stages.explanation: When systemic antibiotics are added to incision and drainage—indicated here by surrounding cellulitis, an abscess larger than 2 cm, and recurrent disease—coverage should target MRSA, so trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (or doxycycline or clindamycin) is appropriate and matches the susceptibilities. Amoxicillin, penicillin V, and cephalexin all lack reliable activity against MRSA: cephalexin covers MSSA and streptococci but not MRSA, and the penicillins are even narrower. Adding an anti-MRSA oral agent after adequate drainage improves cure and reduces recurrence in this contact-sport athlete. A small, fully drained abscess without surrounding cellulitis or systemic signs can often be managed with drainage alone, so antibiotics are an adjunct rather than a substitute for adequate incision and drainage.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: To reduce his recurrent abscesses, what is the most appropriate recommendation?
options: Lifelong daily oral antibiotics
Hygiene measures and consideration of decolonization (e.g., intranasal mupirocin and chlorhexidine washes)
Avoid all physical exercise permanently
Prophylactic IV vancomycin monthly
stages.explanation: For recurrent community-associated MRSA skin infections, personal and environmental hygiene (not sharing towels, razors, or equipment; regular hand and equipment cleaning; covering wounds) plus consideration of a decolonization regimen—intranasal mupirocin with chlorhexidine body washes, sometimes including household contacts—is the recommended strategy. Lifelong daily antibiotics promote resistance and are not standard. Telling him to stop all exercise is impractical and unnecessary if hygiene is addressed. Monthly prophylactic IV vancomycin is inappropriate, carries toxicity and resistance risks, and has no role in outpatient abscess prevention. Educating him to clean shared gym equipment, avoid sharing personal items, and cover draining lesions also limits transmission to teammates and household members.

cases.diagnosis: MRSA cutaneous abscess (community-associated MRSA)
pearls: Incision and drainage is the primary, indispensable treatment for a fluctuant cutaneous abscess—antibiotics alone are inadequate.
Community-associated MRSA is the leading cause of purulent skin abscesses, especially in athletes sharing equipment and in recurrent furunculosis.
Add an anti-MRSA oral antibiotic (TMP-SMX, doxycycline, or clindamycin) for larger abscesses, surrounding cellulitis, systemic signs, or recurrence.
Manage recurrent disease with hygiene measures and decolonization rather than chronic antibiotics.

cases.title: Non-healing foot ulcer with new drainage and redness
cases.chiefComplaint: The sore on the bottom of my foot is draining and the area around it is getting red.
cases.history: A 62-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes is admitted with a plantar foot ulcer that has been present for several weeks and has recently developed increased purulent, malodorous drainage with spreading redness and warmth around it. He reports a low-grade fever but denies severe pain, attributing this to longstanding numbness in his feet. He frequently walks barefoot at home and did not notice when the ulcer began. His diabetes is poorly controlled with a recent HbA1c of 9.6%, and he has known peripheral neuropathy and peripheral arterial disease. Medications include metformin, insulin glargine, and atorvastatin, and he has no drug allergies. He smokes half a pack of cigarettes daily and rarely drinks alcohol. He has no prior foot surgery or amputation. He lives with his wife and works as a bus driver. Family history includes diabetes and coronary disease. He has not been hospitalized recently and has had no recent antibiotics.
cases.exam: On the plantar surface over the first metatarsal head there is a 2 cm ulcer with surrounding erythema, warmth, and induration extending about 3 cm, with purulent malodorous drainage at its base. Using a sterile blunt probe, bone is palpable at the base of the ulcer (positive probe-to-bone test). Sensation to a 10-g monofilament is absent over the forefoot, consistent with peripheral neuropathy. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are diminished bilaterally, and the skin is cool with loss of hair over the distal leg. There is no crepitus, no fluctuant abscess, and no proximal lymphangitic streaking. The contralateral foot shows callus but no ulceration.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the deep ulcer with a positive probe-to-bone test, what is the most appropriate initial diagnostic step to evaluate for underlying bone infection?
options: Plain radiograph of the foot plus inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP)
Immediate below-knee amputation
MRI is the only acceptable first test; do not order radiographs
Surface swab of the ulcer for culture to guide therapy
stages.explanation: A positive probe-to-bone test in a deep diabetic foot ulcer substantially raises the likelihood of osteomyelitis, and the appropriate initial workup is a plain radiograph (looking for cortical erosion, periosteal reaction, or lytic change) together with inflammatory markers such as ESR and CRP, which support the diagnosis and help track response. Amputation is premature without staging and vascular assessment. MRI is the most sensitive imaging test and is valuable when radiographs are equivocal, but plain films are the reasonable, inexpensive first step. Superficial swabs are unreliable—they grow colonizers and do not reflect deep or bone pathogens.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.6 ×10³/µL
ESR 82 mm/hr
CRP 96 mg/L
Glucose 286 mg/dL, HbA1c 9.6%
Probe-to-bone test: positive
Plain radiograph of the foot: cortical erosion and periosteal reaction at the first metatarsal head
Ankle-brachial index: 0.7 (mild-to-moderate peripheral arterial disease)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the gold standard for confirming it and directing therapy?
options: Soft-tissue cellulitis only; confirm with blood cultures
Charcot arthropathy; confirm with nerve conduction studies
Osteomyelitis; confirm with bone biopsy and culture
Gout; confirm with joint aspiration for crystals
stages.explanation: A positive probe-to-bone test, markedly elevated ESR and CRP, and radiographic cortical erosion with periosteal reaction in a chronic diabetic foot ulcer indicate osteomyelitis, and bone biopsy with culture is the gold standard, giving a definitive microbiologic diagnosis to guide targeted, often prolonged antibiotic therapy. Isolated soft-tissue cellulitis would lack bone involvement and bony radiographic changes. Charcot arthropathy causes bony destruction and deformity but is non-infectious, typically without an overlying infected ulcer draining pus and high inflammatory markers. Gout presents with acute monoarticular crystal arthritis, not a chronic draining plantar ulcer with exposed bone.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate empiric management approach while awaiting cultures?
options: Topical antibiotics and continued home weight-bearing
Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (gram-positives including MRSA, gram-negatives, and anaerobes) with debridement, offloading, and glycemic and vascular optimization
Oral fluconazole and observation
A single dose of IV antibiotics then discharge without follow-up
stages.explanation: A limb-threatening, deep diabetic foot infection with probable osteomyelitis warrants broad-spectrum empiric IV antibiotics covering gram-positive cocci including MRSA (given severity and risk), gram-negative bacilli, and anaerobes (a chronic, malodorous ulcer), combined with surgical debridement of necrotic and infected tissue and bone, offloading the ulcer, tight glycemic control, and vascular assessment given his peripheral arterial disease. Topical antibiotics with continued weight-bearing are grossly inadequate and perpetuate tissue breakdown. Fluconazole targets fungi, not the bacterial pathogens here. A single antibiotic dose with no follow-up abandons a serious infection needing source control and prolonged therapy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Bone biopsy grows MRSA. What is the most appropriate definitive antibiotic plan?
options: A 3-day course of oral cephalexin
Culture-directed therapy, typically about 6 weeks of antibiotics active against MRSA
Indefinite lifelong oral antibiotics regardless of cultures
Stop all antibiotics once the wound looks better
stages.explanation: Confirmed osteomyelitis is treated with culture-directed antibiotics for a prolonged course—generally about 6 weeks when infected bone is retained—using an agent active against the isolated pathogen, here MRSA (e.g., vancomycin, with an appropriate oral step-down once stable). Durations shorten substantially (roughly 1–2 weeks) only if all infected bone is fully resected. A 3-day cephalexin course is far too short and does not cover MRSA. Indefinite lifelong antibiotics are unnecessary and harmful. Stopping based on wound appearance alone risks relapse, since bone infection requires a defined, adequate course alongside ongoing offloading and glucose control.

cases.diagnosis: Diabetic foot infection with underlying osteomyelitis
pearls: A positive probe-to-bone test in a deep diabetic foot ulcer markedly increases the likelihood of osteomyelitis.
Start with plain radiographs and ESR/CRP; MRI is most sensitive when films are equivocal, and bone biopsy with culture is the diagnostic gold standard.
Treat limb-threatening infections with broad empiric coverage (gram-positives including MRSA, gram-negatives, anaerobes) plus debridement, offloading, glycemic control, and vascular assessment.
Osteomyelitis with retained bone typically needs about 6 weeks of culture-directed antibiotics; durations shorten only if infected bone is fully resected.

cases.title: Excruciating leg pain after a crush injury
cases.chiefComplaint: My leg hurts way more than it should and it's turning dark.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man presents 18 hours after a motorcycle crash in which his left thigh was pinned beneath the bike. He was seen at an urgent care, where a deep laceration was irrigated and loosely closed. Over the past 6 hours he developed escalating, out-of-proportion pain in the thigh, now severe and unrelenting despite oxycodone. He reports the wound is leaking a thin, foul, brownish fluid and the skin has become discolored. He feels feverish, lightheaded, and nauseated. PMH: type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 8.6%) on metformin 1000 mg twice daily. No known drug allergies. Social: works in construction, smokes half a pack daily, drinks socially. Family history noncontributory. Immunizations: unsure of last tetanus booster. He denies prior wound infections, recent antibiotics, or IV drug use. The pain has progressed so rapidly that he can no longer bear weight, and he noticed a crackling sensation when he touched the area an hour ago.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, diaphoretic man in severe distress. Left thigh markedly swollen and tense with bronze-brown skin discoloration and several tense hemorrhagic bullae. A draining laceration exudes thin, malodorous, serosanguinous fluid. Palpable crepitus extends from the knee to the inguinal region. Tenderness is far out of proportion to visible findings; the overlying skin is partially anesthetic. Distal pulses are diminished but present; capillary refill 3 seconds. No fluctuance suggestive of a simple abscess. Cardiopulmonary exam notable for tachycardia. Mentation intact but anxious. No nuchal rigidity. Remainder of skin exam without additional lesions.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A 45-year-old man has out-of-proportion pain, crepitus, and hemorrhagic bullae after a crush injury. What is the most appropriate immediate priority?
options: Obtain an MRI of the thigh to delineate the extent of soft tissue involvement before any intervention
Apply a compressive dressing, elevate the limb, and reassess in 2 hours
Order an outpatient wound culture and discharge with oral amoxicillin-clavulanate
Begin IV fluid resuscitation and broad-spectrum antibiotics, and obtain emergent surgical consultation for exploration and debridement
stages.explanation: This presentation—pain out of proportion to exam, crepitus, hemorrhagic bullae, systemic toxicity, and rapid progression after a contaminated crush wound—is classic for necrotizing soft tissue infection/clostridial myonecrosis, a surgical emergency. The priority is aggressive fluid resuscitation, immediate broad-spectrum antibiotics, and emergent surgical exploration with debridement, which is both diagnostic and definitive. Obtaining an MRI first dangerously delays surgical care and should never postpone debridement when necrotizing infection is suspected. A compressive dressing with watchful waiting is hazardous given the fulminant trajectory. Outpatient oral antibiotics and discharge would be fatal in this rapidly advancing, limb- and life-threatening process. Time to surgical debridement is the strongest determinant of survival, so source control cannot wait for confirmatory imaging.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 22.4 ×10³/µL with left shift
Lactate 5.8 mmol/L
Na 129 mEq/L
Creatinine 1.9 mg/dL
CK 4,200 U/L
Glucose 268 mg/dL
Plain film left thigh: feathery gas tracking along muscle planes
Gram stain of wound fluid: large gram-positive rods with sparse neutrophils

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The Gram stain shows large gram-positive rods with strikingly scant inflammatory cells. What is the most likely organism?
options: Streptococcus pyogenes
Staphylococcus aureus
Clostridium perfringens
Vibrio vulnificus
stages.explanation: Large, boxcar-shaped, gram-positive rods with a strikingly scant inflammatory infiltrate (toxins destroy neutrophils) in a crush wound producing gas are characteristic of Clostridium perfringens, the classic cause of gas gangrene (clostridial myonecrosis). Streptococcus pyogenes causes necrotizing fasciitis and appears as gram-positive cocci in chains, not large rods. Staphylococcus aureus is a gram-positive coccus in clusters and does not produce this gas-forming myonecrosis picture. Vibrio vulnificus is a curved gram-negative rod tied to seawater or raw shellfish exposure and chronic liver disease, not crush trauma. The combination of paucity of neutrophils, gas tracking through tissue planes, and bronze hemorrhagic bullae strongly points to clostridial infection.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Along with surgical debridement, what is the best antibiotic regimen for clostridial myonecrosis?
options: Penicillin G plus clindamycin
Vancomycin monotherapy
Metronidazole monotherapy
Ceftriaxone plus azithromycin
stages.explanation: The standard regimen for clostridial myonecrosis is high-dose penicillin G combined with clindamycin. Penicillin is bactericidal against Clostridium, while clindamycin suppresses alpha-toxin production by inhibiting ribosomal protein synthesis and remains effective at high bacterial burden where penicillin alone falters (the rationale behind the Eagle effect). Vancomycin monotherapy lacks the toxin-suppressing benefit and is reserved for empiric MRSA coverage within a broader regimen. Metronidazole monotherapy is inadequate and not first-line for clostridial myonecrosis. Ceftriaxone plus azithromycin targets respiratory pathogens, not this anaerobic toxin-mediated process. Antibiotics are adjunctive—surgical debridement remains the cornerstone of therapy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Beyond surgery and antibiotics, which adjunctive therapy is most appropriate to consider?
options: Systemic corticosteroids
Hyperbaric oxygen therapy
Therapeutic plasma exchange
Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF)
stages.explanation: Hyperbaric oxygen (HBO) therapy is a recognized adjunct in clostridial myonecrosis; it raises tissue oxygen tension, inhibits alpha-toxin production, and is detrimental to the strict anaerobe Clostridium perfringens. It is used alongside—never instead of—aggressive surgical debridement and antibiotics, and must not delay the operating room. Systemic corticosteroids have no role and may worsen infection. Therapeutic plasma exchange is not indicated for clostridial myonecrosis. G-CSF treats neutropenia and would not help in this toxin-mediated infection. Evidence for HBO is limited but supportive when available; the patient must be hemodynamically stable enough to tolerate the chamber, and surgical source control always comes first.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Post-debridement he develops hemoglobinuria, rising bilirubin, a rapidly falling hematocrit, and hemodynamic collapse. What is the mechanism?
options: Disseminated intravascular coagulation from gram-negative endotoxin
Rhabdomyolysis-induced pigment nephropathy alone
Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction
Massive intravascular hemolysis from clostridial alpha-toxin (lecithinase/phospholipase C)
stages.explanation: Clostridium perfringens alpha-toxin is a phospholipase C (lecithinase) that hydrolyzes membrane phospholipids, causing massive intravascular hemolysis—producing hemoglobinuria, a rapidly falling hematocrit, jaundice, and shock. This fulminant hemolysis is a hallmark of severe clostridial sepsis and carries very high mortality. Gram-negative endotoxin-driven DIC is not the primary mechanism, as Clostridium is gram-positive and its toxin directly lyses red cells. Rhabdomyolysis with pigment nephropathy contributes to renal injury but does not explain the brisk hemolysis and jaundice. An acute hemolytic transfusion reaction is unlikely given the clinical context and timing. Recognition mandates continued aggressive source control, transfusion support, and ICU care.

cases.diagnosis: Clostridial myonecrosis (gas gangrene) due to Clostridium perfringens following a crush wound
pearls: Pain out of proportion to exam, crepitus, and hemorrhagic bullae after trauma = necrotizing infection until proven otherwise—go to the OR, don't wait for imaging.
Clostridial myonecrosis Gram stain shows large gram-positive rods with strikingly few neutrophils (toxin kills PMNs).
Treat with penicillin G PLUS clindamycin—clindamycin suppresses alpha-toxin production.
Alpha-toxin (lecithinase/phospholipase C) causes massive intravascular hemolysis; surgical debridement is the definitive lifesaving treatment.

cases.title: Rapidly swelling, painful hand after an animal bite
cases.chiefComplaint: My cat bit my hand a few hours ago and now it's red, swollen, and really painful.
cases.history: A 35-year-old woman presents to the ED 8 hours after her indoor cat bit the dorsum of her right hand during play. Within 3 to 4 hours she noted rapidly spreading redness, swelling, and throbbing pain around two puncture wounds. She reports warmth and serous drainage but no pus. She has full finger range of motion, though movement is increasingly painful. She denies fever, chills, numbness, or red streaking up the arm. PMH: unremarkable; no diabetes or immunosuppression. Medications: combined oral contraceptive pill only. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, works as a graphic designer. Last tetanus booster 9 years ago. The cat is an indoor pet, current on rabies vaccination, and remains healthy and observable. She washed the wound with soap and water at home. She is right-hand dominant and concerned about function. No prior hand infections or surgeries. The rapid onset of cellulitis within hours prompted her ED visit.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in mild discomfort. Right hand with two puncture wounds over the dorsum at the level of the metacarpophalangeal joints, surrounded by a 4 cm area of erythema, edema, and warmth with mild serous drainage and no frank purulence. Tenderness localized to the wounds; pain with active and passive finger flexion is present but full range of motion is preserved. No fluctuance, no crepitus. No flexor-sheath tenderness along the volar surface and no fixed flexion posture. Epitrochlear and axillary nodes nontender; no lymphangitic streaking. Neurovascular exam intact with brisk capillary refill and normal sensation. Remainder of exam unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What pathogen most likely caused rapidly progressive cellulitis within hours of this cat bite?
options: Staphylococcus aureus
Capnocytophaga canimorsus
Pasteurella multocida
Eikenella corrodens
stages.explanation: Pasteurella multocida is the most common pathogen in cat (and many dog) bite infections and is notorious for causing rapidly progressive cellulitis within hours of the bite—much faster than staphylococcal or streptococcal infections, which usually take 24 to 72 hours. Staphylococcus aureus and streptococci cause later-onset wound infections. Capnocytophaga canimorsus is associated with dog bites and causes fulminant sepsis, especially in asplenic or cirrhotic patients, not this rapid localized cellulitis. Eikenella corrodens is the classic organism of human bites and clenched-fist injuries, not cat bites. The very short interval (3 to 4 hours) to cellulitis plus deep puncture wounds from sharp feline teeth strongly point to Pasteurella.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the first-line antibiotic for this cat-bite infection?
options: Amoxicillin-clavulanate
Cephalexin
Dicloxacillin
Clindamycin
stages.explanation: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is first-line for cat and dog bite wounds because it covers Pasteurella multocida, oral anaerobes, streptococci, and staphylococci in one agent. Cephalexin and dicloxacillin are inadequate because they have poor activity against Pasteurella multocida—a classic pitfall, since these anti-staphylococcal agents miss the key pathogen. Clindamycin alone also lacks reliable Pasteurella coverage and is typically paired with a fluoroquinolone rather than used as monotherapy. For penicillin-allergic patients, a fluoroquinolone or doxycycline plus an anti-anaerobe agent (metronidazole or clindamycin) is appropriate. Pasteurella's resistance to first-generation cephalosporins and clindamycin reinforces amoxicillin-clavulanate as the standard.

stages.label: WOUND CARE & WORKUP
stages.content:
Plain film right hand: no fracture, no foreign body, no subcutaneous gas
WBC 9.8 ×10³/µL
Wound appearance: two puncture wounds, no drainable abscess
Tetanus status: last booster 9 years ago
Biting animal: healthy, vaccinated, observable indoor cat

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Regarding wound closure and prophylaxis, which approach is most appropriate?
options: Primarily suture the puncture wounds closed and discharge
Close the wounds and start rabies post-exposure prophylaxis immediately
Apply tissue adhesive to seal the puncture sites
Leave the puncture wounds open (no primary closure), irrigate copiously, and give a tetanus (Tdap) booster
stages.explanation: Puncture-type bite wounds—especially on the hand—should generally be left open rather than primarily closed, because closure traps bacteria and increases infection risk; copious irrigation and debridement of devitalized tissue are key. Because her last tetanus-containing vaccine was 9 years ago, a tetanus (Tdap) booster is indicated for this tetanus-prone wound. Primarily suturing the wounds or applying tissue adhesive is contraindicated for these high-risk hand puncture wounds. Immediate rabies post-exposure prophylaxis is not indicated here: the biting animal is a healthy, vaccinated, observable indoor cat, so 10-day observation is appropriate. Hand bites also warrant close follow-up and hand-surgery involvement if deep structures are affected.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which feature would most prompt escalation to IV antibiotics and an urgent hand-surgery consult?
options: Mild erythema that has not expanded after starting oral antibiotics
Pain limited to the puncture sites
A finger held in fixed flexion with fusiform swelling and severe pain on passive extension
Low-grade serous drainage from the puncture site
stages.explanation: A finger held in fixed (semiflexed) posture, fusiform digit swelling, tenderness along the flexor tendon sheath, and severe pain on passive extension are Kanavel's signs of flexor tenosynovitis—a closed-space infection requiring IV antibiotics and emergent hand-surgery evaluation for possible irrigation and debridement. Mild, stable erythema after starting antibiotics suggests adequate response. Pain confined to the puncture sites and low-grade serous drainage are expected early findings that do not by themselves mandate escalation. Hand bites carry a high complication rate (septic arthritis, osteomyelitis, tenosynovitis); any sign of deep-space involvement should lower the threshold for admission, IV ampicillin-sulbactam, and surgical consultation. Close 24 to 48 hour follow-up is essential for outpatient-managed bites.

cases.diagnosis: Pasteurella multocida cellulitis following a cat bite to the hand
pearls: Cellulitis appearing within hours of a cat/dog bite suggests Pasteurella multocida; staph/strep infections usually take more than 24 hours.
Amoxicillin-clavulanate is first-line for bite wounds; cephalexin, dicloxacillin, and clindamycin miss Pasteurella.
Leave bite puncture wounds open (avoid primary closure), irrigate well, and update tetanus.
Kanavel's signs (fusiform swelling, flexor-sheath tenderness, fixed flexion, pain on passive extension) signal flexor tenosynovitis—a surgical emergency.

cases.title: Swollen tender lump under the arm in a boy
cases.chiefComplaint: There's a painful lump in my armpit that's been there for a couple weeks.
cases.history: A 12-year-old boy is brought to clinic by his mother for a tender lump in the right axilla present for about 2 weeks and slowly enlarging. About 10 days before the lump appeared, he was scratched on the right forearm by a new kitten the family adopted. A small reddish bump developed at the scratch site and has persisted. He has had low-grade fevers, mild fatigue, and decreased appetite but no night sweats or weight loss. No sore throat, cough, or rashes elsewhere. He is otherwise healthy, fully immunized, takes no medications, and has no allergies. He attends middle school, plays soccer, and has had no recent travel, tick exposure, or sick contacts. Family history is unremarkable. The kitten is playful and frequently scratches and bites during play. There is no history of tuberculosis exposure, and a prior PPD was negative. The mother is worried because the lump has not improved and is now visibly swollen and warm. He denies trouble breathing or difficulty swallowing.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, comfortable adolescent. On the right volar forearm there is a 5 mm erythematous, crusted papule consistent with a healing inoculation site. In the right axilla there is a 3 cm tender, mobile, mildly erythematous lymph node with overlying warmth but no fluctuance or draining sinus. No epitrochlear adenopathy. No cervical, supraclavicular, or generalized lymphadenopathy. Oropharynx clear; no conjunctival lesions. No hepatosplenomegaly on abdominal exam. Skin without additional rashes or eschars. Lungs clear; heart regular without murmur. No joint swelling. The remainder of the neurologic and abdominal examination is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a healing inoculation papule and tender regional adenopathy after a kitten scratch, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Hodgkin lymphoma
Cat-scratch disease (Bartonella henselae)
Atypical (nontuberculous) mycobacterial lymphadenitis
Sporotrichosis
stages.explanation: The classic triad of a kitten exposure, a papule or pustule at the inoculation site, and tender regional lymphadenopathy 1 to 2 weeks later is diagnostic of cat-scratch disease caused by Bartonella henselae. Hodgkin lymphoma typically causes painless, firm, rubbery, progressively enlarging nodes (often cervical or supraclavicular) with drenching night sweats and weight loss—not a preceding inoculation papule. Atypical (nontuberculous) mycobacterial lymphadenitis usually affects young children with cervical nodes that develop violaceous skin and draining sinuses, without a scratch history. Sporotrichosis follows a thorn or plant inoculation (rose gardener) with nodules ascending along lymphatics, not a kitten scratch. The temporal link to the kitten and the inoculation papule make Bartonella by far the most likely.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate confirmatory test?
options: Excisional lymph node biopsy for all patients
Monospot (heterophile antibody) test
Bartonella henselae serology (IgG/IgM) with PCR if needed
Interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA)
stages.explanation: Cat-scratch disease is usually a clinical diagnosis confirmed serologically with Bartonella henselae IgG/IgM titers; PCR of tissue or aspirate is highly specific when needed. Excisional biopsy for everyone is unnecessary and not first-line; biopsy or aspiration is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty or to drain a suppurative, painful node. A Monospot screens for EBV mononucleosis, which causes generalized adenopathy and pharyngitis—not a localized inoculation papule after a scratch. An IGRA tests for tuberculosis, which is lower on the differential given the clear kitten exposure and inoculation lesion. Histopathology, if obtained, shows necrotizing granulomas with stellate microabscesses and Warthin-Starry-positive bacilli.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Bartonella henselae IgG titer: 1:512 (elevated)
WBC 8.2 ×10³/µL
ESR 32 mm/hr
Ultrasound right axilla: enlarged hypoechoic lymph node, no drainable abscess
HIV screen: negative

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best management for this immunocompetent child with a tender, symptomatic node?
options: Azithromycin
No antibiotics; observation with supportive care only
Surgical excision of the node
Amoxicillin-clavulanate for 14 days
stages.explanation: In a child with substantial, symptomatic lymphadenopathy from cat-scratch disease, azithromycin is the preferred antibiotic; a short course hastens the decrease in lymph node size. While cat-scratch disease is often self-limited and observation alone is reasonable for minimal symptoms, this patient has a sizable, tender, warm node with constitutional symptoms, making azithromycin appropriate. Surgical excision is not indicated for routine adenopathy and is reserved for diagnostic dilemmas; needle aspiration can relieve a painful suppurative node. Amoxicillin-clavulanate treats Pasteurella bite-wound infections, not Bartonella, and is ineffective for cat-scratch disease. Most cases resolve over 2 to 4 months regardless of therapy.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication should you counsel about and screen for if new symptoms arise?
options: Acute glomerulonephritis
Rheumatic fever
Guillain-Barré syndrome
Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome and, rarely, hepatosplenic microabscesses or encephalopathy
stages.explanation: Although most cat-scratch disease is benign and self-limited, recognized complications include Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome (granulomatous conjunctivitis with ipsilateral preauricular adenopathy from ocular inoculation), hepatosplenic disease (microabscesses causing prolonged fever and abdominal pain), and, rarely, encephalopathy or neuroretinitis. Acute glomerulonephritis and rheumatic fever are post-streptococcal complications, not Bartonella. Guillain-Barré syndrome classically follows Campylobacter or certain viral infections, not cat-scratch disease. In immunocompromised hosts, Bartonella henselae can instead cause bacillary angiomatosis or peliosis hepatis, requiring prolonged antibiotics. Counsel the family that the node may persist for weeks to months and to return for high fevers, eye involvement, abdominal pain, or neurologic changes.

cases.diagnosis: Cat-scratch disease caused by Bartonella henselae
pearls: Inoculation papule plus tender regional adenopathy 1 to 2 weeks after a kitten scratch = cat-scratch disease (Bartonella henselae).
Confirm with Bartonella serology; biopsy shows necrotizing granulomas with stellate microabscesses and Warthin-Starry-positive bacilli.
Azithromycin speeds node resolution in symptomatic disease; most cases are self-limited over weeks to months.
Watch for Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome, hepatosplenic microabscesses, and bacillary angiomatosis in the immunocompromised.

cases.title: Painful blistering rash on one side of the chest
cases.chiefComplaint: I have a burning, painful rash that wraps around the left side of my back and chest.
cases.history: A 70-year-old woman presents with a 3-day history of a painful rash on the left side of her trunk. Two days before the rash, she felt a burning, tingling, and itching sensation in the same area, which she initially attributed to a pulled muscle. The rash began as red patches and progressed to grouped fluid-filled blisters confined to a band on the left flank that does not cross the midline. The pain is sharp and burning, rated 7/10, and interferes with sleep. She denies fever, headache, vision changes, or facial involvement. PMH: hypertension and osteoarthritis. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, acetaminophen as needed. Allergies: none. She has not received the recombinant zoster vaccine. Social: retired teacher, lives with her husband, no tobacco or alcohol. She had chickenpox as a child. No recent immunosuppressant use, no known immunodeficiency, and no contact with anyone with active varicella. She is otherwise healthy and concerned about the ongoing pain and whether she is contagious to her grandchildren.
cases.exam: Comfortable but uncomfortable from pain. Skin exam reveals grouped vesicles and a few pustules on an erythematous base, distributed in a unilateral band over the left T8 to T9 dermatome, stopping abruptly at the midline anteriorly and posteriorly. Some lesions are crusting; no lesions on the opposite side and none disseminated beyond the dermatome. The area is exquisitely tender and hyperesthetic to light touch (allodynia). No involvement of the face, eye, or tip of the nose. No vesicles in the ear canal. Cranial nerves intact. No nuchal rigidity or focal weakness. Remaining skin and systemic examination unremarkable.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given a prodrome of pain followed by a unilateral dermatomal vesicular rash, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Contact dermatitis
Bullous impetigo
Herpes zoster (shingles)
Disseminated herpes simplex
stages.explanation: A prodrome of burning/tingling pain followed by grouped vesicles on an erythematous base confined to a single dermatome that does not cross the midline is the textbook presentation of herpes zoster (reactivation of latent varicella-zoster virus in a dorsal root ganglion). Contact dermatitis is pruritic rather than painful and follows areas of contact, not a dermatome. Bullous impetigo presents with honey-colored crusts and flaccid bullae, typically in children, and is neither dermatomal nor preceded by neuralgic pain. Disseminated herpes simplex is generally not restricted to one dermatome and lacks this classic banding; HSV more often causes orolabial or genital grouped vesicles. The unilateral, dermatomal pattern with allodynia is essentially diagnostic of zoster.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her rash is 3 days old. What is the best decision regarding antiviral therapy?
options: No antiviral; topical antibiotic only
Start oral valacyclovir now, since she is within 72 hours of rash onset
Antivirals are useless once any rash appears; treat pain only
Start IV acyclovir and admit
stages.explanation: Oral antiviral therapy (valacyclovir, famciclovir, or acyclovir) is most effective when started within 72 hours of rash onset; it shortens viral shedding and lesion healing and reduces the severity and duration of acute pain. This patient is at 3 days—within the window—so starting valacyclovir is appropriate. A topical antibiotic alone does not treat the viral process. Saying antivirals are useless is incorrect—and even beyond 72 hours they are still recommended in older patients or those with new lesions, ophthalmic involvement, or complications given the high postherpetic neuralgia risk. IV acyclovir with admission is reserved for disseminated zoster, ophthalmic or neurologic involvement, or immunocompromised hosts—not uncomplicated dermatomal zoster in a well outpatient.

stages.label: CLINICAL COURSE
stages.content:
