At 1-week follow-up: lesions crusted over, no new vesicles
Pain improved to 3/10 on valacyclovir and scheduled acetaminophen
No ocular symptoms, no dissemination beyond the dermatome
Confirmatory testing if needed: VZV PCR of vesicle fluid (Tzanck smear shows multinucleated giant cells but is nonspecific)

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: She is 70 and worried about lingering pain. What is the most common complication, and how do you counsel her?
options: Postherpetic neuralgia, whose risk rises with age; antivirals reduce but don't eliminate it
Permanent motor paralysis of the trunk
Progression to systemic varicella pneumonia in all elderly patients
Guaranteed bacterial superinfection requiring oral antibiotics
stages.explanation: Postherpetic neuralgia (PHN)—dermatomal pain persisting beyond about 90 days after rash onset—is the most common complication of zoster, and its incidence and severity rise markedly with age, making this 70-year-old high risk. Early antiviral therapy reduces, but does not fully eliminate, the risk; treatment options include gabapentin or pregabalin, tricyclic antidepressants, and topical lidocaine or capsaicin. Permanent truncal motor paralysis is not a feature; segmental motor weakness is rare and usually transient. Varicella pneumonia is a concern in disseminated or immunocompromised disease, not typical localized zoster. Bacterial superinfection can occur but is not guaranteed and is treated only if it develops. Counsel her about possible lingering pain and the value of prompt antivirals.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: After this episode resolves, what do you recommend for prevention?
options: No vaccine needed since natural infection gives lifelong immunity
Live attenuated varicella vaccine immediately during the acute rash
Lifelong daily valacyclovir prophylaxis
Recombinant zoster vaccine (Shingrix), two doses, after the acute episode resolves
stages.explanation: The recombinant zoster vaccine (Shingrix), given as two doses, is recommended for immunocompetent adults aged 50 and older—including those who have already had an episode of zoster—to prevent recurrence and reduce PHN risk; it is given after the acute episode resolves. Assuming natural infection confers lasting protection is wrong: recurrent zoster occurs, and vaccination is still advised. The live attenuated varicella vaccine is not the appropriate product for zoster prevention in this age group and should never be given during an acute rash, especially since the non-live recombinant vaccine is preferred. Lifelong antiviral prophylaxis is not standard for immunocompetent patients. Note lesions are infectious (can cause varicella in susceptible contacts) until crusted, so she should avoid non-immune and immunocompromised contacts until then.

cases.diagnosis: Herpes zoster (shingles) involving the left T8–T9 dermatome
pearls: Painful prodrome followed by grouped vesicles in a unilateral dermatome that stops at the midline = herpes zoster.
Start antivirals within 72 hours of rash onset (still treat older/complicated patients later) to shorten the course and reduce pain.
Postherpetic neuralgia is the most common complication and rises sharply with age; antivirals reduce but don't eliminate the risk.
Recommend the 2-dose recombinant zoster vaccine (Shingrix) for adults 50 and older, even after a prior episode; lesions are contagious until crusted.

cases.title: Crusty sores around a child's nose and mouth
cases.chiefComplaint: My daughter has these oozing, crusty sores on her face that keep spreading.
cases.history: A 5-year-old girl is brought to clinic by her father for honey-colored crusted sores around the nose and mouth that started 4 days ago. It began as a single small red bump near her right nostril that quickly became a blister, then broke open and crusted. New similar lesions have appeared nearby, and she has been scratching them. She is otherwise well—eating, drinking, and playing normally—with no fever. There is a possible history of a recent insect bite she had been picking at. She attends kindergarten, where a classmate reportedly had similar sores. PMH: mild eczema. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Immunizations up to date. No recent sore throat. Social: lives with both parents and a younger sibling; no pets. Family history unremarkable. The father is concerned about contagiousness and whether she can return to school. She has had no dark or tea-colored urine, no facial swelling, and no decrease in urination. There is no surrounding spreading redness suggestive of deeper infection.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, playful, afebrile child. On the right perinasal and perioral skin there are several discrete erythematous lesions, some with thin-walled vesicles or pustules and others covered by characteristic golden, honey-colored crusts. Lesions are superficial with minimal surrounding erythema and no induration. No deep ulceration, no fluctuance, and no surrounding cellulitic spreading erythema or warmth. No regional lymphangitis; small, mildly tender submandibular nodes are palpable. Oropharynx clear without exudate. No periorbital swelling. Skin elsewhere shows mild flexural eczema but no additional impetiginous lesions. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams normal; no edema.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Honey-colored crusted superficial lesions around the nose and mouth—what is the most likely diagnosis and usual organism?
options: Non-bullous impetigo, usually Staphylococcus aureus and/or Streptococcus pyogenes
Herpes simplex gingivostomatitis
Erysipelas from group A streptococcus
Eczema herpeticum
stages.explanation: Superficial vesicles or pustules that rupture and form golden honey-colored crusts around the nose and mouth in a well-appearing child are classic for non-bullous impetigo, most often caused by Staphylococcus aureus and/or Streptococcus pyogenes. Herpes simplex gingivostomatitis causes painful grouped oral or perioral vesicles and ulcers, often with fever and gingival involvement, not honey crusts. Erysipelas is a raised, sharply demarcated, intensely erythematous, tender plaque involving the deeper dermis and lymphatics with systemic symptoms—not superficial crusting. Eczema herpeticum is a herpes superinfection of eczematous skin producing monomorphic punched-out erosions in a systemically ill child; despite her eczema, the honey-colored crusts and well appearance fit impetigo. Spread among classmates further supports impetigo.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has a few localized lesions and is well-appearing. What is the best first-line treatment?
options: Oral acyclovir
Oral amoxicillin
Topical mupirocin
Topical corticosteroid
stages.explanation: For limited, localized non-bullous impetigo, topical antibiotic therapy with mupirocin (or retapamulin) is first-line and as effective as oral agents while limiting systemic exposure and resistance. Oral acyclovir treats herpetic infections, not bacterial impetigo. Oral antibiotics are reserved for numerous lesions, widespread or bullous disease, systemic symptoms, or outbreaks—and if needed should cover S. aureus (e.g., cephalexin or dicloxacillin), since amoxicillin alone has poor staphylococcal coverage. A topical corticosteroid treats inflammation or eczema and would not address the bacterial infection (and could worsen it). Gentle crust removal with soap and water plus good hand hygiene are useful adjuncts; children can typically return to school 24 hours after starting effective therapy.

stages.label: COUNSELING & FOLLOW-UP
stages.content:
No fever; child remains well-appearing
Lesions limited to the perinasal and perioral region
Advised hand hygiene, avoid sharing towels, keep nails short
Return-to-school after 24 hours of effective treatment
No hematuria, edema, or hypertension at this visit

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The father asks whether these sores can hurt her kidneys. What is the best counseling?
options: Impetigo commonly causes acute rheumatic fever, which antibiotics prevent
Impetigo can rarely be followed by post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis; antibiotic treatment does not reliably prevent it, so watch for dark urine and swelling
There is no association between skin infections and kidney disease
She will need lifelong dialysis if the sores are from strep
stages.explanation: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN) is a recognized, though uncommon, complication following group A streptococcal impetigo, typically appearing 1 to 3 weeks later with hematuria (tea-colored urine), edema, and hypertension. Importantly, unlike with strep pharyngitis, antibiotic treatment of the skin infection does NOT reliably prevent PSGN, so counseling and watchful waiting for dark urine, facial or leg swelling, and decreased urination are appropriate. Acute rheumatic fever follows streptococcal pharyngitis, NOT skin infection, so impetigo does not cause it. Saying there is no association between skin infection and kidney disease is incorrect. Claiming inevitable lifelong dialysis is false—pediatric PSGN is usually self-limited with supportive care and a good prognosis.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: To limit spread to siblings and classmates, what is the best advice?
options: Keep her home from school indefinitely until all crusts are gone, even if it takes weeks
Treat the entire household with oral antibiotics prophylactically
No precautions are necessary; impetigo is not contagious
Hand hygiene, avoid sharing towels/linens, cover lesions, and return to school 24 hours after starting treatment
stages.explanation: Impetigo spreads by direct contact, so the best measures are good hand hygiene, not sharing towels, linens, or clothing, keeping fingernails short, covering lesions when possible, and returning to school 24 hours after starting effective therapy—the standard exclusion period. Keeping her out indefinitely until every crust resolves is excessive once she is treated. Empirically treating the whole household with antibiotics is not indicated unless others develop lesions. Saying no precautions are needed is wrong, since autoinoculation and person-to-person spread are common. Treating active lesions, addressing the underlying eczema, and reinforcing hygiene help prevent recurrence and transmission to the sibling and classmates.

cases.diagnosis: Non-bullous impetigo (Staphylococcus aureus and/or Streptococcus pyogenes)
pearls: Honey-colored crusts around the nose/mouth in a well child = non-bullous impetigo (S. aureus and/or S. pyogenes).
Limited impetigo: treat with topical mupirocin; reserve oral antibiotics (with staph coverage) for extensive, bullous, or systemic disease.
PSGN can follow streptococcal impetigo and is NOT prevented by antibiotics—rheumatic fever follows pharyngitis, not skin infection.
Children can return to school 24 hours after starting effective therapy; reinforce hand hygiene to prevent spread.

cases.title: Fever, severe headache, and confusion
cases.chiefComplaint: My husband has a high fever, a terrible headache, and he's getting confused.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man is brought to the ED by his wife for 1 day of worsening fever, severe global headache, neck stiffness, and increasing confusion. He felt unwell with chills the prior evening and rapidly deteriorated overnight, becoming lethargic and disoriented this morning. He had an upper respiratory infection and ear pain last week. He reports photophobia and one episode of vomiting. No recent travel, no rash noted by his wife, no seizures. PMH: type 2 diabetes and a remote splenectomy after a car accident; he is unsure if his vaccinations are current. Medications: metformin. Allergies: none. Social: works as an accountant, drinks alcohol moderately, no tobacco or drugs. No sick contacts with similar illness. He has no known immunodeficiency beyond asplenia and diabetes. His wife notes he is normally sharp and the confusion is dramatically out of character. He has had no head trauma. There is no prior history of meningitis or neurosurgery, and no recent antibiotic use.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, lethargic man who is arousable but disoriented to time and place (GCS 13). Marked nuchal rigidity with positive Kernig and Brudzinski signs. Photophobia present. No focal motor deficits; pupils equal and reactive. No papilledema on fundoscopic exam. The right tympanic membrane is erythematous and dull, consistent with otitis media. No petechial or purpuric rash on skin survey. Cardiopulmonary exam notable for tachycardia; lungs clear. Abdomen soft with a well-healed surgical scar. No seizure activity observed. Neck range of motion markedly limited by pain and stiffness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A 55-year-old man has fever, nuchal rigidity, and altered mentation. What is the single most important first management step?
options: Obtain CT head, then LP, and start antibiotics only after CSF results return
Draw blood cultures and immediately start empiric antibiotics plus dexamethasone without delay
Start acyclovir and await an MRI brain
Give acetaminophen and observe for 6 hours
stages.explanation: In suspected acute bacterial meningitis, empiric antibiotics must be given as early as possible—after blood cultures—because delay increases mortality. Dexamethasone should be given with or just before the first antibiotic dose. When LP will be delayed (e.g., CT needed first for altered mentation or immunocompromise), antibiotics and steroids are given first and LP performed afterward; brief antibiotic pretreatment does not negate CSF analysis. Waiting for CSF results before treating dangerously delays therapy. Acyclovir with MRI targets HSV encephalitis, which is lower priority here given clear meningismus and a parameningeal (otitis) source—though acyclovir can be added if encephalitis is also suspected. Acetaminophen and observation are unacceptable in a potentially fatal infection.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before LP, which feature in THIS patient warrants a CT head first?
options: Photophobia
Fever alone
Neck stiffness
Altered mental status and immunocompromise (asplenia, diabetes)
stages.explanation: CT of the head before LP is recommended when there is concern for raised intracranial pressure or a mass lesion that could predispose to herniation—indications include altered or decreased consciousness, focal neurologic deficits, new seizures, papilledema, immunocompromised state, or known CNS disease. This patient has altered mental status and is immunocompromised (asplenia, diabetes), so CT before LP is prudent—though antibiotics and dexamethasone must be given first and not delayed for imaging. Photophobia, isolated fever, and neck stiffness are typical meningitis features but are not by themselves indications for pre-LP CT. The key point: imaging should never delay empiric therapy in suspected bacterial meningitis.

stages.label: LABS & CSF
stages.content:
WBC 19.8 ×10³/µL with left shift
Serum glucose 180 mg/dL
CT head: no mass, no hydrocephalus; opacified right mastoid air cells
CSF opening pressure 32 cm H2O
CSF WBC 4,200 /µL (90% neutrophils)
CSF glucose 22 mg/dL
CSF protein 240 mg/dL
CSF Gram stain: gram-positive diplococci

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CSF shows neutrophilic pleocytosis, low glucose, high protein, and gram-positive diplococci. What is the most likely organism?
options: Neisseria meningitidis
Listeria monocytogenes
Streptococcus pneumoniae
Enterovirus
stages.explanation: Gram-positive diplococci in CSF with a neutrophil-predominant pleocytosis, markedly low CSF glucose, and high protein indicate Streptococcus pneumoniae, the most common cause of community-acquired bacterial meningitis in adults—especially with a parameningeal source (otitis media or mastoiditis) and in an asplenic, diabetic host at high risk for encapsulated organisms. Neisseria meningitidis is a gram-negative diplococcus and often presents with a petechial or purpuric rash. Listeria monocytogenes is a gram-positive rod seen in neonates, the elderly, pregnant, and immunocompromised patients and would prompt adding ampicillin. Enterovirus causes aseptic meningitis with lymphocytic pleocytosis and normal glucose, not this purulent profile. The findings here are textbook pneumococcal meningitis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best empiric antibiotic regimen for this adult with suspected pneumococcal meningitis?
options: Vancomycin plus ceftriaxone (add ampicillin if Listeria risk)
Ceftriaxone alone
Ampicillin alone
Meropenem plus acyclovir
stages.explanation: Empiric therapy for adult bacterial meningitis is vancomycin PLUS a third-generation cephalosporin (ceftriaxone or cefotaxime); vancomycin covers potentially penicillin- or cephalosporin-resistant pneumococci until susceptibilities return. In patients over 50 or with immunocompromise or Listeria risk, ampicillin is added—reasonable here given asplenia and diabetes. Ceftriaxone alone inadequately covers resistant pneumococci. Ampicillin alone does not provide reliable pneumococcal coverage and misses resistant strains. Meropenem plus acyclovir is not standard first-line; acyclovir targets HSV encephalitis. Dexamethasone (given with or before antibiotics) improves outcomes in pneumococcal meningitis by reducing hearing loss and mortality and should be continued once pneumococcus is confirmed. After recovery, this asplenic patient needs catch-up vaccination against encapsulated organisms.

cases.diagnosis: Streptococcus pneumoniae bacterial meningitis with a parameningeal otomastoid source
pearls: Give empiric antibiotics plus dexamethasone immediately in suspected bacterial meningitis—never delay for CT or LP.
CT head before LP only for altered consciousness, focal deficits, seizures, papilledema, or immunocompromise.
Bacterial CSF: neutrophilic pleocytosis, low glucose, high protein; gram-positive diplococci = S. pneumoniae.
Empiric adult therapy = vancomycin + ceftriaxone; add ampicillin if Listeria risk (over 50, immunocompromised); dexamethasone helps in pneumococcal disease.

cases.title: Fever and spreading purple spots in a college student
cases.chiefComplaint: I feel awful—high fever, and these purple spots are showing up all over my legs.
cases.history: A 19-year-old male college freshman is brought to the ED by friends for 12 hours of rapidly worsening fever, severe headache, myalgias, and a spreading rash. He felt fine yesterday but woke with chills, vomiting, and leg pain so severe he could barely walk. Over the past few hours, purple spots appeared on his legs and trunk and are multiplying. He lives in a crowded dormitory; he is unsure whether he received the meningococcal vaccine. He reports neck stiffness and photophobia and has become progressively drowsy en route. PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: drinks alcohol on weekends, no smoking, no drugs; sexually active with one partner. No recent travel. No known sick contacts, though a hallmate had a bad cold. Family history noncontributory. His friends note he is normally healthy and athletic, and the speed of his decline alarmed them. He has had no prior similar episodes and no chronic illness.
cases.exam: Toxic-appearing, drowsy young man (GCS 13) with cool, mottled extremities and delayed capillary refill (4 seconds). Diffuse non-blanching petechiae and several larger palpable purpuric lesions over the legs, trunk, and conjunctivae. Nuchal rigidity present with a positive Brudzinski sign and photophobia. Tachycardic with thready pulses and hypotension. Lungs clear. No focal neurologic deficit; pupils equal and reactive. Extremities with early acrocyanosis of the digits. No murmur. The rash does not blanch under pressure. Mucous membranes dry; he is reportedly oliguric.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A toxic dorm resident has fever with rapidly spreading non-blanching purpura and hypotension. What are the immediate priorities?
options: Isolate the patient, draw cultures, give empiric IV ceftriaxone, and start aggressive IV fluids/vasopressors for shock without delay
Obtain a dermatology consult for the rash before treatment
Give oral antibiotics and arrange urgent outpatient follow-up
Perform LP immediately before any antibiotics or fluids
stages.explanation: This is fulminant meningococcemia with septic shock—a true emergency. Priorities are droplet isolation, immediate blood cultures, prompt empiric IV ceftriaxone, and aggressive hemodynamic resuscitation with IV fluids and vasopressors as needed; antibiotics must not be delayed. A dermatology consult wastes critical time on an obvious clinical diagnosis. Oral antibiotics with outpatient follow-up would be rapidly fatal. Performing LP first is wrong: the patient is hemodynamically unstable and coagulopathic, so LP is deferred while antibiotics and resuscitation proceed. Recognizing non-blanching purpura with shock and getting antibiotics in within the first hour are the interventions that save lives in meningococcal sepsis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely organism and its key Gram stain finding?
options: Streptococcus pneumoniae—gram-positive diplococci
Haemophilus influenzae—gram-negative coccobacilli
Neisseria meningitidis—gram-negative diplococci
Listeria monocytogenes—gram-positive rods
stages.explanation: A previously healthy young adult in a crowded dormitory with fulminant fever, meningismus, and rapidly spreading petechiae and purpura plus shock is classic for Neisseria meningitidis, which appears as gram-negative diplococci (kidney-bean pairs) on Gram stain. Streptococcus pneumoniae is a gram-positive diplococcus and, while a common meningitis cause, less typically produces this fulminant purpuric septicemia in a young dorm resident. Haemophilus influenzae is a gram-negative coccobacillus, now uncommon thanks to Hib vaccination and not classically purpuric. Listeria is a gram-positive rod affecting neonates, the elderly, pregnant, and immunocompromised patients. The non-blanching petechial and purpuric rash is the cardinal clue pointing to meningococcal disease.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
WBC 21.5 ×10³/µL with bandemia
Platelets 48 ×10³/µL
INR 1.9, fibrinogen 90 mg/dL, D-dimer markedly elevated
Lactate 6.2 mmol/L
Na 130 mEq/L
Glucose 58 mg/dL
Random cortisol: inappropriately low for the degree of stress
Buffy-coat Gram stain: gram-negative diplococci; blood cultures pending

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Labs show DIC, hypoglycemia, and inappropriately low cortisol with refractory shock. What complication does this represent?
options: Thyroid storm
Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome (bilateral adrenal hemorrhage)
Tumor lysis syndrome
Hemolytic uremic syndrome
stages.explanation: Refractory shock with DIC plus evidence of acute adrenal insufficiency (hypoglycemia, hyponatremia, inappropriately low cortisol) in fulminant meningococcemia indicates Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome—bilateral adrenal hemorrhage causing acute adrenal failure. Management includes stress-dose hydrocortisone alongside antibiotics and aggressive shock resuscitation. Thyroid storm presents with hyperthermia, tachyarrhythmia, and agitation, not adrenal hemorrhage with DIC. Tumor lysis syndrome follows cytotoxic therapy of bulky malignancy with hyperkalemia, hyperuricemia, and hyperphosphatemia—not relevant here. Hemolytic uremic syndrome features microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and renal failure (often after Shiga-toxin E. coli), not adrenal hemorrhage with this septic-purpuric picture. Recognizing Waterhouse-Friderichsen and giving steroids can be lifesaving.

stages.label: PUBLIC HEALTH
stages.question: For close contacts in his dorm, what is the appropriate public-health action?
options: No prophylaxis needed; only vaccinate contacts
Treat all contacts with a full 7-day course of IV antibiotics
Quarantine the entire dormitory for 14 days
Chemoprophylaxis with rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone for close contacts
stages.explanation: Close contacts of a meningococcal case (household members, roommates, intimate or saliva contacts, and those exposed to oral secretions) should receive chemoprophylaxis—rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or a single dose of ceftriaxone—ideally within 24 hours to eradicate nasopharyngeal carriage and prevent secondary cases. Vaccinating contacts without prophylaxis provides no immediate protection, though outbreak vaccination may also be used. Full IV antibiotic courses for all contacts are unnecessary and impractical; prophylaxis is a short oral or single-dose regimen. Quarantining the whole dorm is not standard practice. Healthcare workers need prophylaxis only after intimate respiratory exposure (e.g., unprotected intubation or mouth-to-mouth). Meningococcal disease is reportable, and public health coordinates contact tracing and prophylaxis.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After he stabilizes, which underlying evaluation is appropriate given his risk for severe Neisseria infection?
options: Evaluate for terminal complement (C5–C9) deficiency and ensure meningococcal vaccination
Order a bone marrow biopsy
Begin lifelong daily penicillin without further workup
Test for cystic fibrosis
stages.explanation: Patients with severe or recurrent Neisseria infections (meningococcal or gonococcal) should be evaluated for terminal complement component deficiency (C5–C9, the membrane attack complex) and properdin deficiency, which predispose to neisserial disease; eculizumab therapy similarly raises risk. Confirming and updating meningococcal vaccination (conjugate MenACWY and MenB) is essential, particularly for a college student in congregate housing. A bone marrow biopsy is not indicated absent cytopenias suggesting marrow pathology. Lifelong daily penicillin without workup is not standard after a single episode. Cystic fibrosis testing is unrelated—CF predisposes to Pseudomonas or Staphylococcus respiratory infection, not meningococcal sepsis. Identifying complement deficiency guides vaccination and counseling to prevent recurrence.

cases.diagnosis: Meningococcal meningitis with meningococcemia (Neisseria meningitidis), complicated by septic shock and Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome
pearls: Fever plus rapidly spreading non-blanching petechiae/purpura plus shock in a young dorm resident = meningococcemia; give IV ceftriaxone within the hour.
In hemodynamically unstable or coagulopathic patients, defer LP—never delay antibiotics or resuscitation for it.
Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome = bilateral adrenal hemorrhage with refractory shock; give stress-dose steroids.
Give close contacts chemoprophylaxis (rifampin/ciprofloxacin/ceftriaxone); evaluate recurrent neisserial disease for terminal complement (C5–C9) deficiency.

cases.title: Headache, fever, and a stiff neck in summer
cases.chiefComplaint: I've had a bad headache, fever, and my neck is stiff for the last two days.
cases.history: A 24-year-old woman presents to the ED in August with 2 days of headache, low-grade fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, and mild nausea. The headache is diffuse and worse with bright light. She had a few days of sore throat, low-grade fevers, and loose stools the week prior that resolved. She is fully alert with no confusion. She denies focal weakness, numbness, seizures, or rash. PMH: none. Medications: combined oral contraceptive. Allergies: none. Social: graduate student, recently returned from a summer camp counselor job where several campers had viral illnesses; nonsmoker, occasional alcohol. No recent travel abroad, no recalled tick or mosquito-heavy exposures, no IV drug use. Sexually active with one partner, uses condoms. Immunizations up to date. Family history unremarkable. She has remained able to drink fluids and care for herself, and her main complaints are the headache and neck discomfort. No recent antibiotics. No known TB exposure.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented, and conversant young woman who appears uncomfortable but non-toxic. Mild nuchal discomfort with neck flexion; Kernig and Brudzinski signs equivocal. Photophobia present. No focal neurologic deficits; cranial nerves intact, normal strength and sensation, no papilledema. No petechial or purpuric rash; skin clear. Oropharynx mildly erythematous without exudate. Lungs clear; heart regular. Abdomen soft, nontender. She is well-hydrated with moist mucous membranes. No altered mentation throughout the encounter. No focal seizure activity. Gait normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An alert 24-year-old has fever, headache, and mild meningismus in summer. What is the best next step?
options: Immediate CT head before any further workup because of meningismus
Discharge with oral antibiotics and reassurance
Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis (no CT needed given normal mentation and no focal deficits)
MRI brain with contrast as the first test
stages.explanation: In a young, immunocompetent patient who is alert with a normal neurologic exam, no focal deficits, no papilledema, and no immunocompromise, lumbar puncture can proceed without prior CT—the indications for pre-LP CT (altered consciousness, focal deficits, seizures, papilledema, immunocompromise) are all absent. CSF analysis is essential to distinguish bacterial from viral meningitis. Routine CT first is unnecessary and only delays diagnosis when no risk features exist. Discharging on oral antibiotics without a diagnosis is inappropriate and would not even adequately treat bacterial meningitis. MRI as the first test is not the initial study for suspected meningitis. Empiric antibiotics can still be started if bacterial meningitis is a concern while awaiting CSF results.

stages.label: CSF RESULTS
stages.content:
CSF opening pressure 16 cm H2O
CSF WBC 180 /µL (85% lymphocytes)
CSF glucose 60 mg/dL (serum glucose 95 mg/dL)
CSF protein 55 mg/dL
CSF Gram stain: no organisms
Bacterial cultures pending; enterovirus PCR sent

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, mildly elevated protein, and a negative Gram stain in summer. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Bacterial meningitis
Aseptic (viral) meningitis, most likely enteroviral
Tuberculous meningitis
Subarachnoid hemorrhage
stages.explanation: A lymphocyte-predominant CSF pleocytosis with normal glucose, only mildly elevated protein, and a negative Gram stain—especially in summer or early fall with a preceding viral prodrome—is characteristic of aseptic (viral) meningitis, most commonly enteroviral. Bacterial meningitis typically shows neutrophilic pleocytosis with low CSF glucose and high protein, and patients appear sicker. Tuberculous meningitis also causes lymphocytic pleocytosis but with markedly LOW glucose, very high protein, a subacute course, and relevant exposures—not this acute summertime picture. Subarachnoid hemorrhage presents with thunderclap headache and shows xanthochromia or RBCs in CSF, not a lymphocytic pleocytosis. The normal glucose is the key discriminator pointing away from bacterial and tuberculous causes.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best management for this enteroviral meningitis?
options: Supportive care (analgesia, antiemetics, hydration); stop antibiotics once bacterial meningitis is excluded
Continue a full 14-day course of ceftriaxone regardless
Start IV acyclovir indefinitely
High-dose corticosteroids for all viral meningitis
stages.explanation: Enteroviral meningitis is self-limited and managed with supportive care—analgesics, antiemetics, fluids, and rest—with recovery typically within 7 to 10 days. If empiric antibiotics were started before CSF results, they can be stopped once bacterial meningitis is excluded (reassuring CSF profile, negative Gram stain, and negative cultures at 48 hours). A full antibiotic course is unnecessary for confirmed viral meningitis. Indefinite IV acyclovir is not indicated for enteroviral disease; acyclovir is reserved when HSV is suspected (e.g., encephalitis or until HSV PCR returns negative). Routine high-dose steroids are not standard for viral meningitis. Reassurance plus clear return precautions for worsening mental status, focal deficits, or persistent high fevers complete the plan.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which finding would prompt you to reconsider and treat empirically for bacterial or HSV disease rather than discharge?
options: Improvement of headache after analgesia and IV fluids
Normal mental status with stable vitals
Lymphocytic CSF with normal glucose
New confusion, focal neurologic deficit, or seizure
stages.explanation: New confusion, focal neurologic deficits, or seizures suggest encephalitis (e.g., HSV) or a more serious process and should prompt empiric IV acyclovir and broader workup rather than discharge, since HSV encephalitis is treatable and time-sensitive. Improvement with analgesia and fluids and a normal mental status with stable vitals are reassuring and support outpatient management of viral meningitis. A lymphocytic CSF with normal glucose is itself the expected viral profile and is reassuring, not alarming. The teaching point: meningitis (meningismus with preserved mentation) is distinguished from encephalitis (altered mentation, focal deficits, seizures), and the latter mandates empiric acyclovir while awaiting HSV PCR. Otherwise, well-appearing viral meningitis patients with reliable follow-up can often be discharged with return precautions.

cases.diagnosis: Aseptic (viral) meningitis, most likely enteroviral
pearls: Viral meningitis CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, NORMAL glucose, mildly high protein, negative Gram stain—often summer/fall enterovirus.
An alert patient with no focal deficits, papilledema, or immunocompromise can undergo LP without prior CT.
Enteroviral meningitis is self-limited—supportive care; stop empiric antibiotics once bacterial meningitis is excluded.
Altered mentation, focal deficits, or seizures point to encephalitis—start empiric acyclovir, don't just discharge.

cases.title: Fever, confusion, and a seizure
cases.chiefComplaint: He's been feverish and bizarrely confused for two days, and just had a seizure.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man admitted to the medical ward for fever and confusion is evaluated after a witnessed generalized seizure. Per his wife, he had 3 days of fever and headache, then became increasingly confused over the past 48 hours, with odd behavior, difficulty finding words, and one episode where he did not recognize her. This morning he had a seizure with twitching that began in the right arm before generalizing. He has been intermittently agitated and reports a strange smell that others do not perceive. PMH: hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications: lisinopril, atorvastatin. Allergies: none. Social: retired engineer, rare alcohol, no tobacco or drugs, no recent travel. No known immunocompromise; HIV status unknown. Family history noncontributory. There is no history of head trauma, no recent antibiotics, and no prior seizures or psychiatric disease. The wife emphasizes the personality and language changes are new and alarming. No rash has been noted. He is up to date on routine vaccinations.
cases.exam: Febrile, disoriented man (oriented to self only) who is intermittently agitated. Expressive aphasia with word-finding difficulty. Mild nuchal discomfort. Cranial nerves grossly intact; no papilledema. Subtle right upper extremity postictal weakness; reflexes symmetric. No petechial or purpuric rash. Lungs clear; heart regular and tachycardic. He has impaired short-term memory and appears to misidentify familiar objects, suggesting temporal-lobe dysfunction. No neck rigidity to the degree seen in bacterial meningitis. No focal cerebellar signs. Pupils equal and reactive. Skin without vesicular lesions.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A 60-year-old has fever, progressive confusion, aphasia, and focal seizures localizing to the temporal lobe. What is the most critical immediate action?
options: Start empiric IV acyclovir immediately, even before CSF PCR results
Wait for CSF HSV PCR to return before starting any antiviral
Start empiric IV ceftriaxone alone and observe
Order EEG and defer treatment until results
stages.explanation: Fever, altered mentation, personality and behavioral change, aphasia, and focal (temporal-lobe) seizures strongly suggest herpes simplex encephalitis—the most common sporadic fatal encephalitis. Because untreated HSV encephalitis carries very high mortality and outcomes worsen with each hour of delay, empiric IV acyclovir must be started immediately, before CSF PCR results return. Waiting for PCR is dangerous, as results take time and early treatment dramatically improves survival and neurologic outcome. Ceftriaxone alone treats bacterial meningitis but not HSV; empiric bacterial coverage may be added pending CSF but cannot replace acyclovir. EEG supports the diagnosis (e.g., temporal periodic discharges) but must never delay antiviral therapy. The mantra: in suspected HSV encephalitis, treat first and confirm later.

stages.label: IMAGING & CSF
stages.content:
MRI brain: T2/FLAIR hyperintensity and edema of the right medial temporal lobe and insula with restricted diffusion
EEG: periodic lateralized epileptiform discharges over the right temporal region
CSF opening pressure 22 cm H2O
CSF WBC 110 /µL (lymphocyte predominant) with elevated RBCs
CSF glucose 58 mg/dL
CSF protein 88 mg/dL
CSF Gram stain: no organisms
HSV-1 PCR: pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: MRI shows medial temporal lobe involvement, and CSF is lymphocytic with RBCs and normal glucose. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Bacterial meningitis
Ischemic stroke
Herpes simplex (HSV-1) encephalitis
Wernicke encephalopathy
stages.explanation: Asymmetric medial temporal lobe and insular involvement on MRI, a lymphocytic CSF pleocytosis with red blood cells (reflecting characteristic hemorrhagic necrosis) and normal glucose, plus temporal periodic discharges on EEG are classic for HSV-1 encephalitis. Bacterial meningitis shows neutrophilic CSF with low glucose and a purulent picture, without focal temporal MRI changes. Ischemic stroke follows a vascular territory and does not cause fever with progressive encephalopathy and CSF pleocytosis. Wernicke encephalopathy presents with confusion, ophthalmoplegia, and ataxia in malnourished or alcoholic patients with mammillary body changes—not temporal-lobe hemorrhagic necrosis or CSF pleocytosis. The limbic and temporal predilection explains the memory, behavior, language, and olfactory hallucination findings; CSF HSV PCR is confirmatory.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: HSV PCR is still pending and the family asks about stopping acyclovir. What is correct?
options: Stop acyclovir now since PCR has not confirmed HSV
Continue acyclovir; a single early CSF HSV PCR can be falsely negative, so don't stop prematurely
Switch to oral valacyclovir immediately for convenience
Add corticosteroids and discontinue antiviral therapy
stages.explanation: Continue IV acyclovir. CSF HSV PCR is highly sensitive and specific but can be falsely negative very early (within roughly the first 72 hours of symptoms); treatment should continue empirically when clinical and imaging suspicion is high, and a repeat PCR can be done in 3 to 7 days if the first is negative. Stopping acyclovir based on a single early negative or a pending PCR risks a fatal relapse. Switching to oral valacyclovir is inappropriate for acute HSV encephalitis, which requires a full IV acyclovir course (typically 14 to 21 days). Discontinuing antivirals to give steroids alone is wrong; steroids are adjunctive at best and never replace acyclovir. Maintain hydration and monitor renal function on high-dose IV acyclovir.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which is the most important monitoring or supportive measure while he is on high-dose IV acyclovir?
options: Restrict all IV fluids to prevent cerebral edema
Start prophylactic broad-spectrum antifungals
Begin therapeutic anticoagulation
Maintain adequate IV hydration and monitor renal function to prevent acyclovir crystal nephropathy
stages.explanation: High-dose IV acyclovir can crystallize in renal tubules and cause obstructive crystal nephropathy and acute kidney injury, so adequate IV hydration and monitoring of renal function (with dose adjustment for renal impairment and slow infusion) are essential. Strict fluid restriction is incorrect and would worsen acyclovir nephrotoxicity; fluid management should balance cerebral edema concerns against the need for hydration. Prophylactic broad-spectrum antifungals are not indicated in HSV encephalitis. Therapeutic anticoagulation is not part of management and could worsen the hemorrhagic necrosis characteristic of HSV encephalitis. Additional supportive care includes seizure control with antiepileptics, neurologic monitoring, and managing raised intracranial pressure. Early acyclovir plus good supportive care optimizes survival, though memory and behavioral sequelae are common.

cases.diagnosis: Herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1) encephalitis
pearls: Fever plus acute personality/behavior change, aphasia, and temporal-lobe seizures = HSV encephalitis until proven otherwise.
Start empiric IV acyclovir immediately—never wait for CSF PCR; delays increase mortality.
MRI shows medial temporal/insular involvement; CSF is lymphocytic with RBCs; EEG may show temporal periodic discharges.
Early CSF HSV PCR can be falsely negative—continue acyclovir and repeat PCR; hydrate to prevent acyclovir crystal nephropathy.

cases.title: Weeks of worsening headache and fevers
cases.chiefComplaint: My head has been pounding for two weeks and now I'm nauseated and can't think straight.
cases.history: A 40-year-old man is admitted with 2 weeks of progressively worsening headache, low-grade fevers, malaise, and nausea, with intermittent vomiting over the past few days. The headache is constant, worse when lying flat, and associated with photophobia and, more recently, mild blurring of vision and difficulty concentrating. He was diagnosed with HIV years ago but stopped antiretroviral therapy and missed appointments; his last CD4 count was low. He reports a 10-pound weight loss and night sweats. He denies seizures, focal weakness, or neck trauma. PMH: HIV/AIDS, prior oral candidiasis. Medications: none currently (nonadherent to ART and prophylaxis). Allergies: none. Social: lives alone, former smoker, occasional alcohol, no current drug use; previously kept pet pigeons. Family history noncontributory. He has had no recent travel. He is concerned that his symptoms have steadily worsened despite over-the-counter analgesics. No prior episodes of meningitis. No recent antibiotics or hospitalizations.
cases.exam: Chronically ill-appearing, thin man who is alert but slowed in responses. Mild nuchal discomfort; meningismus is subtle rather than dramatic. Fundoscopic exam shows mild papilledema. Cranial nerves intact aside from subjective blurry vision; no focal motor or sensory deficits. Oropharynx with white plaques consistent with thrush. No petechial rash. Lungs clear; heart regular. No lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Photophobia present. Gait cautious due to headache. No focal cerebellar findings. Skin without umbilicated papules. He is cachectic with temporal wasting.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A 40-year-old man with AIDS has subacute headache, fever, and papilledema. What is the best diagnostic approach?
options: Obtain CT head first (given papilledema/immunocompromise), then LP with measurement of opening pressure and CSF studies including cryptococcal antigen
Perform LP immediately without imaging despite papilledema
Give empiric antibiotics and discharge with outpatient MRI
Treat for migraine and observe
stages.explanation: In an immunocompromised patient (AIDS) with papilledema and subacute meningitis, neuroimaging (CT) should precede LP to exclude a mass lesion (e.g., toxoplasmosis, lymphoma) that could raise herniation risk; then LP is performed with careful measurement of opening pressure and CSF studies, including cryptococcal antigen (CrAg), India ink, cell count, glucose, protein, and culture. Performing LP immediately despite papilledema and immunocompromise skips an important safety step. Empiric antibiotics with discharge is unsafe and fails to diagnose the likely opportunistic infection. Treating as migraine ignores the fever, papilledema, and AIDS context. The pigeon exposure and low CD4 raise suspicion for Cryptococcus neoformans, where measuring and managing elevated opening pressure is central to care.

stages.label: CT, CSF & LABS
stages.content:
CT head: no mass lesion or hydrocephalus
CSF opening pressure: 38 cm H2O (markedly elevated)
CSF WBC 25 /µL (lymphocyte predominant)
CSF glucose 38 mg/dL (mildly low)
CSF protein 70 mg/dL
India ink: encapsulated yeast with narrow-based budding
Serum and CSF cryptococcal antigen (CrAg): positive, high titer
CD4 count: 28 cells/µL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: India ink shows encapsulated yeast and CrAg is positive. What is the diagnosis and organism?
options: Tuberculous meningitis
Toxoplasma encephalitis
Pneumocystis pneumonia
Cryptococcal meningitis (Cryptococcus neoformans)
stages.explanation: An AIDS patient (CD4 28) with subacute meningitis, markedly elevated opening pressure, a relatively bland lymphocytic CSF, India ink showing encapsulated narrow-based budding yeast, and positive serum/CSF cryptococcal antigen has cryptococcal meningitis due to Cryptococcus neoformans (pigeon-dropping exposure is a classic clue). Tuberculous meningitis also causes lymphocytic CSF with low glucose but very high protein and a basilar picture, and lacks encapsulated yeast or positive CrAg. Toxoplasma encephalitis presents with ring-enhancing brain lesions and focal deficits, not encapsulated yeast in CSF. Pneumocystis is a pulmonary opportunistic infection (hypoxemia, diffuse infiltrates), not meningitis. CrAg is highly sensitive and specific, and CSF fungal culture confirms the diagnosis; the strikingly high opening pressure is a hallmark and major driver of morbidity.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best induction antifungal therapy?
options: Fluconazole monotherapy
Liposomal amphotericin B plus flucytosine (5-FC)
Voriconazole alone
Caspofungin
stages.explanation: Induction therapy for cryptococcal meningitis is liposomal amphotericin B PLUS flucytosine (5-FC) for at least 2 weeks, followed by consolidation with high-dose fluconazole and then maintenance fluconazole. This combination clears the organism faster and improves survival versus monotherapy. Fluconazole monotherapy is inadequate for induction and is reserved for consolidation or maintenance or for resource-limited settings when amphotericin is unavailable. Voriconazole is not standard first-line for cryptococcal meningitis. Caspofungin and other echinocandins have essentially NO activity against Cryptococcus and must not be used. Antiretroviral therapy should be started but typically delayed about 4 to 6 weeks after antifungal initiation to reduce the risk of immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS). Flucytosine requires monitoring for cytopenias.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: With an opening pressure of 38 cm H2O plus headache and visual changes, what is the most important intervention to reduce morbidity?
options: High-dose corticosteroids to lower the pressure
Acetazolamide as primary therapy and discharge
Therapeutic serial lumbar punctures (or CSF drainage) to lower elevated intracranial pressure
A single mannitol bolus alone with no further LPs
stages.explanation: Management of elevated intracranial pressure is critical in cryptococcal meningitis because high opening pressure drives mortality, headache, and vision and hearing loss. The cornerstone is therapeutic serial lumbar punctures to drain CSF and reduce pressure (repeated, often daily, until stable); a lumbar drain or shunt may be needed for refractory cases. Corticosteroids are NOT recommended for routine ICP control in HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis and have been linked to worse outcomes (they are used for IRIS or TB meningitis, not here). Acetazolamide as primary therapy with discharge is ineffective and inappropriate. A single mannitol bolus without ongoing CSF drainage does not address the recurrent pressure elevation. Repeated LPs, antifungals, and eventual ART are the pillars of care.

cases.diagnosis: Cryptococcal meningitis (Cryptococcus neoformans) in AIDS
pearls: Subacute headache plus fever plus very high CSF opening pressure in AIDS (CD4 under 100) = cryptococcal meningitis; send serum/CSF CrAg and India ink.
Induction = liposomal amphotericin B + flucytosine; echinocandins have NO activity against Cryptococcus.
Manage elevated opening pressure with serial therapeutic LPs—this reduces morbidity and mortality; steroids are not used for routine ICP control.
Delay ART about 4 to 6 weeks after starting antifungals to lower IRIS risk; image before LP when papilledema or immunocompromise raises herniation concern.

cases.title: Worsening headache, fever, and arm clumsiness
cases.chiefComplaint: I've had a pounding headache for over a week and now I can't think straight.
cases.history: A 48-year-old man is admitted with 10 days of progressively worsening, dull right-sided headache, subjective fevers, and two days of nausea with one episode of vomiting. Over the past 48 hours his family noticed mild left-arm clumsiness and intermittent inattention. He has a long history of chronic right-sided otitis media with recurrent foul-smelling ear drainage, most recently flaring three weeks ago, for which he took an incomplete course of oral antibiotics. He denies seizures, neck stiffness, photophobia, head trauma, or recent travel. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. He takes no regular medications and has no known drug allergies. Social history: works as an accountant, drinks alcohol socially, does not smoke, and has never used injection drugs. He is in a monogamous relationship. Review of systems is positive for decreased appetite and poor sleep from the headache. Family history is noncontributory. He has had no recent dental procedures and no prior neurosurgery.
cases.exam: The patient is alert but slightly inattentive and slow to answer. Funduscopy reveals bilateral papilledema. There is a subtle left pronator drift with 4/5 strength in the left upper extremity; the right side is normal. Cranial nerves are otherwise intact and there is no facial droop. Neck is supple without meningismus. The right external auditory canal contains purulent discharge with a perforated, dull tympanic membrane, and there is tenderness over the right mastoid. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are normal. No skin rash, splinter hemorrhages, or peripheral stigmata of endocarditis are seen. Gait is mildly unsteady.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: This patient has subacute headache, fever, a focal neurologic deficit, and papilledema. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Perform an immediate lumbar puncture to evaluate for meningitis
Start empiric oral antibiotics and arrange outpatient follow-up
Obtain contrast-enhanced neuroimaging (CT or MRI) before any lumbar puncture
Order an EEG to evaluate for seizure activity
stages.explanation: A focal neurologic deficit plus papilledema indicates a space-occupying process with raised intracranial pressure, so neuroimaging must precede any lumbar puncture to avoid precipitating cerebral herniation. Imaging also rapidly identifies a mass such as an abscess. An immediate LP is dangerous in this setting and could be fatal. Oral antibiotics with outpatient follow-up underestimate a potentially life-threatening intracranial infection in an unstable, focally deficient patient. An EEG evaluates seizures but does not explain the headache, fever, or focal weakness and would delay definitive diagnosis. Contrast imaging is both the safest and the highest-yield next move here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 14.5 ×10³/µL (82% neutrophils)
CRP 96 mg/L
ESR 64 mm/hr
Blood cultures: pending
MRI brain with contrast: 2.4 cm ring-enhancing lesion in the right temporal lobe with surrounding vasogenic edema and 4 mm midline shift
DWI: central restricted diffusion within the lesion
Additional finding: opacification of the right mastoid air cells and middle ear

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The MRI shows a ring-enhancing lesion with surrounding edema and central restricted diffusion on DWI, adjacent to an opacified mastoid. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Glioblastoma multiforme
Cerebral metastasis
Pyogenic brain abscess
Cerebral toxoplasmosis
stages.explanation: Central restricted diffusion on DWI reflects the thick, viscous pus inside a pyogenic abscess and, combined with a contiguous mastoid/middle-ear infection, strongly favors brain abscess. Glioblastoma and metastasis can both ring-enhance, but their necrotic centers usually facilitate diffusion (no central restriction) and they lack an adjacent infectious source. Toxoplasmosis typically occurs in advanced HIV, favors the basal ganglia, often shows multiple lesions, and does not characteristically restrict diffusion. The clinical context of chronic otitis/mastoiditis with fever and elevated inflammatory markers seals the diagnosis of an otogenic brain abscess.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best management approach for this lesion?
options: Stereotactic aspiration plus empiric IV vancomycin, metronidazole, and ceftriaxone
High-dose corticosteroids alone
Oral metronidazole as an outpatient
Whole-brain radiation therapy
stages.explanation: Optimal care combines neurosurgical aspiration—which is diagnostic (Gram stain, culture) and therapeutic (decompression)—with prolonged broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering streptococci, anaerobes, and gram-negative rods typical of otogenic abscesses (vancomycin plus metronidazole plus a third-generation cephalosporin). Corticosteroids are only an adjunct to control significant edema or mass effect and do not treat the infection. Oral outpatient therapy cannot adequately treat an intracranial abscess with midline shift and is unsafe. Radiation treats tumors, not infection, and would be harmful here. Source identification by aspiration also tailors definitive antibiotic selection.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Aspiration confirms polymicrobial growth including anaerobes. What additional intervention best addresses the source of infection?
options: Begin lifelong suppressive oral antibiotics
Refer to ENT for mastoidectomy/surgical management of the otogenic focus
Initiate hyperbaric oxygen therapy
Repeat lumbar puncture in 48 hours
stages.explanation: An otogenic abscess arises from a contiguous infected focus, so eradicating that source with ENT surgery (mastoidectomy and middle-ear debridement) is essential to achieve cure and prevent relapse once the abscess is drained and antibiotics are running. Lifelong suppressive antibiotics are not standard and do not remove the nidus. Hyperbaric oxygen is at best an unproven adjunct and not source control. A repeat lumbar puncture remains hazardous given the mass effect and provides no therapeutic benefit. Definitive treatment requires both the intracranial drainage and surgical control of the primary ear infection.

cases.diagnosis: Otogenic pyogenic brain abscess (right temporal lobe) secondary to chronic otitis media/mastoiditis
pearls: Central restricted diffusion on DWI distinguishes a pyogenic abscess from a necrotic tumor, which usually does not restrict centrally.
Otogenic and sinogenic abscesses are frequently polymicrobial—cover streptococci, anaerobes, and gram-negative rods (ceftriaxone + metronidazole ± vancomycin).
Never perform LP first when there is a focal deficit or papilledema—image first to avoid herniation.
Cure requires both abscess drainage and surgical source control of the contiguous focus (mastoidectomy or sinus surgery).

cases.title: Years of stabbing leg pains and an unsteady gait
cases.chiefComplaint: My legs feel like lightning is shooting through them and I lose my balance, especially in the dark.
cases.history: A 52-year-old man presents to clinic with about three years of intermittent, brief, severe shooting ("lightning") pains in both legs that come without warning. Over the same period he has developed progressive unsteadiness when walking, markedly worse in the dark or when his eyes are closed, and he has fallen twice. He also reports urinary hesitancy with occasional incontinence and several years of erectile dysfunction. He recalls a single painless genital ulcer roughly 18 years ago that healed on its own; he never sought treatment. He has been sexually active with both men and women, with multiple lifetime partners and inconsistent condom use, and has never been tested for HIV. He has no diabetes. Social history: drinks alcohol moderately, does not smoke, and denies injection drug use. He takes no regular medications and has no known drug allergies. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable. Family history is noncontributory. He works as a delivery driver and the imbalance now threatens his job.
cases.exam: He is well-appearing and afebrile. Pupils are small and irregular and constrict briskly to accommodation but do not react to light (Argyll Robertson pupils) bilaterally. Visual acuity is preserved. Motor strength and tone are normal with no spasticity. Deep tendon reflexes are absent at the ankles and knees. There is marked loss of vibration and proprioception in both lower extremities, with reduced pinprick distally. Romberg sign is strongly positive, and his gait is broad-based, high-stepping, and "stomping." Cranial nerves are otherwise intact, and there is no meningismus. Mental status is normal, and there is no facial weakness or dysarthria.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given lightning leg pains, sensory ataxia, and abnormal pupils, what is the best initial diagnostic step?
options: MRI of the lumbar spine
Nerve conduction studies
Vitamin B12 level only
Serum nontreponemal (RPR) and treponemal (FTA-ABS) testing, plus HIV testing
stages.explanation: The constellation of lightning pains, posterior-column sensory ataxia, and pupils that accommodate but do not react to light is classic for late neurosyphilis (tabes dorsalis), so serologic screening with a nontreponemal test (RPR) confirmed by a treponemal test (FTA-ABS), plus HIV testing given shared risk, is the highest-yield first step. Vitamin B12 deficiency causing subacute combined degeneration is a reasonable differential but checking B12 alone would miss the most likely diagnosis. Nerve conduction studies assess peripheral neuropathy and would not explain the pupillary findings. MRI of the spine may follow but does not establish the etiology.

stages.label: SEROLOGY
stages.content:
Serum RPR: reactive, titer 1:32
FTA-ABS: reactive
HIV 4th-generation Ag/Ab: negative
Vitamin B12: 410 pg/mL (normal)
Fasting glucose and HbA1c: normal
CBC and comprehensive metabolic panel: unremarkable

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Serum testing confirms syphilis and he has clear neurologic signs. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including CSF-VDRL
Treat with a single dose of IM benzathine penicillin
Begin oral doxycycline for 14 days
Repeat serum RPR in 6 months
stages.explanation: When syphilis is confirmed and neurologic signs are present, a lumbar puncture is required to evaluate for neurosyphilis (CSF-VDRL, cell count, protein), because confirming CNS involvement mandates intravenous rather than intramuscular therapy. A single IM benzathine penicillin dose treats early syphilis but is inadequate for neurosyphilis because it does not achieve treponemicidal CSF levels. Oral doxycycline is a non-preferred alternative for early syphilis and is not appropriate for documented neurologic disease. Simply repeating serum RPR delays definitive diagnosis and risks irreversible neurologic progression. The CSF result directly determines the treatment regimen.

stages.label: CSF ANALYSIS
stages.content:
CSF appearance: clear and colorless
CSF WBC: 22 cells/µL (lymphocyte predominant)
CSF protein: 68 mg/dL (elevated)
CSF glucose: 58 mg/dL (normal)
CSF-VDRL: reactive

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits the clinical picture and CSF findings?
options: Subacute combined degeneration from B12 deficiency
Diabetic peripheral neuropathy
Tabes dorsalis (late neurosyphilis)
Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis
stages.explanation: Argyll Robertson pupils, dorsal-column degeneration (sensory ataxia, lost ankle/knee reflexes, positive Romberg), lightning pains, and a reactive CSF-VDRL with lymphocytic pleocytosis confirm tabes dorsalis, a late form of neurosyphilis. Subacute combined degeneration is excluded by a normal B12 and would not cause Argyll Robertson pupils or a reactive CSF-VDRL. Diabetic neuropathy is unlikely with normal glucose and does not explain the pupils or CSF findings. ALS is a motor neuron disease with combined upper and lower motor neuron signs but no sensory loss and no pupillary abnormality, so it does not fit.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best treatment for this patient?
options: IM benzathine penicillin G 2.4 million units weekly for 3 weeks
Aqueous crystalline penicillin G IV 18-24 million units/day for 10-14 days
Oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 28 days
Ceftriaxone 250 mg IM once
stages.explanation: Neurosyphilis requires intravenous aqueous crystalline penicillin G 18-24 million units per day for 10-14 days because only IV dosing reliably achieves treponemicidal concentrations in the CNS. Weekly IM benzathine penicillin is the regimen for late latent syphilis without neurologic involvement and does not adequately penetrate the CSF. Oral doxycycline is reserved for penicillin-allergic patients with early disease and is suboptimal for CNS infection. A single dose of IM ceftriaxone is insufficient for neurosyphilis. If the patient were truly penicillin-allergic, the preferred approach would be penicillin desensitization rather than a substitute agent.

cases.diagnosis: Tabes dorsalis (late neurosyphilis) with Argyll Robertson pupils and sensory ataxia
pearls: Argyll Robertson pupils "accommodate but do not react" and are a hallmark of neurosyphilis.
Tabes dorsalis is posterior-column degeneration: lightning pains, sensory ataxia, positive Romberg, and lost lower-extremity reflexes.
Neurosyphilis requires IV penicillin G, not IM benzathine—and always test for HIV.
A reactive CSF-VDRL is highly specific but not sensitive; a negative result does not exclude neurosyphilis.

cases.title: Jaw stiffness and violent body spasms after a farm wound
cases.chiefComplaint: I can't open my mouth and my whole body keeps locking up.
cases.history: A 60-year-old farmer is brought to the emergency department because of jaw stiffness and recurrent painful body spasms. Eight days ago, while working in a livestock field, he stepped on a soil-contaminated nail that punctured the sole of his right foot through his boot; he washed it at home and did not seek care. Over the past two days he has developed progressive difficulty opening his mouth ("lockjaw"), painful neck and back stiffness, difficulty swallowing, and episodes of generalized, intensely painful muscle spasms that are triggered by noise, light, or touch. During spasms his back arches. He denies headache, recent illness, or new medications that could cause a dystonic reaction. He has never received a tetanus vaccine and recalls no booster shots in his adult life. Social history: lives and works rurally with cattle and manure, drinks alcohol occasionally, does not use drugs. He takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. Past medical and family histories are unremarkable.
cases.exam: He is fully awake, alert, and oriented, with completely preserved consciousness even during spasms. There is severe trismus limiting mouth opening, a sustained fixed grimace (risus sardonicus), and nuchal rigidity. The abdomen is rigid and board-like. Intermittent stimulus-provoked generalized spasms produce opisthotonic posturing (arched back). He is diffusely hyperreflexic and profoundly diaphoretic, with labile blood pressure and tachycardia. On the right sole there is a deep puncture wound with surrounding necrotic tissue and scant discharge. There are no focal motor or sensory deficits, no photophobia from meningeal irritation, and no rash.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Bacterial meningitis
Generalized tetanus
Acute dystonic reaction
Strychnine poisoning
stages.explanation: Trismus, risus sardonicus, opisthotonos, stimulus-triggered painful spasms, and autonomic instability with fully preserved consciousness following a contaminated puncture wound in an unvaccinated patient are classic for generalized tetanus, which is a clinical diagnosis. Bacterial meningitis produces fever and altered mental status with meningismus but not risus sardonicus or the characteristic spasm pattern. An acute dystonic reaction follows dopamine-blocking drug exposure and responds to anticholinergics. Strychnine poisoning can mimic tetanus with generalized spasms but lacks the wound and incubation history and is confirmed by toxicology. The wound history, incubation period, and immunization status make tetanus the clear diagnosis.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
Lumbar puncture (to exclude CNS infection): opening pressure normal; WBC 2/µL; protein 32 mg/dL; glucose 64 mg/dL — normal
CBC, electrolytes, creatinine: within normal limits
Serum and urine toxicology screen: negative
Spatula test: gentle touch of the posterior pharynx provokes reflex masseter spasm (the patient bites down) rather than a gag — positive

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which intervention most directly neutralizes circulating tetanus toxin?
options: Human tetanus immune globulin (TIG)
High-dose IV corticosteroids
IV magnesium sulfate
Oral vancomycin
stages.explanation: Human tetanus immune globulin neutralizes unbound (circulating) toxin before it is internalized by neurons; it cannot reverse toxin already bound at the synapse, so it must be given promptly. Definitive care also includes wound debridement and metronidazole (the preferred antibiotic) to halt further toxin production. IV magnesium sulfate helps control spasms and autonomic instability but does not neutralize toxin. Corticosteroids have no role in toxin neutralization. Oral vancomycin is not the antibiotic of choice for tetanus; metronidazole (or penicillin) is preferred. Passive immunization with TIG is therefore the key toxin-directed therapy.

stages.label: SUPPORTIVE CARE
stages.question: What is the best strategy to control this patient's muscle spasms and rigidity?
options: Intermittent succinylcholine boluses as needed
Haloperidol
Phenytoin
Benzodiazepines (e.g., IV diazepam or midazolam) in a quiet, low-stimulation environment
stages.explanation: Benzodiazepines are first-line for the spasms and rigidity of tetanus because of their GABAergic, muscle-relaxant, and sedating effects, and they should be paired with a dark, quiet environment to minimize triggering stimuli; severe cases require intubation with neuromuscular blockade and ventilation, plus magnesium for autonomic instability. Intermittent succinylcholine is a paralytic for intubation, not a spasm-control strategy. Haloperidol is a dopamine blocker that can itself cause dystonia and does not treat tetanic spasms. Phenytoin is an anticonvulsant ineffective against the non-seizure muscular spasms of tetanus. Benzodiazepines plus sensory minimization are the cornerstone of supportive control.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Beyond ICU admission, what must be ensured for this patient during recovery?
options: Nothing further—surviving tetanus confers lifelong immunity
Active immunization with a tetanus toxoid–containing vaccine, because natural infection does NOT produce protective immunity
Lifelong daily oral metronidazole
Annual tetanus immune globulin injections
stages.explanation: The amount of toxin that causes clinical tetanus is too small to induce a protective antibody response, so natural infection does not confer immunity and every survivor needs a full tetanus toxoid vaccination series begun during recovery. ICU admission is needed for airway protection and autonomic monitoring, as autonomic instability drives mortality. Lifelong oral metronidazole is unnecessary once the wound is treated and the acute illness resolves. Annual tetanus immune globulin is not a maintenance strategy; active immunization with toxoid, not passive immunoglobulin, provides durable protection. Vaccination at recovery is a frequently missed but essential step.

cases.diagnosis: Generalized tetanus from a contaminated puncture wound in an unvaccinated patient
pearls: Tetanus is a clinical diagnosis; preserved consciousness with trismus, risus sardonicus, and opisthotonos is classic.
Give human TIG to neutralize unbound toxin, debride the wound, and use metronidazole; benzodiazepines control spasms.
Natural tetanus does not confer immunity—always begin active toxoid vaccination during recovery.
Autonomic instability (labile BP, tachycardia, diaphoresis) drives mortality; magnesium and ICU support are key.

cases.title: A floppy, constipated baby who stopped feeding well
cases.chiefComplaint: My baby has gone limp, won't suck during feeds, and hasn't had a bowel movement in days.
cases.history: A 4-month-old boy, previously healthy and developing normally, is brought in for progressive weakness. He had several days of constipation followed by two to three days of poor feeding, a weak and feeble cry, decreased spontaneous movement, and worsening floppiness with poor head control. His parents say he seems sleepy and tires quickly during feeds. He has been afebrile throughout. They recently began dipping his pacifier in honey to soothe him. He was born full-term by uncomplicated vaginal delivery, is up to date on immunizations for age, and has had no sick contacts. The family lives in a rural area; there has been no recent travel and no trauma. He takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. There is no family history of neuromuscular disease. The parents deny choking episodes, fever, vomiting, or rash, but note he is much less active than his baseline.
cases.exam: The infant is afebrile and alert-appearing but strikingly hypotonic ("floppy"), with marked head lag and poor truncal tone when pulled to sit. He has a weak suck and a diminished gag, bilateral ptosis, and pupils that are somewhat dilated and sluggishly reactive. Deep tendon reflexes are diminished, and weakness is symmetric and descending (face and bulbar muscles before limbs). Mucous membranes are dry, bowel sounds are decreased, and the abdomen is mildly distended. There is no fever, no rash, and the anterior fontanelle is soft and flat without bulging. Respirations are currently adequate but shallow.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains a previously well, afebrile, constipated infant with bilateral ptosis, weak suck, dilated sluggish pupils, and symmetric descending hypotonia?
options: Spinal muscular atrophy type 1
Guillain-Barré syndrome
Infant botulism
Neonatal sepsis
stages.explanation: Symmetric descending weakness with bulbar involvement (ptosis, weak suck and cry) plus autonomic signs (dilated sluggish pupils, constipation) in an afebrile infant after honey exposure is classic for infant botulism, in which ingested Clostridium botulinum spores colonize the gut and produce toxin. Spinal muscular atrophy type 1 causes progressive weakness with tongue fasciculations but not acute constipation, pupillary changes, or a honey link. Guillain-Barré classically causes ascending weakness with areflexia and is rare in this age group. Neonatal sepsis typically produces fever, an ill toxic appearance, and instability rather than a pure, symmetric descending paralysis with preserved alertness.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
Stool sample: sent for C. botulinum toxin assay and culture — subsequently positive for botulinum toxin type B
Electromyography: brief, small-amplitude motor unit potentials with an incremental response to rapid repetitive nerve stimulation
CSF analysis: normal protein and normal cell count
CBC, electrolytes, and full sepsis workup: unremarkable; patient remains afebrile

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best treatment for this infant?
options: Equine botulism antitoxin (heptavalent)
IV ceftriaxone and gentamicin
Plasmapheresis
Human-derived botulism immune globulin (BabyBIG)
stages.explanation: Infant botulism is treated with human-derived botulism immune globulin (BabyBIG), which neutralizes circulating toxin, shortens hospital stay, and reduces complications; supportive care with close respiratory monitoring is essential. Equine (heptavalent) antitoxin is used for wound and foodborne botulism in older children and adults but is avoided in infants because of the risk of anaphylaxis and serum sickness. Antibiotics are not indicated for the intestinal colonization, and aminoglycosides specifically can worsen neuromuscular blockade. Plasmapheresis is a therapy for Guillain-Barré syndrome, not botulism. BabyBIG is the disease-specific, evidence-based treatment for infant botulism.

stages.label: PITFALL
stages.question: Which medication should specifically be AVOIDED because it can potentiate the neuromuscular blockade?
options: Acetaminophen
Aminoglycoside antibiotics (e.g., gentamicin)
Intravenous fluids
BabyBIG
stages.explanation: Aminoglycosides impair presynaptic acetylcholine release and can potentiate the toxin-mediated blockade, worsening paralysis and potentially precipitating respiratory failure, so they must be avoided; antibiotics in general are not used to treat the gut colonization. Acetaminophen is a safe antipyretic/analgesic with no effect on neuromuscular transmission. Intravenous fluids are appropriate supportive care for an infant with poor feeding. BabyBIG is the treatment itself and is beneficial, not harmful. Recognizing the danger of aminoglycosides is a high-yield safety point in managing infant botulism.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most important supportive priority during this admission?
options: Close monitoring of respiratory status with readiness to intubate
Early introduction of solid foods
Scheduled laxatives only
Strict isolation precautions to prevent person-to-person spread
stages.explanation: The leading cause of morbidity and mortality in infant botulism is respiratory failure from diaphragmatic and bulbar muscle weakness, so the priority is close respiratory monitoring with prompt intubation and ventilatory support if needed, along with nutritional support (often nasogastric feeding given the unsafe suck). Introducing solids is inappropriate and unsafe in a baby with bulbar weakness. Scheduled laxatives alone do not address the paralysis or the airway risk. Infant botulism is not transmitted person-to-person, so isolation precautions are unnecessary. Anticipating and managing respiratory compromise is the cornerstone of supportive care.

cases.diagnosis: Infant botulism (intestinal colonization with Clostridium botulinum) following honey exposure
pearls: Classic infant botulism: an afebrile "floppy baby" with constipation first, then descending bulbar weakness and dilated, sluggish pupils.
Avoid honey before age 1 year; treat confirmed infant botulism with human BabyBIG, not equine antitoxin.
Avoid aminoglycosides and antibiotics—they can worsen the paralysis; care is otherwise supportive.
Respiratory failure is the main threat—monitor the airway closely and intubate early when needed.

cases.title: A month of fatigue, low fevers, and night sweats
cases.chiefComplaint: I've felt completely wiped out with low fevers, chills, and night sweats for almost five weeks.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man is admitted with five weeks of progressive malaise, intermittent low-grade fevers, drenching night sweats, poor appetite, and 4 kg of unintentional weight loss. He underwent a dental extraction about seven weeks ago without antibiotic prophylaxis. He has a long-standing history of mitral valve prolapse with mild mitral regurgitation noted on prior echocardiography. He denies injection drug use, recent hospitalization, indwelling catheters, or focal symptoms such as cough or dysuria. Review of systems is notable for new mild exertional dyspnea. Social history: retired teacher, drinks alcohol occasionally, never smoked, in a monogamous relationship. He takes no regular medications and has no known drug allergies. He has not traveled recently. Family history is noncontributory. He initially attributed his symptoms to a lingering viral illness, but the persistence of fevers and new breathlessness prompted his primary physician to send him in for admission and evaluation.
cases.exam: He appears chronically ill and pale. Cardiac exam reveals a new grade III/VI holosystolic murmur at the apex radiating to the axilla, louder than documented on prior records. Peripheral findings include splinter hemorrhages under two fingernails, a few nontender erythematous macules on the palms (Janeway lesions), and tender violaceous nodules on the finger pads (Osler nodes). Funduscopy shows a Roth spot. There is mild splenomegaly. Lungs have faint bibasilar crackles. There are no needle track marks and no meningismus. The remainder of the exam, including the abdomen and neurologic exam, is otherwise unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: This stable patient has a subacute febrile illness with a new murmur and embolic stigmata. What is the best initial diagnostic step?
options: Start empiric vancomycin immediately, then draw a single blood culture
Order a single set of blood cultures and a chest CT
Begin oral antibiotics and arrange outpatient cardiology follow-up
Obtain three sets of blood cultures from separate sites before antibiotics, plus transthoracic echocardiography
stages.explanation: In a hemodynamically stable patient with suspected subacute endocarditis, the priority is to obtain at least three sets of blood cultures from separate venipuncture sites over time before starting antibiotics, plus a transthoracic echocardiogram to look for vegetations. Persistently positive cultures of a typical organism are a major Duke criterion, and premature antibiotics can sterilize cultures and obscure the diagnosis. A single blood culture undermines the microbiologic criterion. Oral antibiotics with outpatient follow-up are unsafe for endocarditis. Empiric antibiotics before cultures are reserved for unstable or septic patients, which this patient is not.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Blood cultures: 3 of 3 sets positive for viridans group streptococci (Streptococcus sanguinis)
WBC 11.8 ×10³/µL
ESR 72 mm/hr; CRP 58 mg/L
Urinalysis: microscopic hematuria
TTE: 1.1 cm mobile vegetation on the anterior mitral leaflet with moderate mitral regurgitation

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Using the modified Duke criteria, how is this case best classified?
options: Possible infective endocarditis (1 major + 1 minor)
