Rejected—findings explained by another diagnosis
Definite infective endocarditis (2 major criteria)
Definite IE based on pathologic criteria from a surgical specimen
stages.explanation: Two major criteria are satisfied—a typical organism (viridans streptococci) grown from persistently positive blood cultures, and echocardiographic evidence of a vegetation—establishing clinically definite infective endocarditis. Several minor criteria (fever, a predisposing valvular lesion, and vascular/immunologic phenomena such as Janeway lesions, Osler nodes, and Roth spots) further support it. It is more than "possible," which would require fewer criteria. It is not rejected, since there is no firm alternative diagnosis. Pathologic criteria require histologic or microbiologic examination of a surgical or autopsy specimen, which was not obtained, so the diagnosis is clinical.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best definitive antibiotic regimen for penicillin-susceptible viridans streptococcal native-valve endocarditis?
options: IV penicillin G or ceftriaxone for 4 weeks (gentamicin may shorten to 2 weeks in selected cases)
Oral amoxicillin for 7 days
IV vancomycin for 1 week
Oral levofloxacin for 2 weeks
stages.explanation: Penicillin-susceptible viridans streptococcal native-valve endocarditis is treated with intravenous penicillin G or ceftriaxone for 4 weeks; adding gentamicin can shorten therapy to 2 weeks in selected uncomplicated cases. Short oral courses cannot eradicate organisms embedded in a vegetation's avascular fibrin matrix, so a 7-day oral course is inadequate. Vancomycin is reserved for penicillin-allergic patients or resistant organisms and still requires a prolonged course rather than one week. Fluoroquinolones such as levofloxacin are not standard therapy for streptococcal endocarditis. Prolonged, bactericidal, intravenous therapy is essential for cure.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If the initial TTE had been non-diagnostic but clinical suspicion remained high, what is the best next step?
options: Repeat blood cultures only and observe
Obtain a transesophageal echocardiogram (TEE)
Proceed directly to valve surgery
Stop antibiotics to avoid masking findings
stages.explanation: Transesophageal echocardiography is substantially more sensitive than transthoracic imaging for detecting vegetations, valve perforation, and perivalvular abscess, and it is the appropriate next step when the TTE is non-diagnostic but suspicion remains high. Repeating blood cultures alone does not visualize the valve. Surgery is reserved for specific indications such as heart failure, perivalvular abscess, persistent or refractory infection, or large mobile vegetations, and is not a first-line diagnostic maneuver. Stopping antibiotics in a patient with documented bacteremia is dangerous and could allow progression. TEE provides the needed anatomic confirmation while treatment continues.

cases.diagnosis: Subacute native-valve infective endocarditis due to viridans group streptococci (Streptococcus sanguinis) after dental work
pearls: Subacute viridans streptococcal endocarditis follows dental/oral procedures and seeds abnormal native valves.
Two major Duke criteria (typical organism in persistent cultures + a vegetation) make IE clinically definite.
Draw at least three blood culture sets before antibiotics in stable subacute presentations.
TEE outperforms TTE when suspicion is high but the transthoracic study is non-diagnostic.

cases.title: Fever, cough, and pleuritic chest pain in a young man
cases.chiefComplaint: I've had high fevers, chills, a cough, and it really hurts to breathe.
cases.history: A 32-year-old man with active injection heroin use is admitted with eight days of high spiking fevers, shaking rigors, a productive cough, progressive shortness of breath, and right-sided pleuritic chest pain. He injects into the veins of both forearms and has shared injection equipment. He denies any known prior cardiac disease, recent dental work, or sick contacts. Social history: unstable housing, smokes tobacco, drinks alcohol occasionally, and last injected two days ago. Past medical history is notable for several prior skin and soft-tissue abscesses. He takes no regular medications and has no known drug allergies. Review of systems is negative for peripheral embolic symptoms, focal weakness, headache, or visual changes. Family history is noncontributory. He initially tried to manage at home but presented when the breathlessness and chest pain worsened and he could no longer lie flat comfortably because of the pain on inspiration.
cases.exam: He is febrile, tachycardic, and ill-appearing. There are multiple needle track marks over both forearms. Cardiac auscultation reveals a soft holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration (Carvallo sign), with prominent jugular venous v waves. Lungs have scattered bibasilar crackles. Notably, there are no splinter hemorrhages, Janeway lesions, Osler nodes, or Roth spots—peripheral stigmata are typically absent in right-sided disease. The abdomen is soft without splenomegaly. There is no focal neurologic deficit and no meningismus. Skin shows healed and a few fresh injection sites without surrounding cellulitis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this febrile patient who injects drugs and has a right-sided regurgitant murmur, what is the best initial workup?
options: Three sets of blood cultures, chest imaging, and echocardiography
Sputum culture and a trial of outpatient azithromycin for pneumonia
D-dimer and lower-extremity ultrasound only
Bronchoscopy
stages.explanation: Fever plus injection drug use plus a tricuspid regurgitant murmur that intensifies on inspiration strongly suggests right-sided endocarditis with septic pulmonary emboli, so the workup is blood cultures from multiple sites, chest imaging (CT readily demonstrates septic emboli), and echocardiography to identify a vegetation. Treating this as simple community-acquired pneumonia with outpatient azithromycin would miss endocarditis and bacteremia. D-dimer and lower-extremity ultrasound target bland venous thromboembolism rather than an infected cardiac source. Bronchoscopy is not an appropriate first step and does not establish the diagnosis. Cultures and echocardiography drive both diagnosis and therapy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Blood cultures: 4 of 4 bottles positive for Staphylococcus aureus (methicillin-sensitive, MSSA)
WBC 17.6 ×10³/µL
CT chest: multiple bilateral peripheral nodular and wedge-shaped lesions, several cavitating — consistent with septic pulmonary emboli
TTE: 1.4 cm mobile vegetation on the tricuspid valve with moderate tricuspid regurgitation; left-sided valves normal
ECG: sinus tachycardia

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which statement best summarizes the diagnosis?
options: Community-acquired pneumonia with parapneumonic effusion
Left-sided mitral valve endocarditis
Right-sided (tricuspid) S. aureus endocarditis with septic pulmonary emboli
Pulmonary tuberculosis
stages.explanation: Injection drug use with Staphylococcus aureus bacteremia, a tricuspid valve vegetation, and multiple bilateral peripheral cavitary lung lesions is right-sided endocarditis complicated by septic pulmonary emboli. Community-acquired pneumonia would not produce a tricuspid vegetation or multifocal cavitary emboli seeded by S. aureus bacteremia. The vegetation is on the tricuspid valve, not the mitral valve, and the left-sided valves are normal. Tuberculosis tends to be more indolent with upper-lobe cavitation and a different microbiologic and clinical picture, and would not explain acute S. aureus bacteremia with a tricuspid vegetation. The findings together define right-sided staphylococcal endocarditis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Cultures confirm MSSA. What is the best antibiotic choice for this endocarditis?
options: Vancomycin
Ceftriaxone monotherapy
Oral linezolid
Nafcillin (or cefazolin)
stages.explanation: For methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus, an antistaphylococcal beta-lactam such as nafcillin or oxacillin—or cefazolin—is the first-line agent and is clinically superior to vancomycin for MSSA bacteremia and endocarditis. Vancomycin is reserved for MRSA or for patients with a beta-lactam allergy and has slower bactericidal activity, making it inferior when MSSA is confirmed. Ceftriaxone monotherapy provides inadequate antistaphylococcal coverage for endocarditis. Oral linezolid is not standard initial therapy for S. aureus endocarditis or bacteremia, which require bactericidal intravenous treatment. Choosing the beta-lactam optimizes outcomes once susceptibility is known.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which is an appropriate component of comprehensive care for this patient?
options: Discharge on oral antibiotics once afebrile for 24 hours
Offer medication for opioid use disorder (e.g., buprenorphine or methadone) and addiction support
Immediate tricuspid valve replacement for all right-sided IE
Avoid discussing substance use to preserve rapport
stages.explanation: Addressing the underlying opioid use disorder with medication (buprenorphine or methadone) and harm-reduction counseling is central to comprehensive care, because untreated addiction drives reinjection, recurrent bacteremia, and reinfection. Right-sided endocarditis usually responds to prolonged intravenous antibiotics, and surgery is reserved for specific indications such as persistent bacteremia, large or refractory vegetations, or right heart failure—not for all cases. Early discharge on oral antibiotics after S. aureus endocarditis is inappropriate and risks relapse. Avoiding the topic of substance use neglects the most important modifiable driver of his disease and worsens outcomes.

cases.diagnosis: Right-sided (tricuspid) Staphylococcus aureus infective endocarditis with septic pulmonary emboli in a person who injects drugs
pearls: Right-sided endocarditis in people who inject drugs is usually S. aureus and presents with respiratory symptoms from septic pulmonary emboli.
A tricuspid regurgitation murmur that grows louder on inspiration (Carvallo sign) is a clue; peripheral embolic stigmata are typically absent.
MSSA endocarditis is treated with nafcillin or cefazolin, not vancomycin.
Treat the opioid use disorder—it is central to preventing relapse and reinfection.

cases.title: Migrating joint pains and new jerky movements in a child
cases.chiefComplaint: His joints hurt one after another, and now he's making strange movements he can't control.
cases.history: A previously healthy 10-year-old boy is admitted with five days of migratory joint pain and swelling. It began in the knees, then moved to the ankles and wrists, with each joint inflamed for a day or two before improving as the next was affected. About three weeks ago he had a sore throat that was neither tested nor treated. Over the past few days his parents noticed increasing clumsiness, dropping objects, fidgety involuntary movements, emotional lability with frequent crying, and a marked decline in his handwriting. He has had low-grade fevers. The family did not notice any rash. Immunizations are up to date and there has been no recent travel. He has taken intermittent ibuprofen with partial relief. He has no known drug allergies. Social history: lives with his parents and a younger sibling, attends school. Family history is notable for a maternal uncle who had "heart valve problems" as a young adult.
cases.exam: He has a warm, swollen, tender right wrist and left ankle, with other joints currently improving (migratory pattern). Cardiac exam reveals a new grade II/VI apical holosystolic murmur radiating to the axilla. Neurologic exam shows nonrhythmic, involuntary, dance-like movements of the limbs and face, a "milkmaid grip" (waxing-and-waning grip strength), a darting protruded tongue, and hypotonia. Skin examination reveals a faint pink, annular, non-pruritic rash with central clearing on the trunk (erythema marginatum) and small, firm, painless nodules over the elbows and knees (subcutaneous nodules). There is no meningismus and the remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which workup best establishes evidence of antecedent group A streptococcal infection and assesses cardiac involvement?
options: Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies
Blood cultures and a CT chest
Anti-streptolysin O (ASO) and anti-DNase B titers, plus ECG and echocardiography
Serum uric acid and joint aspiration for crystals
stages.explanation: Acute rheumatic fever requires evidence of a preceding group A streptococcal infection, best shown by elevated anti-streptolysin O and anti-DNase B titers (a throat culture is often negative weeks later), along with cardiac assessment by ECG (which may show PR prolongation) and echocardiography (to detect valvulitis). Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP target rheumatoid arthritis, which does not fit this post-streptococcal, migratory, self-limited arthritis with chorea. Blood cultures and CT chest do not address this syndrome. Uric acid and crystal analysis evaluate gout, which is essentially never the cause in a 10-year-old with migratory post-streptococcal arthritis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ASO titer: markedly elevated; anti-DNase B: elevated
Throat culture: negative (the pharyngitis has already resolved)
ESR 68 mm/hr; CRP 54 mg/L
ECG: prolonged PR interval (first-degree AV block)
Echocardiogram: mild mitral regurgitation consistent with valvulitis
CBC: mild normocytic anemia

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: With confirmed antecedent GAS infection, which combination establishes the diagnosis under the Jones criteria?
options: One minor criterion alone is sufficient
Carditis, polyarthritis, and chorea—multiple major criteria—confirm acute rheumatic fever
Elevated ESR and fever alone (two minor criteria) confirm it
The diagnosis requires a positive throat culture and cannot be made without it
stages.explanation: With evidence of preceding GAS infection, acute rheumatic fever is diagnosed by 2 major criteria or 1 major plus 2 minor criteria. This child has multiple major criteria—carditis (a new murmur with mitral regurgitation and a prolonged PR interval), migratory polyarthritis, and Sydenham chorea—plus erythema marginatum and subcutaneous nodules, far exceeding the threshold. A single minor criterion, or two minor criteria alone, is insufficient. Antecedent streptococcal infection can be proven serologically (elevated ASO/anti-DNase B), so a positive throat culture is not required and is frequently negative by the time ARF appears.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best acute management for this child?
options: High-dose anti-inflammatory therapy (aspirin/NSAIDs) for arthritis and carditis, plus penicillin to eradicate residual GAS
Colchicine and allopurinol
Methotrexate and a TNF inhibitor
Observation alone, since the illness is self-limited
stages.explanation: Acute rheumatic fever is treated with anti-inflammatory therapy—high-dose aspirin or NSAIDs for the arthritis and carditis, with corticosteroids considered for severe carditis—plus a course of penicillin to eradicate any residual group A streptococci. Colchicine and allopurinol treat gout, which is not the diagnosis. Methotrexate and TNF inhibitors are used for chronic inflammatory arthritides such as rheumatoid or juvenile idiopathic arthritis, not for ARF. Observation alone is inappropriate because untreated carditis can progress to permanent rheumatic heart disease. Anti-inflammatory control plus streptococcal eradication is the standard acute approach.

stages.label: PROPHYLAXIS
stages.question: What is the best long-term strategy to prevent recurrence and progressive valve damage?
options: A single 10-day course of penicillin only
As-needed antibiotics with each future sore throat
Daily low-dose aspirin indefinitely
Secondary prophylaxis with intramuscular benzathine penicillin G every 3-4 weeks for a prolonged period, longest when carditis is present
stages.explanation: Because recurrent group A streptococcal infections worsen rheumatic heart disease, patients need continuous secondary prophylaxis—typically intramuscular benzathine penicillin G every 3-4 weeks—for many years; with carditis or residual valve disease this continues much longer, often into adulthood. A single 10-day course treats only the current infection and does not prevent future episodes. Treating only symptomatic sore throats misses asymptomatic streptococcal carriage that can still trigger recurrence. Daily aspirin reduces inflammation but does nothing to prevent streptococcal infection. Long-term penicillin prophylaxis is the cornerstone of preventing recurrent carditis.

cases.diagnosis: Acute rheumatic fever with carditis, migratory polyarthritis, and Sydenham chorea following untreated streptococcal pharyngitis
pearls: Diagnose ARF with the Jones criteria plus evidence of antecedent GAS (ASO/anti-DNase B); throat culture is often negative by presentation.
Major criteria spell JONES: Joints (migratory polyarthritis), heart (carditis), Nodules (subcutaneous), Erythema marginatum, and Sydenham chorea.
Sydenham chorea can appear weeks later and may be the sole presenting manifestation.
Secondary prophylaxis with benzathine penicillin prevents recurrent carditis; the duration is longest when carditis is present.

cases.title: A limping boy who won't bear weight on his leg
cases.chiefComplaint: He's had a fever and his leg hurts so much he won't walk on it.
cases.history: A 9-year-old boy is admitted with five days of progressively worsening pain in the lower right thigh just above the knee, fevers to 39 °C at home, and a limp that has now progressed to refusing to bear weight at all. About ten days ago he scraped his knee playing soccer; the abrasion healed without obvious infection. He has had no joint swelling and no prior bone or joint infections. Immunizations are up to date. He has no history of sickle cell disease. Acetaminophen has given only partial relief. He has no known drug allergies. Social history: active child, lives with his family, attends school, no sick contacts. Review of systems is negative for rash, recent travel, dysuria, or abdominal pain. Family history is noncontributory. His parents became alarmed when he woke overnight crying and was unable to stand on the leg in the morning.
cases.exam: He is a well-developed boy who appears in pain and guards the right leg. There is focal point tenderness, warmth, and mild swelling over the distal femoral metaphysis just proximal to the knee. He firmly resists weight-bearing. The adjacent knee joint has no effusion, and passive range of motion of the knee is only mildly uncomfortable and nearly full—markedly better tolerated than direct palpation of the bone. There is no overlying skin break, fluctuance, or erythematous streaking. The leg is neurovascularly intact distally with normal pulses, capillary refill, and sensation. The contralateral leg and the rest of the exam are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the best initial workup for this febrile child with metaphyseal point tenderness and refusal to bear weight?
options: Plain radiograph alone; if normal, reassure and discharge
Inflammatory markers (CBC, ESR, CRP) and blood cultures, plain radiographs, and MRI of the affected bone
Joint aspiration of the knee as the sole diagnostic test
Whole-body bone scan before any blood work
stages.explanation: Acute hematogenous osteomyelitis is the leading concern, so the workup combines inflammatory markers (CBC, ESR, CRP) and blood cultures (often positive), plain radiographs, and MRI, which is the most sensitive early modality and reveals marrow edema before plain films change. A normal early radiograph does NOT exclude osteomyelitis, so reassurance and discharge would be unsafe. Knee aspiration targets septic arthritis, but here the joint is relatively spared, so it is not the sole appropriate test. A bone scan is less specific and not the first step when MRI is available. This combined approach establishes diagnosis and guides therapy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 16.4 ×10³/µL (neutrophil predominant)
ESR 74 mm/hr; CRP 96 mg/L
Blood cultures: growing Staphylococcus aureus
Plain radiograph: soft-tissue swelling, no definite bony lytic changes
MRI distal femur: bone-marrow edema and a subperiosteal fluid collection in the metaphysis, consistent with osteomyelitis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis and causative organism?
options: Septic arthritis of the knee due to Kingella kingae
Ewing sarcoma of the femur
Transient (toxic) synovitis of the hip
Acute hematogenous osteomyelitis of the distal femur due to Staphylococcus aureus
stages.explanation: Metaphyseal point tenderness, elevated inflammatory markers, marrow edema with a subperiosteal collection on MRI, and S. aureus bacteremia indicate acute hematogenous osteomyelitis, most commonly caused by Staphylococcus aureus in children. Septic arthritis would show a joint effusion and severe pain with any joint motion, whereas this joint is relatively spared. Ewing sarcoma typically produces a destructive lesion with a periosteal reaction over a more chronic course and would not explain acute S. aureus bacteremia. Transient (toxic) synovitis is afebrile or low-grade, centers on the hip, and has normal or near-normal inflammatory markers, none of which fit this child.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best initial treatment?
options: Oral antibiotics as an outpatient for 5 days
NSAIDs alone with activity restriction
Empiric IV antistaphylococcal therapy (e.g., cefazolin or nafcillin; vancomycin if MRSA is likely), transitioning to oral therapy as the child improves
Immediate above-knee amputation
stages.explanation: Treatment begins with empiric intravenous antistaphylococcal therapy—cefazolin or nafcillin, or vancomycin where MRSA is prevalent or disease is severe—with a transition to oral antibiotics once the child is clinically improving and the CRP is falling; children frequently complete therapy orally. A brief 5-day outpatient oral course is insufficient to cure bone infection. NSAIDs alone do not treat the underlying infection. Amputation is not indicated for uncomplicated acute osteomyelitis and would be grossly inappropriate. Prompt targeted antibiotics, adjusted to culture results, are the foundation of management.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After 48 hours of appropriate IV antibiotics he remains febrile with a rising CRP. What is the best next step?
options: Continue the same antibiotics and reassess in another week
Switch to oral antibiotics and discharge
Obtain repeat imaging and consult orthopedics for surgical drainage of a possible abscess/subperiosteal collection
Stop antibiotics to allow cultures to clarify
stages.explanation: Failure to improve after 48 hours of appropriate antibiotics suggests a drainable collection, such as a subperiosteal or intraosseous abscess, so repeat imaging and orthopedic consultation for surgical drainage and debridement are indicated alongside continued antibiotics. Simply waiting another week risks ongoing bone destruction and chronic osteomyelitis. Stepping down to oral therapy and discharging a child who is still febrile and worsening is unsafe. Stopping antibiotics in a patient with confirmed bacteremic osteomyelitis is dangerous and could precipitate sepsis. Source control by drainage is the appropriate escalation.

cases.diagnosis: Acute hematogenous osteomyelitis of the distal femoral metaphysis due to Staphylococcus aureus
pearls: Acute hematogenous osteomyelitis in children localizes to the long-bone metaphysis (rich, sluggish blood supply); S. aureus is most common.
Early plain films are often normal; MRI is the most sensitive early test.
Metaphyseal point tenderness without a joint effusion favors osteomyelitis over septic arthritis.
Failure to improve on appropriate antibiotics suggests an abscess that needs surgical drainage.

cases.title: Relentless focal back pain that's worse at night
cases.chiefComplaint: My lower back has been killing me for weeks—it even hurts when I'm lying perfectly still.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man with a history of injection drug use is admitted with five weeks of progressively worsening, focal mid-to-lower back pain. The pain is constant, worse at night, and not relieved by rest or position change, which is atypical for mechanical back pain. He reports intermittent low-grade fevers and night sweats. He last injected several weeks ago and has had skin abscesses in the past. He firmly denies leg weakness, numbness, gait change, or bowel or bladder incontinence, and reports no saddle numbness. Past medical history includes hepatitis C. Social history: smokes tobacco, has unstable housing, and drinks alcohol occasionally. He takes no regular medications and has no known drug allergies. There has been no recent trauma or heavy lifting. Family history is noncontributory. He sought care because over-the-counter analgesics no longer touched the pain and the night-time symptoms were keeping him from sleeping.
cases.exam: There is focal tenderness to percussion over the lower thoracic/upper lumbar spinous processes with paraspinal muscle spasm, and pain that limits spinal flexion. There are needle track marks on both arms. Critically, the neurologic exam is normal: full lower-extremity strength, intact sensation, symmetric and normal deep tendon reflexes, no clonus or upgoing toes, normal anal sphincter tone, and no saddle anesthesia. There is no abdominal pulsatile mass or bruit and no costovertebral angle tenderness. The skin shows healed injection sites without active cellulitis, and the rest of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the best initial workup for this injection drug user with focal, night-predominant back pain and low-grade fevers?
options: Lumbar plain films and a short course of muscle relaxants, with PT referral
Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics started immediately, before any cultures
Electromyography and nerve conduction studies
ESR, CRP, blood cultures, and contrast-enhanced MRI of the spine
stages.explanation: Injection drug use with focal, rest-unrelieved, night-predominant back pain and fevers points to vertebral osteomyelitis/discitis, so the workup is ESR and CRP (almost always elevated and useful for tracking response), blood cultures, and contrast-enhanced MRI of the spine, the most sensitive and specific imaging test. Plain films plus muscle relaxants and physical therapy treat mechanical pain and would miss a spinal infection. Starting broad-spectrum antibiotics before obtaining cultures in a stable patient can prevent microbiologic diagnosis. EMG and nerve conduction studies evaluate radiculopathy or neuropathy, not osteomyelitis. Inflammatory markers, cultures, and MRI together establish the diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ESR 92 mm/hr; CRP 110 mg/L
WBC 11.2 ×10³/µL
Blood cultures (two sets): no growth to date
MRI spine with contrast: T1 hypointense and T2 hyperintense signal in the T11-T12 vertebral bodies with disc-space involvement and endplate erosion; enhancement of the disc and adjacent marrow — consistent with discitis-osteomyelitis
No epidural collection or cord compression identified

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Blood cultures remain negative and the patient is hemodynamically stable. What is the best next step to guide therapy?
options: Image-guided needle biopsy of the disc/vertebra for culture before starting antibiotics
Start empiric vancomycin and discharge with oral follow-up
Proceed directly to spinal fusion surgery
Treat empirically for tuberculosis without sampling
stages.explanation: When blood cultures are negative and the patient is stable, a CT-guided needle biopsy of the disc/vertebra for microbiologic culture before antibiotics is the best next step, because long-course targeted therapy depends on identifying the pathogen and its susceptibilities. Starting empiric vancomycin and discharging skips the diagnosis and risks under- or over-treatment. Surgery is not indicated without instability, a neurologic deficit, or an epidural abscess. Empiric anti-tuberculous therapy without sampling is inappropriate when pyogenic infection (commonly S. aureus) is far more likely in this clinical setting. Obtaining tissue first optimizes the entire treatment course.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Biopsy grows methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus. What is the best treatment?
options: Two weeks of oral cephalexin
Approximately 6 weeks of culture-directed IV antibiotics (e.g., cefazolin/nafcillin for MSSA)
A single dose of IV ceftriaxone
Surgical fusion as the primary therapy
stages.explanation: Pyogenic vertebral osteomyelitis is treated with culture-directed antibiotics for roughly 6 weeks; for MSSA, cefazolin or nafcillin is preferred. A 2-week oral course or a single intravenous dose is far too short to sterilize an infected vertebra and disc. Surgery is reserved for neurologic compromise, spinal instability, an epidural abscess, or failure of medical therapy, none of which are present here given the normal neurologic exam and absence of an abscess. Prolonged, organism-specific intravenous therapy with monitoring of clinical response and inflammatory markers is the appropriate management.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: On hospital day 4 he develops new bilateral leg weakness and urinary retention. What is the best next step?
options: Increase the antibiotic dose and continue observation
Order outpatient physical therapy
Emergent repeat MRI and urgent neurosurgical/spine evaluation for epidural abscess with cord compression
Start gabapentin for neuropathic pain
stages.explanation: New bilateral leg weakness and urinary retention signal an epidural abscess causing spinal cord or cauda equina compression—a surgical emergency requiring emergent repeat MRI and urgent neurosurgical or spine evaluation for decompression. Merely increasing the antibiotic dose and observing wastes the narrow window in which surgery can preserve neurologic function. Outpatient physical therapy and gabapentin treat the symptoms while ignoring an evolving neurologic emergency. Time to surgical decompression strongly influences recovery, so rapid imaging and operative evaluation take precedence over all other measures.

cases.diagnosis: Vertebral osteomyelitis with discitis (T11-T12) due to Staphylococcus aureus in a person who injects drugs
pearls: Vertebral osteomyelitis/discitis causes focal back pain that is worse at night and at rest, with elevated ESR/CRP; MRI is the imaging test of choice.
Obtain a microbiologic diagnosis (blood cultures or image-guided biopsy) before antibiotics when the patient is stable.
S. aureus is the most common pathogen; treat with about 6 weeks of culture-directed therapy.
New neurologic deficits suggest an epidural abscess—obtain emergent MRI and urgent surgical decompression.

cases.title: All-over joint pains, sore tendons, and a few skin sores
cases.chiefComplaint: My joints have been hurting all over, my wrists are swollen, and I've got these spots on my skin.
cases.history: A 25-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with several days of migratory joint pains involving the wrists, knees, and ankles, along with pain and swelling over the backs of both hands and wrists. She has had fevers and chills and noticed a few small pus-filled bumps on her arms and legs. She is sexually active with a new male partner over the past month and uses condoms inconsistently. She reports mild dysuria but no vaginal discharge. Her last menstrual period was two weeks ago. She has no prior joint disease, no recent gastrointestinal illness, and no tick exposure or travel. Social history: drinks alcohol occasionally, does not smoke or use drugs. Her only medication is a combined oral contraceptive, and she has no known drug allergies. Family history is noncontributory. She came in when the joint pains and the swelling over her wrists became severe enough to limit using her hands.
cases.exam: She is febrile. There is tenderness and swelling along the extensor tendon sheaths of both wrists and the dorsum of the hands, with pain on finger extension (tenosynovitis). The joint involvement is migratory without a single dominant large effusion early on. The skin shows about six scattered, painless pustular and vesiculopustular lesions on the distal extremities. There are no genital ulcers, and pelvic examination reveals no overt cervical discharge or adnexal tenderness. Cardiac exam reveals no murmur. There is no conjunctivitis, no oral ulcers, no nuchal rigidity, and no focal neurologic deficit. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given migratory polyarthralgia, tenosynovitis, and a pustular rash in a young sexually active woman, what is the most appropriate diagnostic workup?
options: Serum uric acid and start indomethacin for presumed gout
Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP, then start methotrexate
Blood cultures, arthrocentesis with synovial fluid analysis/culture, and NAAT of cervical, urethral, rectal, and pharyngeal sites
Lyme serology alone
stages.explanation: The triad of migratory polyarthralgia, tenosynovitis, and a pustular rash in a young, sexually active patient points to disseminated gonococcal infection, so the workup includes blood cultures, arthrocentesis with synovial fluid analysis and culture, and—highest yield—nucleic acid amplification testing from cervical, urethral, rectal, and pharyngeal sites even when those sites are asymptomatic. Gout is highly unlikely in a 25-year-old woman and does not cause tenosynovitis or pustules. Rheumatoid factor/anti-CCP and methotrexate target rheumatoid arthritis, not an acute febrile infection. Lyme disease can cause arthritis but does not produce this pustular, tenosynovial, migratory triad.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Synovial fluid (wrist): WBC 38,000/µL with neutrophil predominance; no crystals; Gram stain negative
Synovial and blood cultures: pending (often negative in the arthritis-dermatitis form)
Cervical and rectal NAAT: positive for Neisseria gonorrhoeae
Chlamydia NAAT: pending
WBC 13.5 ×10³/µL; ESR 48 mm/hr
HIV and syphilis (RPR) screening: sent

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the best diagnosis?
options: Crystal-induced (gout) arthritis
Reactive arthritis (post-enteric/urethral)
Acute rheumatoid arthritis
Disseminated gonococcal infection (arthritis-dermatitis syndrome)
stages.explanation: A positive gonococcal NAAT plus the classic triad of migratory polyarthralgia, tenosynovitis, and pustular dermatitis confirms disseminated gonococcal infection in its arthritis-dermatitis form; synovial Gram stain and culture are frequently negative in this bacteremic form, so the diagnosis rests on NAAT and the clinical pattern. Gout would reveal crystals in synovial fluid, which were absent. Reactive arthritis follows enteric or urethral infection and may include conjunctivitis and urethritis but does not yield a positive gonococcal NAAT as its cause and lacks pustular lesions. Rheumatoid arthritis is chronic and symmetric, not an acute febrile illness with pustules and tenosynovitis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best treatment for this patient?
options: Oral azithromycin single dose only
IV/IM ceftriaxone, plus treatment for possible chlamydial co-infection (doxycycline), with partner notification and treatment
Oral ciprofloxacin for 7 days
Intra-articular corticosteroid injection
stages.explanation: Disseminated gonococcal infection is treated with ceftriaxone (intravenous or intramuscular), usually combined with doxycycline to cover frequent chlamydial co-infection, along with partner evaluation and treatment and STI counseling. A single oral dose of azithromycin is inadequate for disseminated disease. Fluoroquinolones such as ciprofloxacin are no longer recommended for gonorrhea because of widespread resistance. An intra-articular corticosteroid injection does not treat the infection and could worsen a septic joint. Ceftriaxone-based therapy with chlamydia coverage and partner management is the standard of care.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After starting ceftriaxone, what additional step is most appropriate?
options: Screen for other sexually transmitted infections (HIV, syphilis, chlamydia, hepatitis) and counsel on prevention
No further testing is needed
Begin chronic immunosuppression
Restrict to strict bed rest for 6 weeks
stages.explanation: A diagnosed sexually transmitted infection warrants screening for coexisting infections—HIV, syphilis, chlamydia, and hepatitis B and C—along with prevention counseling and partner notification and treatment, because co-infection is common. Doing no further testing would miss these frequently associated infections. Chronic immunosuppression is a treatment for autoimmune arthritis, not for an infection, and would be harmful here. Prolonged strict bed rest is not indicated; while a frankly purulent joint may need drainage, the patient should be mobilized as the infection is controlled. Comprehensive STI screening and counseling complete appropriate management.

cases.diagnosis: Disseminated gonococcal infection (arthritis-dermatitis syndrome) due to Neisseria gonorrhoeae
pearls: Disseminated gonococcal infection presents either as the triad (migratory polyarthralgia, tenosynovitis, pustular rash) or as a purulent monoarthritis.
Synovial Gram stain and culture are often negative in DGI; mucosal NAAT (cervix, urethra, rectum, pharynx) is high-yield.
Treat with ceftriaxone and cover chlamydia; do not use fluoroquinolones because of resistance.
Always screen for and treat coexisting STIs and ensure partner notification and treatment.

cases.title: Hot, swollen knee for two days
cases.chiefComplaint: My right knee blew up and I can barely move it.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man presents with 2 days of progressively worsening right knee pain, swelling, and warmth. The pain began without trauma and is now so severe he cannot bear weight or flex the joint. He reports subjective fevers and chills overnight. He denies prior similar episodes, recent travel, tick exposure, or new sexual partners. He scraped his shin gardening a week ago and noted a small area of overlying skin redness that has since improved. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 8.9%), hypertension, and osteoarthritis of both knees. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, and acetaminophen as needed. He has no known drug allergies. He drinks two beers nightly and is a former smoker. He worked as a plumber and lives with his wife. Family history is notable for gout in his father, but he has never had a documented gout flare or an elevated uric acid. He has taken no recent antibiotics and has no indwelling hardware or prosthetic joints. Review of systems is negative for rash, urethral discharge, conjunctivitis, low back pain, or other joint involvement.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing but alert. The right knee is markedly swollen, erythematous, and warm with a large effusion and a positive patellar tap. Active and passive range of motion are severely limited by pain; even minimal flexion is intolerable. There is no overlying skin ulceration or fluctuance, and the healed shin abrasion is dry without surrounding cellulitis. No other joint is swollen or tender, and there are no tophi. There is no conjunctival injection and no genital lesions or discharge. Distal pulses, sensation, and capillary refill in the foot are intact. Cardiac exam reveals no murmur. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Start empiric IV vancomycin immediately and arrange outpatient rheumatology follow-up
Perform arthrocentesis of the knee for cell count, crystals, Gram stain, and culture before antibiotics
Treat empirically for an acute gout flare with colchicine and an NSAID
Obtain an MRI of the knee before any intervention
stages.explanation: An acutely hot, swollen, immobile joint is septic arthritis until proven otherwise, and arthrocentesis is the essential first step. Synovial fluid cell count with differential, crystal analysis, Gram stain, and culture confirm the diagnosis and guide therapy; whenever feasible, fluid should be obtained before antibiotics to maximize culture yield. Option A starts antibiotics without sampling and discharges a potentially septic joint, risking rapid cartilage destruction and a missed diagnosis. Option C anchors on gout despite his lack of prior flares and his systemic signs—treating presumptively for crystal disease while a bacterial infection festers can be catastrophic, and crystals and infection can coexist. Option D delays a bedside, definitive test; MRI may show an effusion but cannot distinguish sterile from septic fluid and should not postpone aspiration. Prompt diagnosis and joint drainage preserve the joint and reduce mortality.

stages.label: LABS & SYNOVIAL FLUID
stages.content:
WBC 15.8 ×10³/µL (88% neutrophils)
ESR 74 mm/hr
CRP 142 mg/L
Serum urate 5.1 mg/dL
Synovial fluid: cloudy, yellow
Synovial WBC 68,000 /µL (92% neutrophils)
Synovial crystals: none seen
Synovial Gram stain: gram-positive cocci in clusters
Blood cultures: pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits the synovial fluid findings?
options: Acute gout flare
Calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease (pseudogout)
Bacterial (non-gonococcal) septic arthritis
Reactive arthritis
stages.explanation: The synovial white count of 68,000/µL with 92% neutrophils, absence of crystals, and gram-positive cocci in clusters indicate bacterial septic arthritis; clusters of gram-positive cocci point to Staphylococcus aureus, the most common cause of non-gonococcal septic arthritis. A synovial WBC >50,000/µL with neutrophil predominance markedly raises the probability of infection. Gout (A) and pseudogout (B) are excluded by the absence of monosodium urate or calcium pyrophosphate crystals, and his serum urate is normal—though crystal disease and infection can coexist, the Gram stain settles it. Reactive arthritis (D) follows GI or genitourinary infection, is usually oligoarticular with extra-articular features (conjunctivitis, urethritis), and would not show organisms on Gram stain. The combination of high inflammatory markers, purulent fluid, and visible organisms confirms a closed-space infection requiring urgent treatment.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management?
options: Empiric IV vancomycin plus urgent surgical or arthroscopic joint drainage
Oral cephalexin and outpatient follow-up
Intra-articular corticosteroid injection
Ceftriaxone alone without drainage
stages.explanation: Septic arthritis requires both prompt empiric antibiotics and source control via drainage. Gram-positive cocci in clusters mandate empiric coverage for MRSA with IV vancomycin until susceptibilities return, and the purulent joint must be decompressed—by arthroscopic or open washout, or serial large-bore aspiration—to remove the destructive inflammatory load. Option B (oral cephalexin, outpatient) is grossly inadequate for a closed-space infection with bacteremia risk and provides no source control. Option C (intra-articular steroid) is contraindicated and worsens infection. Option D (ceftriaxone alone) neither reliably covers MRSA nor addresses the need for drainage, and antibiotics alone cannot sterilize a pus-filled joint. Combining empiric anti-staphylococcal therapy with mechanical drainage limits cartilage loss and systemic spread; therapy is later tailored to culture and susceptibility results.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Synovial culture grows methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA) and blood cultures are positive. What is the best next step?
options: Narrow to oral cephalexin and discharge home
Switch to IV cefazolin (or nafcillin) and obtain transthoracic echocardiography
Continue vancomycin indefinitely as the preferred agent
Add rifampin to vancomycin for biofilm penetration
stages.explanation: Once methicillin-sensitive S. aureus is confirmed, a beta-lactam such as cefazolin or nafcillin is superior to vancomycin and becomes the agent of choice—vancomycin is inferior for MSSA bacteremia and should be abandoned (so option C is wrong). S. aureus bacteremia mandates evaluation for endovascular seeding: echocardiography to exclude infective endocarditis, assessment for metastatic foci, and documentation of blood-culture clearance. Option A underdoses a deep-seated, bacteremic infection with oral therapy and premature discharge. Option D (rifampin) is used for staphylococcal infection involving prosthetic material or hardware to penetrate biofilm; this is a native joint, so rifampin is not indicated and should not be added to a failing regimen. Continued source control with drainage plus IV beta-lactam therapy, typically for several weeks, is standard.

cases.diagnosis: Non-gonococcal (Staphylococcus aureus) septic arthritis of the knee
pearls: Any acute hot, swollen joint is septic arthritis until proven otherwise—aspirate before antibiotics.
Synovial WBC >50,000/µL with neutrophil predominance strongly suggests bacterial infection, and crystals can coexist with infection.
S. aureus is the most common cause of non-gonococcal septic arthritis; treat MSSA with cefazolin or nafcillin, not vancomycin.
Source control (drainage) plus IV antibiotics is essential, and S. aureus bacteremia warrants echocardiography.

cases.title: Aching hip that never settled after surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: My new hip has ached deep inside ever since the replacement, and now it's getting worse.
cases.history: A 72-year-old woman presents with 13 months of deep groin and anterior thigh pain on the right, dating back to her elective total hip arthroplasty for osteoarthritis. She says the pain never fully resolved after surgery and is now worsening, aggravated by weight-bearing and present at rest. Her early postoperative course was notable for delayed wound healing with prolonged serous drainage. She denies acute trauma, falls, or recent dental procedures. Over the past month she has had intermittent low-grade fevers and fatigue but no chills, night sweats, or new urinary or respiratory symptoms. Past medical history includes osteoarthritis, hypertension, and hypothyroidism. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily and levothyroxine 88 mcg daily. She has no known drug allergies. She is a retired schoolteacher, a lifelong nonsmoker, and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is unremarkable. She has had no other joint replacements and no indwelling lines or catheters. Review of systems is otherwise negative.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no acute distress with an antalgic gait. The surgical incision over the right hip is well-healed with no erythema, warmth, fluctuance, or sinus tract, and no active drainage. There is deep tenderness in the groin and pain reproduced with both active and passive hip range of motion, particularly internal rotation. Leg lengths are symmetric and there is no overlying effusion or skin breakdown. Distal pulses, sensation, and strength in the leg are intact, and the contralateral hip and both knees are nontender. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are normal. No peripheral stigmata of endocarditis are present.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the best initial workup?
options: Obtain ESR and CRP and weight-bearing plain radiographs of the hip
Refer directly for revision arthroplasty
Reassure her that pain is expected and prescribe NSAIDs
Order a hip MRI as the first test
stages.explanation: Persistent or worsening pain a year after arthroplasty—particularly pain present since surgery—raises concern for prosthetic joint infection (PJI), and the recommended initial workup is serum inflammatory markers (ESR and CRP) plus plain radiographs. Elevated ESR and CRP are sensitive screens for PJI, while radiographs can reveal loosening, lucency, or osteolysis. Option B (immediate revision) is premature without diagnostic data and inappropriate before infection is evaluated. Option C (reassurance and NSAIDs) anchors on mechanical pain and risks missing an indolent infection—pain that never resolved after surgery, especially with delayed wound healing, is a red flag. Option D (MRI) is degraded by metallic artifact around the prosthesis and is not the first-line study; radiographs and markers come first. If markers are elevated or radiographs abnormal, joint aspiration is the next definitive step.

stages.label: INFLAMMATORY MARKERS & X-RAY
stages.content:
ESR 62 mm/hr
CRP 48 mg/L
WBC 8.9 ×10³/µL
Hip radiograph: focal periprosthetic lucency at the femoral stem with early component loosening
No periprosthetic fracture

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given the elevated markers and radiographic loosening, what is the key next diagnostic step?
options: Empiric oral antibiotics for 6 weeks
Repeat radiographs in 3 months
Image-guided arthrocentesis for synovial cell count, differential, and culture
Technetium bone scan only
stages.explanation: With elevated ESR/CRP and radiographic loosening, joint aspiration is the key diagnostic step. Synovial fluid is sent for cell count with differential and culture; in chronic PJI the diagnostic thresholds are far lower than in native joints (roughly synovial WBC >3,000/µL with >80% neutrophils), and alpha-defensin can support the diagnosis. Crucially, antibiotics must be withheld before sampling to preserve culture yield—so option A (empiric antibiotics) sabotages the diagnosis. Option B (watchful waiting) delays care while infection erodes bone and loosens the implant. Option D (bone scan alone) is nonspecific—uptake persists for months to years after arthroplasty and cannot distinguish infection from aseptic loosening. Aspiration with culture identifies the organism and guides both the surgical strategy and targeted antibiotics.

stages.label: SYNOVIAL FLUID & CULTURE
stages.content:
Synovial WBC 4,800 /µL (86% neutrophils)
Synovial crystals: none
Synovial alpha-defensin: positive
Synovial culture: Staphylococcus epidermidis (coagulase-negative staphylococcus), same organism on two separate aspirate cultures
Methicillin resistance: present

stages.label: INTERPRETATION
stages.question: How should the coagulase-negative staphylococcus result be interpreted?
options: Dismiss the culture as a skin contaminant and stop the workup
Regard the coagulase-negative staphylococcus as the true pathogen given concordant cultures, elevated markers, and a positive alpha-defensin
Diagnose aseptic loosening and proceed to simple revision without antibiotics
Attribute the symptoms to gout
stages.explanation: Coagulase-negative staphylococci such as S. epidermidis are common skin contaminants in a single specimen, but in the context of a prosthetic joint they are leading causes of indolent PJI because they form biofilm on implanted material. When the same organism grows from multiple aspirate or operative cultures, with elevated ESR/CRP, a positive alpha-defensin, and synovial pleocytosis, it is the pathogen, not a contaminant (so option A is wrong). Option C mislabels an infected, loose implant as purely aseptic and would lead to an inadequate single-stage swap without treating infection, risking relapse. Option D ignores the microbiology entirely. Recognizing biofilm-forming coagulase-negative staph as a genuine pathogen is essential because it dictates implant removal and prolonged targeted antibiotics rather than a simple exchange.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Debridement, antibiotics, and implant retention (DAIR)
Lifelong oral antibiotic suppression alone
One-stage exchange without antibiotics
Two-stage exchange arthroplasty with prolonged organism-directed IV antibiotics, adding rifampin for biofilm activity
stages.explanation: This is chronic PJI—symptoms for about a year with radiographic loosening—so the standard of care is two-stage exchange: removal of all components and cement, placement of an antibiotic spacer, several weeks of organism-directed IV antibiotics, then reimplantation once infection is controlled. DAIR (option A) is appropriate only for acute infection (symptoms under ~3–4 weeks, or early postoperative) with a well-fixed implant; it fails in chronic infection with a loose prosthesis and established biofilm. Option B (suppression alone) is reserved for patients who cannot undergo surgery, not as primary cure. Option C (one-stage without antibiotics) abandons antimicrobial therapy and would relapse. Rifampin combination therapy is added for staphylococcal device-related infection because it penetrates biofilm, always used with a companion agent. Two-stage exchange offers the highest cure rate here.

cases.diagnosis: Chronic prosthetic joint infection due to coagulase-negative Staphylococcus (Staphylococcus epidermidis)
pearls: Indolent pain after arthroplasty with elevated ESR/CRP suggests low-grade PJI, often biofilm-forming coagulase-negative staphylococcus.
Synovial fluid thresholds for chronic PJI are lower than for native joints (synovial WBC roughly >3,000/µL with >80% neutrophils).
Coagulase-negative staph from a prosthetic joint should not be dismissed as a contaminant when markers and clinical picture concur.
Chronic PJI with a loose implant requires two-stage exchange; DAIR is reserved for acute infection with a well-fixed prosthesis, and rifampin targets staphylococcal biofilm.

cases.title: Confused and feverish, brought in from home
cases.chiefComplaint: Family reports he became confused, feverish, and stopped making sense today.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man is brought in by his wife for one day of confusion and lethargy on a background of 2 days of dysuria, urinary frequency, and right flank pain. This morning he developed shaking chills and a high fever, then became difficult to rouse and disoriented. He has not been eating or drinking well and his urine output has dropped. He has benign prostatic hyperplasia with a history of incomplete bladder emptying and one prior urinary tract infection. Past medical history also includes type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. Medications: tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, and amlodipine 10 mg daily. He has no known drug allergies. He is a retired accountant, lives with his wife, drinks alcohol socially, and quit smoking 20 years ago. There is no recent travel, no indwelling urinary catheter, and no known kidney stones. Family history is noncontributory. On arrival he is hypotensive and tachycardic, and collateral history confirms no chest pain, cough, headache, or neck stiffness.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing and lethargic but arousable to voice, oriented only to self. Mucous membranes are dry. He is tachycardic with a regular rhythm and no murmur; extremities are warm with bounding pulses and brisk capillary refill (early distributive shock). Lungs are clear. There is marked right costovertebral angle tenderness and mild suprapubic tenderness without rebound or guarding. There is no rash, no meningismus, and no focal neurologic deficit. No flank ecchymosis or skin breakdown is present. The bladder is not palpably distended.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Obtain a CT abdomen and pelvis before any treatment
Give acetaminophen and observe on telemetry
Initiate the Hour-1 sepsis bundle: measure lactate, draw blood cultures, give broad-spectrum antibiotics, and start fluid resuscitation
Start norepinephrine immediately as the first intervention
stages.explanation: This patient meets criteria for sepsis with shock—fever, tachycardia, hypotension, and altered mentation from a urinary source—and the priority is the Surviving Sepsis Hour-1 bundle: measure serum lactate, obtain blood cultures before antibiotics, administer broad-spectrum antibiotics, begin 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4 mmol/L, and add vasopressors if hypotension persists. Option A (CT first) delays life-saving resuscitation and antibiotics; imaging for source follows stabilization. Option B (antipyretic and observe) dangerously underestimates shock. Option D (norepinephrine first) is premature—vasopressors are added for hypotension refractory to adequate fluid resuscitation, not before volume is given. Early, simultaneous cultures, antibiotics, and fluids reduce mortality in septic shock; every hour of antibiotic delay increases death.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
WBC 19.4 ×10³/µL (16% bands)
Lactate 5.2 mmol/L
Creatinine 2.4 mg/dL (baseline 1.0)
BUN 38 mg/dL
Glucose 244 mg/dL
Urinalysis: WBC >50/hpf, leukocyte esterase positive, nitrites positive, many bacteria
Blood and urine cultures: pending
Repeat lactate after 30 mL/kg crystalloid: 3.8 mmol/L

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which combination best reflects appropriate resuscitation?
options: Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam) after cultures, plus 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid
Oral ciprofloxacin and discharge
Antibiotics only after imaging confirms a source
A single 500 mL bolus and reassessment in 6 hours
stages.explanation: Resuscitation centers on prompt broad-spectrum empiric antibiotics drawn after—but without delaying beyond—blood cultures, plus an initial 30 mL/kg crystalloid bolus for hypotension or lactate ≥4. For urosepsis, empiric coverage targets gram-negative bacilli including resistant organisms—piperacillin-tazobactam, a third/fourth-generation cephalosporin, or a carbapenem if risk factors for ESBLs exist. Option B (oral ciprofloxacin and discharge) is unacceptable in septic shock and ignores rising fluoroquinolone resistance in E. coli. Option C (antibiotics only after imaging) delays therapy and increases mortality—antibiotics must not await imaging. Option D (500 mL then wait 6 hours) under-resuscitates a hypoperfused patient with lactate >4; adequate early volume is essential. Balanced crystalloids are preferred, and therapy is later de-escalated based on culture and susceptibility data.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After 30 mL/kg of crystalloid his mean arterial pressure remains 58 mmHg. What is the next step?
options: Give another 4 L of crystalloid rapidly
Start norepinephrine titrated to a mean arterial pressure ≥65 mmHg
Start dopamine as the first-line vasopressor
Begin a phenylephrine infusion
stages.explanation: When hypotension persists despite adequate fluid resuscitation (here, MAP 58 after 30 mL/kg), the next step is a vasopressor, and norepinephrine is first-line in septic shock, titrated to a MAP ≥65 mmHg. Option A (further unguided large-volume fluids) risks volume overload without reassessing fluid responsiveness; additional boluses should be dynamically guided, not given reflexively. Option C (dopamine) is no longer preferred—it causes more arrhythmias and worse outcomes than norepinephrine. Option D (phenylephrine), a pure alpha-agonist, is reserved for select situations such as tachyarrhythmia limiting norepinephrine and is not first-line. If hypotension persists on norepinephrine, vasopressin is added as the second agent, and stress-dose hydrocortisone is considered for refractory shock. Maintaining perfusion pressure protects organs while source control proceeds.

stages.label: SOURCE CONTROL
stages.question: What is the most important additional step to address the source?
options: Continue antibiotics alone; no imaging is needed
Discharge once blood pressure improves
Start hemodialysis for the elevated creatinine
Obtain renal and bladder imaging (CT or ultrasound); if an obstructing stone with infection is found, arrange urgent decompression
stages.explanation: Source control is fundamental in sepsis. In urosepsis, an obstructing stone (or other anatomic obstruction) above an infected urinary tract creates pyonephrosis—a closed-space infection that antibiotics alone cannot clear and a classic cause of refractory septic shock. Imaging (CT or ultrasound) identifies obstruction or abscess; if present, urgent drainage via percutaneous nephrostomy or ureteral stent is required. Option A (antibiotics alone) misses a drainable focus and risks persistent shock and death. Option B (discharge) is unsafe—this patient needs ICU care and source evaluation. Option C (dialysis) targets the acute kidney injury, which is largely septic/prerenal and should improve with resuscitation; dialysis is not the immediate priority and does not address the infectious source. Prompt decompression of an obstructed, infected system is lifesaving.

cases.diagnosis: Septic shock secondary to urosepsis (complicated pyelonephritis)
pearls: Septic shock is sepsis with vasopressor-requiring hypotension and lactate >2 mmol/L despite fluids; lactate >4 mmol/L marks tissue hypoperfusion.
Hour-1 bundle: measure lactate, draw blood cultures before antibiotics, give broad-spectrum antibiotics, and start 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4.
Norepinephrine is the first-line vasopressor, titrated to a MAP ≥65 mmHg.
Always achieve source control—an obstructing stone or abscess in urosepsis requires urgent decompression or drainage.

cases.title: Sudden high fever and a sunburn-like rash
cases.chiefComplaint: I spiked a fever, started vomiting, and my whole body turned red.
cases.history: A 22-year-old woman presents with about 36 hours of high fever, severe myalgias, vomiting, watery diarrhea, and a diffuse red rash, accompanied by progressive lightheadedness. She is on the third day of her menstrual period and has been using high-absorbency tampons, recalling that she left one in place far longer than usual. She denies recent travel, new medications, sick contacts, or recent surgery. She is sexually active and takes a combined oral contraceptive; her last sexual activity was over a week ago. She has no chronic medical conditions and takes no other medications. She has no known drug allergies. She is a college student, drinks alcohol occasionally on weekends, does not smoke, and uses no injection drugs. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for sore throat, headache, and muscle aches and negative for cough, neck stiffness, focal weakness, abdominal surgery, or skin wounds. She has had no recent abscesses or insect or tick bites.
cases.exam: Acutely ill, flushed, and anxious. There is a diffuse, blanching, macular erythroderma resembling a sunburn that involves the trunk, extremities, palms, and soles. The oropharynx is hyperemic with a strawberry tongue, and there is nonpurulent bilateral conjunctival injection. Pelvic exam reveals a retained tampon in the vaginal vault with surrounding erythema and no foul discharge or abscess. The abdomen is mildly diffusely tender without peritoneal signs. There is no nuchal rigidity, no petechiae or purpura, and no focal neurologic deficit. Extremities are warm with rapid capillary refill consistent with distributive shock.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Give oral antipyretics and discharge with return precautions
Remove the retained tampon, obtain cultures, and begin aggressive IV fluid resuscitation
Order a CT head and lumbar puncture before any intervention
Start high-dose corticosteroids as first-line therapy
stages.explanation: This young woman in shock with high fever, diffuse erythroderma, vomiting, and a retained tampon has toxic shock syndrome until proven otherwise, and the immediate priorities are source control and resuscitation: remove the tampon (the toxin-producing nidus), obtain blood and vaginal/site cultures, and give aggressive IV crystalloids for distributive shock. Option A grossly underestimates a life-threatening toxin-mediated illness. Option C (CT/LP first) delays resuscitation; although meningococcemia is on the differential, there is no meningismus or petechial rash, and stabilization plus source removal cannot wait for neuroimaging. Option D (steroids as first-line) is not primary therapy; corticosteroids are not standard, whereas source control, fluids, and antibiotics are. Rapid removal of the foreign body markedly reduces ongoing toxin production and is a cornerstone of management alongside fluids and antimicrobials.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
WBC 17.6 ×10³/µL (22% bands)
Platelets 96 ×10³/µL
Creatinine 2.1 mg/dL
AST 142 U/L, ALT 130 U/L
Total bilirubin 2.3 mg/dL
Creatine kinase 1,180 U/L
Calcium 7.4 mg/dL
Lactate 3.6 mmol/L
Blood cultures: pending (frequently negative)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits this presentation?
options: Staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome
Streptococcal toxic shock syndrome
Kawasaki disease
Rocky Mountain spotted fever
stages.explanation: The picture—fever, hypotension, diffuse macular (sunburn-like) erythroderma involving palms and soles, vomiting, and multiorgan involvement (renal, hepatic, hematologic, muscular, mucous membranes) in a menstruating woman using high-absorbency tampons—meets criteria for staphylococcal TSS, mediated by TSST-1; blood cultures are typically negative because the disease is toxin-driven. Streptococcal TSS (B) usually arises from a painful invasive soft-tissue infection (e.g., necrotizing fasciitis) with positive blood cultures and lacks a tampon association—no such focus exists here. Kawasaki disease (C) affects young children, not adults, and follows a subacute course. Rocky Mountain spotted fever (D) causes a petechial rash beginning on the wrists and ankles after tick exposure, not a diffuse erythroderma with a retained tampon. The menstrual context and erythroderma make staphylococcal TSS the diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which antibiotic regimen is most appropriate?
options: Vancomycin alone
Ceftriaxone alone
An antistaphylococcal agent (empiric vancomycin pending MRSA status) plus clindamycin for toxin suppression
Oral doxycycline as an outpatient
stages.explanation: Antibiotic therapy for staphylococcal TSS pairs an antistaphylococcal agent with a protein-synthesis inhibitor: empiric vancomycin (until MRSA is excluded; otherwise an antistaphylococcal beta-lactam) PLUS clindamycin. Clindamycin is essential because it suppresses ribosomal toxin (TSST-1) production—reducing the toxin burden that drives shock—an effect beta-lactams lack. Option A (vancomycin alone) kills bacteria but does not halt toxin synthesis. Option B (ceftriaxone alone) provides neither reliable MRSA coverage nor toxin suppression. Option D (oral doxycycline, outpatient) is wholly inadequate for a patient in shock. Add IV immunoglobulin (IVIG) in severe or refractory cases to neutralize circulating toxin. Definitive care combines source control (tampon removal), aggressive fluid resuscitation, and this dual antibiotic strategy in an ICU setting.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which finding is expected during recovery and supports the diagnosis?
options: Permanent renal failure requiring lifelong dialysis
A petechial rash progressing to purpura fulminans
Chronic arthritis of the large joints
Desquamation of the palms and soles 1–2 weeks after symptom onset
stages.explanation: A hallmark of TSS during convalescence is full-thickness desquamation of the palms and soles, occurring roughly 1–2 weeks after symptom onset; it is part of the diagnostic criteria and supports the diagnosis retrospectively. The acute renal and hepatic dysfunction here is typically reversible with resuscitation and source control, so permanent dialysis-dependent failure (A) is not expected. A petechial rash progressing to purpura fulminans (B) characterizes meningococcemia or DIC-predominant sepsis, not the macular erythroderma of TSS. Chronic large-joint arthritis (C) is not a recognized sequela. Counsel the patient about recurrence risk and avoidance of high-absorbency tampons, since menstrual TSS can recur. Recognizing the expected desquamation prevents alarm and helps confirm the clinical diagnosis.

cases.diagnosis: Menstrual staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome (TSST-1 mediated)
pearls: Staphylococcal TSS is a clinical diagnosis: fever, hypotension, diffuse macular erythroderma, and multiorgan involvement; blood cultures are usually negative (toxin-mediated by TSST-1).
Source control is immediate—remove the tampon or other foreign material or nidus.
Treat with an antistaphylococcal agent PLUS clindamycin to suppress toxin production; add IVIG in refractory cases.
Desquamation of the palms and soles occurs 1–2 weeks after onset and supports the diagnosis retrospectively.

cases.title: Fever about a week after chemotherapy
cases.chiefComplaint: I've got a fever and I just feel completely wiped out.
cases.history: A 55-year-old woman admitted for supportive care reports a new fever and profound fatigue. She is 8 days out from her most recent cycle of dose-dense cytotoxic chemotherapy for acute myeloid leukemia and is at her expected neutrophil nadir. She has mild mouth soreness from mucositis but denies cough, dysuria, abdominal pain, diarrhea, headache, or skin lesions. She has a tunneled central venous catheter (port) that she reports is nontender. Past medical history is otherwise limited to the AML diagnosis 3 months ago and well-controlled hypothyroidism. Medications: levothyroxine 100 mcg daily, ondansetron as needed, and acyclovir prophylaxis. She has no known drug allergies and is not on antibacterial or antifungal prophylaxis. She is a married accountant, a lifelong nonsmoker, and does not drink alcohol. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is otherwise negative, and she specifically denies perianal pain, rectal symptoms, or new rashes.
cases.exam: Fatigued but alert and hemodynamically stable. The oral mucosa shows mild mucositis without thrush or ulcerated lesions. The tunneled catheter exit site is clean, dry, and intact with no erythema, tenderness, or purulence along the tunnel. Lungs are clear and the heart is regular without a new murmur. The abdomen is soft and nontender (no digital rectal examination is performed because of neutropenia). The perianal area is inspected and is without fluctuance, erythema, or tenderness. The skin shows no petechiae, nodules, or cellulitis, and there is no lymphadenopathy. Neurologic exam is nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the single most important next step?
options: Wait for the morning CBC to confirm the ANC before acting
Give acetaminophen and reassess in the morning
Obtain blood cultures (peripheral and from the port) and start empiric IV anti-pseudomonal antibiotics within 60 minutes
Discharge on oral amoxicillin-clavulanate
stages.explanation: Fever in a patient expected to be neutropenic after chemotherapy is a medical emergency. The standard is to obtain blood cultures (from a peripheral vein and each catheter lumen) and administer empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 60 minutes—do not wait to confirm the ANC if neutropenia is anticipated. Option A (await CBC) and option B (antipyretic, reassess later) introduce dangerous delays; untreated gram-negative sepsis in neutropenia can be rapidly fatal. Option D (oral amoxicillin-clavulanate, discharge) lacks anti-pseudomonal activity and is inappropriate for a hospitalized high-risk patient. Empiric monotherapy with an anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam is first-line. Prompt cultures plus rapid antibiotics is the single most important intervention and clearly improves survival in febrile neutropenia.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Temp 38.6 °C
WBC 0.4 ×10³/µL
ANC 180 /µL
Hemoglobin 9.2 g/dL
Platelets 44 ×10³/µL
Chest radiograph: no infiltrate
Urinalysis: unremarkable
Blood cultures: pending

stages.label: ANTIBIOTIC CHOICE
stages.question: Which empiric antibiotic is most appropriate?
options: Cefepime
Ceftriaxone
Vancomycin monotherapy
Oral ciprofloxacin plus amoxicillin-clavulanate
stages.explanation: Empiric therapy for high-risk febrile neutropenia is monotherapy with an anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam—cefepime, piperacillin-tazobactam, or a carbapenem (meropenem/imipenem). Cefepime fits because Pseudomonas aeruginosa and other gram-negative bacilli are the most feared early pathogens. Option B (ceftriaxone) lacks anti-pseudomonal coverage and is inadequate. Option C (vancomycin monotherapy) covers only gram-positives, missing the critical gram-negative threat; vancomycin is added to a beta-lactam only for specific indications, never used alone empirically. Option D (oral ciprofloxacin plus amoxicillin-clavulanate) is the regimen for selected LOW-risk outpatients (e.g., MASCC score ≥21, no significant comorbidities, expected short neutropenia)—not for a hospitalized high-risk patient with profound neutropenia and thrombocytopenia. Choosing reliable anti-pseudomonal coverage promptly is the priority; therapy is later adjusted to culture results and clinical course.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: She is hemodynamically stable with a clean port site and no localizing findings. What is the appropriate next step regarding vancomycin?
options: Add vancomycin routinely to all febrile neutropenic patients
Add an aminoglycoside for double gram-negative coverage by default
Stop antibiotics if she defervesces within 24 hours
Continue cefepime monotherapy; do not add vancomycin without a specific indication
stages.explanation: Vancomycin is NOT part of routine empiric therapy for febrile neutropenia. It is added only for specific indications: hemodynamic instability or severe sepsis, suspected catheter-related infection, skin or soft-tissue infection, pneumonia, known MRSA colonization, severe mucositis, or gram-positive growth in blood pending identification. This stable patient with a clean port site and no localizing findings has none, so continuing cefepime monotherapy is correct (option D). Option A (routine vancomycin) promotes resistance with no survival benefit when unindicated. Option B (default aminoglycoside) adds nephro- and ototoxicity without clear benefit unless resistant gram-negatives or instability are suspected. Option C (stopping at 24 hours) is unsafe while neutropenic—antibiotics continue until neutrophil recovery and clinical resolution, even if afebrile. Daily reassessment determines whether coverage should be broadened or narrowed.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: She remains febrile after 5 days on cefepime with an ANC still <200 and negative cultures. What is the best next step?
options: Stop all antibiotics and observe
Add empiric antifungal therapy (e.g., an echinocandin or voriconazole) and obtain CT of the chest and sinuses
Switch to oral antibiotics
Add corticosteroids
stages.explanation: Persistent or recurrent fever after 4–7 days of broad-spectrum antibiotics with ongoing neutropenia and negative cultures should prompt empiric antifungal therapy and a search for an occult fungal focus (invasive candidiasis or a mold such as Aspergillus). Add an echinocandin or a mold-active azole (e.g., voriconazole), and obtain CT of the chest and paranasal sinuses; consider serum galactomannan and beta-D-glucan. Option A (stopping antibiotics) would expose the patient to overwhelming infection while still neutropenic. Option C (switch to oral) inappropriately narrows therapy during persistent fever and profound neutropenia. Option D (corticosteroids) has no role here and would further impair immunity. Empiric antifungal coverage for persistent neutropenic fever reduces invasive fungal mortality while the marrow recovers.

cases.diagnosis: Febrile neutropenia (high-risk, post-chemotherapy)
pearls: Febrile neutropenia (single temp ≥38.3°C, or ≥38.0°C for 1 hour, with ANC <500/µL) is an emergency: cultures then empiric antibiotics within 60 minutes.
Empiric therapy is anti-pseudomonal monotherapy—cefepime, piperacillin-tazobactam, or a carbapenem.
Add vancomycin only for specific indications (instability, line or skin infection, pneumonia, MRSA, severe mucositis), not routinely.
Persistent fever after 4–7 days of antibiotics with ongoing neutropenia warrants empiric antifungal therapy and a fungal source workup.

cases.title: Persistent fevers in the ICU despite antibiotics
cases.chiefComplaint: The team reports ongoing spiking fevers despite a week of antibiotics.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man has been in the ICU for 3 weeks recovering from necrotizing pancreatitis complicated by an exploratory laparotomy and a prolonged ileus. He is receiving total parenteral nutrition through a right internal jugular central venous catheter and has completed more than 10 days of broad-spectrum antibacterial therapy with meropenem for an intra-abdominal source. Over the past 4 days he has had recurrent fevers to 39 °C with a relative tachycardia despite escalating antibacterial coverage, and cultures drawn earlier in the admission were negative. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus and chronic kidney disease stage 3. Medications: TPN, sliding-scale insulin, meropenem, pantoprazole, and subcutaneous heparin. He has no known drug allergies. He is a retired contractor, a former smoker, and drinks alcohol heavily, which precipitated his pancreatitis. Family history is noncontributory. He has had no new surgical interventions in the past week, and his abdominal drains have minimal serous output.
cases.exam: Chronically ill-appearing and fatigued but arousable. The right internal jugular central catheter is in place with a clean, dry dressing and no exit-site erythema or purulence. The midline laparotomy incision is healing with staples intact; the abdomen is soft with expected postoperative tenderness and no new peritoneal signs, and drain output is scant and serous. The lungs have bibasilar atelectatic crackles. The heart is regular with no new murmur. There are no Janeway lesions, splinter hemorrhages, or skin nodules. A fundoscopic exam has not yet been performed. There is no focal neurologic deficit and no costovertebral angle tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the best next diagnostic step?
options: Broaden antibacterials to add another gram-negative agent and continue observation
Draw blood cultures from a peripheral vein and the central catheter, and send fungal blood cultures and a serum beta-D-glucan given candidemia risk factors
Stop all antibiotics and observe off therapy
Order a CT abdomen and defer cultures until results return
stages.explanation: A critically ill patient with persistent fever despite several days of broad-spectrum antibacterials, who has the classic candidemia risk quartet—a central venous catheter, total parenteral nutrition, prolonged broad-spectrum antibiotics, and recent abdominal surgery—should be evaluated for invasive candidiasis. The cornerstone is drawing blood cultures from both a peripheral site and the catheter; fungal-specific cultures and a serum beta-D-glucan improve early detection. Option A (reflexively adding antibacterials) ignores the high pretest probability of fungemia and is unlikely to help.
