Morulae in monocytes = monocytic ehrlichiosis (E. chaffeensis); morulae in granulocytes = granulocytic anaplasmosis (A. phagocytophilum).
Start doxycycline empirically at any age; do not wait for confirmatory testing. Smear sensitivity is low, so confirm with PCR/serology.
Beta-lactams and fluoroquinolones are ineffective; rapid defervescence on doxycycline supports the diagnosis.

cases.title: Weeks of waxing-and-waning fevers, sweats, and aching joints
cases.chiefComplaint: I keep getting fevers and drenching night sweats that come and go, and my back and joints ache.
cases.history: A 48-year-old man presents with six weeks of intermittent fevers that rise in the evening and subside by morning, accompanied by drenching night sweats, profound fatigue, a 4-kg weight loss, and diffuse arthralgias, most prominent in his low back and hips. He emigrated from rural Mexico eight months ago and regularly eats unpasteurized goat cheese and drinks raw goat milk sent by relatives, and he previously helped tend goats on the family farm. He denies cough, hemoptysis, diarrhea, dysuria, or rash. He has no significant past medical history, takes no medications, and has no known drug allergies. He does not smoke, drinks alcohol occasionally, and works in construction. He has had no recent hospitalizations or sick contacts. Family history is noncontributory. He notes that the fevers seem to come in waves lasting several days, separated by relatively well periods, and that the sweats have an unusually strong odor. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable aside from the constitutional and musculoskeletal complaints described.
cases.exam: He appears chronically ill but is not in acute distress. There is mild generalized lymphadenopathy and hepatosplenomegaly, with the spleen tip palpable below the left costal margin. Cardiac examination reveals a regular rate and rhythm with no murmur, an important negative given the risk of endocarditis. The lungs are clear. There is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and pain on provocative sacroiliac maneuvers, with reduced lumbar range of motion; peripheral joints are without effusion. The skin shows no rash or stigmata of endocarditis. Neurologic examination is nonfocal. The abdomen is soft with mild left-upper-quadrant fullness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial workup?
options: Blood cultures (alert the lab to incubate longer) plus Brucella serum agglutination titers
Rapid strep test
Stool culture
Monospot test
stages.explanation: The undulant fever pattern, drenching sweats, arthralgias and sacroiliitis, and hepatosplenomegaly after consuming unpasteurized goat dairy strongly suggest brucellosis, so the appropriate workup is blood cultures, with the laboratory specifically alerted to incubate them longer because Brucella is a slow-growing fastidious organism, plus Brucella serum agglutination titers (option A). A rapid strep test (option B) evaluates pharyngitis, which is irrelevant here. Stool culture (option C) targets enteric pathogens and would not explain this systemic illness. A monospot (option D) screens for mononucleosis, which can cause fever and splenomegaly but not undulant fever after raw-dairy exposure with sacroiliitis, and would not establish this diagnosis. Notifying the lab is also a safety issue, because Brucella is highly infectious to laboratory personnel. Blood cultures plus serology are the cornerstone of diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS & CULTURES
stages.content:
WBC 3.6 ×10³/µL (mild leukopenia)
Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL
Platelets 130 ×10³/µL
AST 64 U/L, ALT 58 U/L (mild transaminitis)
Blood cultures: small, slow-growing gram-negative coccobacilli isolated after extended incubation
Brucella serum (tube) agglutination test: titer 1:640 (markedly elevated)
Sacroiliac MRI: findings consistent with sacroiliitis
HIV antigen/antibody: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Tuberculosis
Brucellosis
Q fever
Lymphoma
stages.explanation: The diagnosis is brucellosis (option B): undulant fevers, drenching malodorous sweats, arthralgias with sacroiliitis, hepatosplenomegaly, mild cytopenias, and, decisively, slow-growing gram-negative coccobacilli on blood culture with a markedly elevated Brucella agglutination titer, all after unpasteurized goat-dairy exposure. Tuberculosis (option A) can cause chronic fevers, sweats, and weight loss, but the exposure history, organism morphology and serology, and absence of pulmonary findings point away from it. Q fever (option C) is also acquired from livestock but typically presents with pneumonia or hepatitis and is diagnosed serologically (Coxiella), not by these blood-culture coccobacilli with high Brucella titers. Lymphoma (option D) can cause B symptoms and organomegaly but would not yield positive Brucella cultures and serology. The microbiology and exposure confirm brucellosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate antibiotic regimen?
options: Doxycycline monotherapy for 10 days
Combination doxycycline plus rifampin (or plus an aminoglycoside) for at least 6 weeks
Azithromycin for 5 days
Penicillin V for 10 days
stages.explanation: Brucellosis requires prolonged combination antibiotic therapy to prevent relapse, because the organism survives intracellularly. The standard regimen is doxycycline plus rifampin (or doxycycline plus an aminoglycoside such as streptomycin or gentamicin) for a minimum of six weeks (option B). Doxycycline monotherapy for 10 days (option A) is inadequate and is associated with high relapse rates; single agents and short courses fail. Azithromycin for five days (option C) and penicillin V for 10 days (option D) are neither the appropriate drugs nor the appropriate duration. The principles, two active agents and a long (>=6-week) course, with aminoglycoside-containing regimens often favored when focal disease such as spondylitis is present, distinguish correct treatment. Combination therapy with doxycycline plus rifampin (or an aminoglycoside) is the best answer.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication is the most common cause of death in brucellosis and should be screened for?
options: Endocarditis
Sacroiliitis
Epididymo-orchitis
Hepatic abscess
stages.explanation: Although focal complications such as sacroiliitis and spondylitis (option B) are the most common musculoskeletal manifestations, endocarditis is the leading cause of brucellosis-related death and must be screened for, which is why careful cardiac examination and a low threshold for echocardiography matter (option A). Sacroiliitis (option B) is common and debilitating but rarely fatal. Epididymo-orchitis (option C) is a recognized genitourinary complication but is not the chief cause of mortality. Hepatic abscess (option D) can occur but is uncommon and is not the principal lethal complication. Recognizing that Brucella endocarditis drives mortality, and that it often requires combined prolonged antibiotic therapy and sometimes valve surgery, is the key clinical point.

cases.diagnosis: Brucellosis (from unpasteurized goat dairy)
pearls: Suspect brucellosis with undulant fever, drenching sweats, arthralgias/sacroiliitis, and hepatosplenomegaly after unpasteurized dairy or livestock exposure.
Diagnose with blood cultures (alert the lab; slow-growing, biohazardous gram-negative coccobacilli) and Brucella serum agglutination titers.
Treat with combination therapy (doxycycline + rifampin, or + an aminoglycoside) for at least 6 weeks; monotherapy and short courses relapse.
Endocarditis is the leading cause of death; screen the heart even when symptoms are musculoskeletal.

cases.title: Fever, bloodshot eyes, and severe leg pain after an open-water race
cases.chiefComplaint: I've had high fevers and my eyes are bright red, and now my legs are killing me and my skin looks yellow.
cases.history: A 30-year-old recreational triathlete presents with eight days of illness that began about ten days after competing in an open-water swim in a freshwater lake following heavy regional rains. His illness started abruptly with high fever, rigors, a severe headache, and intense muscle pains, especially in his calves, along with markedly red eyes. After a few days he felt briefly better, but he has since worsened, developing yellowing of his skin and eyes, decreased urine output, dark urine, and increasing fatigue and confusion noted by his partner. He denies cough or hemoptysis, though he now feels short of breath. He has no significant past medical history, takes no regular medications, and has no known drug allergies. He drinks alcohol socially, does not smoke, and works as an engineer. He has not traveled internationally or to malaria-endemic areas. He recalls the lake water being muddy and is aware that rodents were common around the venue. Family history is noncontributory.
cases.exam: He is ill-appearing and jaundiced. There is prominent conjunctival suffusion, diffuse conjunctival redness without purulent discharge. The calves are markedly tender to palpation. Scleral icterus is evident. The cardiac examination is unremarkable, and the lungs have scattered crackles at the bases. The abdomen is soft with mild right-upper-quadrant tenderness and a palpable liver edge. There is no meningismus. Mild flank discomfort is present, and the patient appears oliguric. Neurologic examination shows mild inattention but no focal deficits. The skin shows no rash, eschar, or petechiae.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial workup?
options: CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, bilirubin, creatine kinase, urinalysis, and blood cultures
Discharge with oral fluids
CT head
Throat culture
stages.explanation: This severely ill, jaundiced, oliguric febrile patient needs a broad initial panel: CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, bilirubin, creatine kinase (myalgias suggest myositis/rhabdomyolysis), urinalysis, and blood cultures, with consideration of malaria smears given any relevant exposure (option A). These tests characterize the hepatic, renal, and muscle involvement central to severe leptospirosis (Weil disease). Discharging him with oral fluids (option B) is unsafe given jaundice, AKI, and hypoxemia. CT head (option C) is not indicated without focal neurologic findings and would delay the needed metabolic and infectious workup. A throat culture (option D) is irrelevant to this systemic illness. Objective data on liver function, renal function, muscle enzymes, blood counts, and cultures are required to recognize and stratify severe leptospirosis and to guide antibiotics and supportive care.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Total bilirubin 11.4 mg/dL (markedly elevated)
AST 88 U/L, ALT 76 U/L (only mildly elevated, disproportionately low relative to bilirubin)
Creatinine 3.8 mg/dL (acute kidney injury)
WBC 16.2 ×10³/µL with neutrophilia and left shift
Platelets 78 ×10³/µL
Creatine kinase 1,450 U/L (elevated)
Urinalysis: bilirubin and trace blood; granular casts
Malaria smear: negative
Leptospira serology/PCR: sent, pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute viral hepatitis A
Severe leptospirosis (Weil disease)
Dengue fever
Hantavirus infection
stages.explanation: This is severe leptospirosis, Weil disease, the icteric form with jaundice, acute kidney injury, and bleeding tendency (option B). The clues are decisive: freshwater exposure after heavy rain, a biphasic illness, conjunctival suffusion, severe calf myalgias with elevated CK, marked hyperbilirubinemia with only mild transaminase elevation, AKI, neutrophilic leukocytosis, and thrombocytopenia. Acute hepatitis A (option A) causes jaundice but with markedly elevated transaminases (often in the thousands), not this bilirubin-predominant pattern with AKI and conjunctival suffusion. Dengue (option C) is possible in travelers but typically causes leukopenia (not neutrophilic leukocytosis) and is less associated with this freshwater-exposure, jaundice-plus-renal-failure picture. Hantavirus (option D) classically causes a pulmonary or renal syndrome but not this icteric picture with conjunctival suffusion. The exposure and laboratory pattern define Weil disease.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment for this severe presentation?
options: Oral doxycycline managed as an outpatient
Oseltamivir
IV penicillin G or ceftriaxone, with supportive care and dialysis as needed
No antibiotics; leptospirosis is always self-limited
stages.explanation: Severe leptospirosis is treated with intravenous antibiotics, penicillin G or ceftriaxone, together with aggressive supportive care, including renal replacement therapy (dialysis) for severe AKI and management of bleeding and respiratory complications (option C). Oral doxycycline as an outpatient (option A) is appropriate only for mild, anicteric disease, not for a jaundiced, oliguric, hypoxemic patient who requires admission and IV therapy. Oseltamivir (option B) treats influenza and has no role here. Withholding antibiotics on the premise that leptospirosis is always self-limited (option D) is incorrect and dangerous; severe disease carries significant mortality. Clinicians should also anticipate a possible Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction after the first antibiotic dose. IV penicillin G or ceftriaxone plus intensive supportive care is the correct management for this severe presentation.

stages.label: CLINICAL CLUE
stages.question: Which physical finding is the classic clue to this diagnosis?
options: Koplik spots
Janeway lesions
Erythema migrans
Conjunctival suffusion
stages.explanation: Conjunctival suffusion, diffuse redness of the conjunctivae without purulent discharge, is the classic and relatively specific physical clue to leptospirosis, especially when paired with severe calf myalgias and a relevant water exposure (option D). Koplik spots (option A) are pathognomonic for measles. Janeway lesions (option B) are nontender hemorrhagic macules on the palms and soles seen in infective endocarditis. Erythema migrans (option C) is the target rash of early Lyme disease. While conjunctival suffusion can be subtle and is not universally present, recognizing it in a febrile patient with myalgias and freshwater or rodent-urine exposure should prompt strong consideration of leptospirosis and early empiric treatment. It is the discriminating examination finding among the options offered.

cases.diagnosis: Leptospirosis with progression to Weil disease (severe icteric leptospirosis)
pearls: Leptospirosis follows freshwater/floodwater or rodent-urine exposure; conjunctival suffusion with severe calf myalgias is a classic clue.
Weil disease is the severe icteric form: jaundice with disproportionately mild transaminase elevation, AKI, thrombocytopenia, and bleeding.
Treat mild disease with doxycycline; treat severe disease with IV penicillin G or ceftriaxone plus supportive care/dialysis. Anticipate a Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction.
Neutrophilic leukocytosis (unlike many viral illnesses) and an elevated CK help distinguish leptospirosis.

cases.title: Days of fever, runny nose, and red eyes, now a rash spreading down from the face
cases.chiefComplaint: He's had a high fever and cold symptoms for days, and now a rash started on his face and is spreading down his body.
cases.history: A 4-year-old boy is brought in by his parents for four days of high fever (up to 40 °C), a harsh barky cough, profuse clear nasal discharge, and red, watery eyes with light sensitivity. Two days ago his mother noticed tiny white spots inside his cheeks. Today an erythematous rash appeared at his hairline and behind the ears and on his face, and over several hours has begun spreading onto his neck and upper trunk. He has been increasingly irritable with poor oral intake. He has received no childhood vaccinations because his parents declined immunizations for personal beliefs. Two weeks ago the family attended a large international gathering, and the parents later learned that another attendee had been diagnosed with a measles-like illness. He was born full-term with no medical problems, takes no medications, and has no known drug allergies. He lives at home with his parents and an unvaccinated 4-month-old sibling. There are no pets and no recent antibiotic use. Family history is noncontributory.
cases.exam: He is febrile, ill-appearing, and irritable but consolable. The conjunctivae are markedly injected with clear discharge, and he is photophobic. There is copious clear rhinorrhea and a harsh cough. On the buccal mucosa opposite the molars are clusters of tiny blue-white papules on an erythematous base (Koplik spots). An erythematous, blanching maculopapular rash is present at the hairline, behind the ears, and over the face and neck, becoming confluent on the cheeks and extending onto the upper chest. The lungs are clear without retractions or distress. There is no nuchal rigidity, and the neurologic examination is nonfocal. Mucous membranes are slightly dry, but he is making tears and has moist axillae.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Roseola (HHV-6)
Scarlet fever
Measles (rubeola)
Kawasaki disease
stages.explanation: This is measles (rubeola) (option C). The classic sequence is a 3-4 day prodrome of high fever with the three C's, cough, coryza, and conjunctivitis, followed by pathognomonic Koplik spots (blue-white papules on the buccal mucosa) and then an erythematous maculopapular rash that begins at the hairline/face and spreads cephalocaudally, all in an unvaccinated child with a known exposure. Roseola (option A) causes high fever that breaks just as a rash appears, but the rash starts on the trunk and there is no conjunctivitis or Koplik spots. Scarlet fever (option B) produces a sandpaper rash with a strawberry tongue and pharyngitis, not Koplik spots or the three C's. Kawasaki disease (option D) features prolonged fever with mucositis, extremity changes, and lymphadenopathy but lacks Koplik spots and this prodrome and rash progression.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most important immediate next step?
options: Place the child in airborne isolation, notify the public health department, and send confirmatory measles testing
Discharge home with reassurance
Start empiric IV antibiotics
Admit to a shared pediatric room
stages.explanation: Measles is extraordinarily contagious and airborne, so the immediate priority is to place the child in airborne isolation (a negative-pressure room or, at minimum, an isolated room with the door closed and staff using respirators), immediately notify the public health department, and send confirmatory testing (serum measles IgM and a throat or nasopharyngeal swab for RT-PCR) (option A). Discharging home with reassurance (option B) risks ongoing community transmission, especially to unvaccinated contacts. Empiric IV antibiotics (option C) do not treat a viral infection and are reserved for bacterial superinfection. Admitting to a shared pediatric room (option D) would expose other vulnerable patients to an airborne pathogen. Measles is a nationally notifiable disease; prompt isolation, reporting, and laboratory confirmation are essential both for the patient and for outbreak control.

stages.label: CONFIRMATORY TESTING
stages.content:
Measles-specific IgM antibody: positive
Measles RT-PCR (throat swab): positive for measles RNA
WBC 4.2 ×10³/µL (leukopenia)
Chest X-ray: no infiltrate
Reported to local public health department

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What treatment reduces measles morbidity and mortality in children?
options: Oral acyclovir
Vitamin A supplementation plus supportive care
Oral amoxicillin
Ribavirin
stages.explanation: Treatment of measles is supportive (antipyretics, fluids, rest), but vitamin A supplementation is specifically recommended for children with measles because it reduces measles-associated morbidity and mortality, including the severity of complications; the WHO and AAP recommend it for affected children (option B). Acyclovir (option A) treats herpesviruses, not measles. Amoxicillin (option C) treats bacterial infections and is not indicated unless a bacterial complication such as otitis media or pneumonia develops. Ribavirin (option D) is not standard therapy for routine measles. Vitamin A is given as age-based dosing on two consecutive days (with an additional dose later if the child is vitamin A deficient). Supportive care plus vitamin A together represent the evidence-based approach to childhood measles.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: His unvaccinated 4-month-old sibling was exposed at home. What is the appropriate post-exposure prophylaxis?
options: MMR vaccine immediately
Intramuscular immunoglobulin within 6 days of exposure (the infant is too young for MMR)
Oral acyclovir
No prophylaxis is needed
stages.explanation: A 4-month-old infant is too young for the measles (MMR) vaccine, which is not given before 6 months of age, so appropriate post-exposure prophylaxis is intramuscular immunoglobulin, given within six days of exposure to susceptible high-risk contacts (including infants under 6 months and the immunocompromised) (option B). MMR vaccine immediately (option A) is not an option at 4 months and would not be protective in this age group. Oral acyclovir (option C) has no role in measles prophylaxis. Saying no prophylaxis is needed (option D) is incorrect and leaves a vulnerable infant unprotected against a potentially severe infection. For exposed individuals who are eligible, MMR within 72 hours is preferred, but when the contact is too young or otherwise cannot receive the vaccine, immunoglobulin within six days is the correct choice.

cases.diagnosis: Measles (rubeola)
pearls: Measles prodrome = the three C's (cough, coryza, conjunctivitis) plus high fever; Koplik spots are pathognomonic and precede the rash.
The maculopapular rash starts at the hairline/face and spreads cephalocaudally (head to toe).
Management is supportive plus vitamin A, which reduces measles morbidity and mortality; isolate (airborne) and report to public health.

Post-exposure prophylaxis: MMR within 72 hours for eligible contacts, or immunoglobulin within 6 days for those too young (<6 months) or unable to be vaccinated. Pneumonia is the most common cause of measles death; SSPE is a rare late complication.
